title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
1. Comparing Several Means
Suppose we have an interval-level dependent variable (Y), such as:

test score
income
life expectancy
thermometer rating
infant mortality rate
air quality index

And suppose we have a categorical independent variable (X), such as:

political party
school
classroom
region
regime type
treatment category

If (X) has only two categories, we can compare the two means using a t-test.
But if (X) has more than two categories, we need another approach.
For example, suppose we want to compare student test scores across five classrooms. The null hypothesis is that all five classrooms have the same mean test score. The alternative is that at least one classroom has a different mean.
This is precisely the kind of question ANOVA is designed to answer.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
2. What Does ANOVA Ask?
ANOVA asks whether the means of an interval-level variable differ across categories of one or more categorical variables.
For a one-way ANOVA, we have:

one interval-level dependent variable, (Y)
one categorical independent variable, (X)

The null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: _1 = _2 = _3 = = _G ]
where (G) is the number of groups.
The alternative hypothesis is:
[ H_A: ]
Notice that the alternative hypothesis does not say exactly which group differs. It only says that not all group means are equal.
If ANOVA rejects the null hypothesis, we know that at least one group mean differs, but we may need additional analysis to determine which specific groups differ from one another.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
section: 3. Example: Party Identification and Obama Thermometer Scores
text:
3. Example: Party Identification and Obama Thermometer Scores
Suppose respondents are asked to rate Barack Obama on a feeling thermometer from 0 to 100.

0 means very cold feelings.
100 means very warm feelings.

The overall mean thermometer score is:
[ {Y} = 60.72 ]
Now suppose we compare mean thermometer scores across party identification:









Party ID
Mean Obama Thermometer Score
Standard Deviation
Sample Size




Democrat
85.31
19.10
2,197


Independent
55.70
27.10
1,776


Republican
26.62
25.40
1,501


Overall
60.72

5,474



The means clearly differ.
Democrats rate Obama much more warmly than Republicans do, with independents in the middle.
The question is not whether the sample means differ. They plainly do. The question is whether those differences are large enough, relative to the variation within groups, to reject the null hypothesis that the population means are all the same.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
4. Analysis of Variance
ANOVA stands for analysis of variance.
That name may seem surprising because we are comparing means. But the method works by analyzing variance.
Specifically, ANOVA partitions the total variation in (Y) into two parts:

Between-group variation
Within-group variation

The key question is:

Is variation between group means large relative to variation within groups?

If the group means are far apart and individuals within each group are clustered close to their group mean, then group membership explains a lot of variation in (Y).
If the group means are close together or the groups overlap heavily, then group membership does not explain much variation in (Y).

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
5. Between-Group Variation
Between-group variation refers to variation in the group means.
In the Obama thermometer example, the group means are:

Democrats: 85.31
Independents: 55.70
Republicans: 26.62

These means are far apart.
That suggests party identification explains a substantial amount of variation in Obama thermometer scores.
Between-group variation asks:

How far are the group means from the overall mean?

The overall mean is:
[ {Y} = 60.72 ]
So we examine how far each group mean is from 60.72, weighting by group size.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
6. Within-Group Variation
Within-group variation refers to variation among individuals inside the same group.
Not all Democrats rate Obama exactly the same way. Not all Republicans do either. There is variation within each party category.
For Democrats, the standard deviation is 19.10.
For Independents, it is 27.10.
For Republicans, it is 25.40.
Within-group variation asks:

How much do individuals vary around their own group means?

If within-group variation is large, then group membership may not explain much. If within-group variation is small, and group means are far apart, then group membership explains more.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
7. The F Statistic
ANOVA uses an F statistic.
The F statistic is a ratio:
[ F = {} ]
If between-group variance is large relative to within-group variance, (F) will be large.
If between-group variance is small relative to within-group variance, (F) will be small.
The smallest possible F statistic is 0. That would occur when all group means are identical.
Because variances are always nonnegative, F statistics cannot be negative.
Like chi-square tests, ANOVA uses a one-sided test. We reject the null hypothesis only for large values of (F).

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
8. Visual Intuition
Imagine three groups.
In one scenario, each group is tightly clustered around a different mean. The group distributions barely overlap. Between-group variation is large, and within-group variation is small. ANOVA will produce a large F statistic.
In another scenario, the three group means differ slightly, but the observations within each group are widely spread out. The distributions overlap heavily. Between-group variation is small relative to within-group variation. ANOVA will produce a smaller F statistic.
The F statistic formalizes this comparison:
[ ]

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
9. Notation
Suppose we have:

total sample size (N)
number of groups (G)
sample size in group (g): (n_g)
outcome for person (i) in group (g): (Y_{ig})
mean of group (g): ({Y}_g)
overall mean: ({Y})

ANOVA compares the group means ({Y}_g) to the overall mean ({Y}), while also accounting for variation among individuals within each group.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
10. Between Sum of Squares
The between sum of squares measures how far group means are from the overall mean, weighted by group size.
[ SS_{between} = _{g=1}^{G} n_g({Y}_g - {Y})^2 ]
For each group:

Take the group mean.
Subtract the overall mean.
Square the difference.
Multiply by the group size.
Add across groups.

Groups with more cases contribute more to the between-group sum of squares.
The degrees of freedom for the between-group component are:
[ df_{between} = G - 1 ]
The between-group variance, also called the mean square between, is:
[ MS_{between} = ]

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
11. Within Sum of Squares
The within sum of squares measures variation of individuals around their own group means.
[ SS_{within} = {g=1}^{G} {i=1}^{n_g} (Y_{ig} - {Y}_g)^2 ]
This is the sum of the within-group sums of squares.
Another way to calculate it is:
[ SS_{within} = _{g=1}^{G} (n_g-1)s_g^2 ]
where (s_g^2) is the sample variance within group (g).
This second formula is often more convenient if we already know group standard deviations.
The degrees of freedom for the within-group component are:
[ df_{within} = N - G ]
The within-group variance, also called the mean square within, is:
[ MS_{within} = ]

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
12. The F Statistic Formula
The F statistic is:
[ F = ]
or:
[ F = {SS_{within}/(N-G)} ]
The F statistic has two degrees of freedom:
[ F_{G-1,;N-G} ]
The first is the numerator degrees of freedom.
The second is the denominator degrees of freedom.
Software will report both.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
13. Interpreting the F Statistic
A large F statistic means that group means are far apart relative to within-group variation.
A small F statistic means that group means are not far apart relative to within-group variation.
The F test evaluates:
[ H_0: _1 = _2 = = _G ]
against:
[ H_A: ]
If the p-value associated with F is less than alpha, we reject the null hypothesis.

text:

text:
15. Calculating Between-Group Variation
The between sum of squares is:
[ SS_{between} = _{g=1}^{G} n_g({Y}_g - {Y})^2 ]
Substitute values:
[ SS_{between} = 2197(85.31 - 60.72)^2 + 1776(55.70 - 60.72)^2 + 1501(26.62 - 60.72)^2 ]
This gives approximately:
[ SS_{between} = 2{,}881{,}370.9 ]
Degrees of freedom:
[ df_{between} = G - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2 ]
So:
[ MS_{between} = ]

16. Calculating Within-Group Variation
Using group standard deviations:
[ SS_{within} = _{g=1}^{G} (n_g-1)s_g^2 ]
Substitute values:
[ SS_{within} = (2197-1)(19.10)^2 + (1776-1)(27.10)^2 + (1501-1)(25.40)^2 ]
This gives approximately:
[ SS_{within} = 3{,}689{,}000 ]
Degrees of freedom:
[ df_{within} = N - G = 5474 - 3 = 5471 ]
So:
[ MS_{within} = ]
[ MS_{within} ]

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
17. Calculating the F Statistic
Now calculate:
[ F = ]
[ F = ]
[ F ]
This is extremely large.
The p-value is effectively zero.
Therefore, we reject the null hypothesis that Democrats, Independents, and Republicans all have the same mean Obama thermometer score.
Party identification explains a substantial amount of variation in Obama thermometer ratings.
Not exactly a plot twist worthy of prestige television, but statistically speaking, it is very clear.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
18. ANOVA Output
Statistical software usually reports an ANOVA table with columns such as:

The row for Party ID represents between-group variation.
The residual row represents within-group variation.
The F statistic is:
[ {} ]
The p-value tells us whether the group means differ with statistical significance.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
19. R-Squared in ANOVA
Software may also report (R^2).
In ANOVA, (R^2) represents the proportion of total variation in (Y) explained by the group categories.
[ R^2 = ]
In the Obama thermometer example, party ID explains roughly 44 percent of the variation in Obama thermometer scores.
That is a large amount of explained variation for social science data.
We will return to (R^2) in more detail when discussing regression.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
20. Test Scores Across Schools
Suppose we have math test scores for students in an urban school district.
There are:

8,064 students
25 schools

The question is:

Do mean math test scores differ across schools?

The null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: _1 = 2 = = {25} ]
The alternative is:
[ H_A: ]
A one-way ANOVA can test whether school membership explains statistically significant variation in test scores.
If the F statistic is large and the p-value is small, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that mean test scores differ across schools.
This does not tell us why schools differ. Differences may reflect school quality, neighborhood inequality, student composition, resources, peer effects, or other factors. ANOVA tells us whether there are differences, not what caused them.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
21. Adding a Second Categorical Variable
A two-way ANOVA uses two categorical independent variables to explain variation in an interval-level dependent variable.
For example, suppose we want to explain Obama thermometer scores using:

party identification
region

Party ID has three categories:

Democrat
Independent
Republican

Region has two categories:

South
not South

Together, they create six combinations:

Democrat, not South
Democrat, South
Independent, not South
Independent, South
Republican, not South
Republican, South

Two-way ANOVA allows us to ask whether party matters after controlling for region, and whether region matters after controlling for party.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
section: 22. Example: Party ID, Region, and Obama Thermometer Scores
text:
22. Example: Party ID, Region, and Obama Thermometer Scores
Suppose the table looks roughly like this:



Party ID
Not South Mean
South Mean
Overall Mean




Democrat
82.0
86.2
85.3


Independent
54.2
51.5
55.7


Republican
27.1
24.7
26.6



We can ask several questions.
First:

Do all six means differ from one another?

That is the model-level question.
Second:

After controlling for region, does party ID still explain differences in Obama thermometer scores?

Third:

After controlling for party ID, does region still explain differences in Obama thermometer scores?

ANOVA can provide F tests for each question.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
23. Interpreting Two-Way ANOVA Output
A two-way ANOVA output may include rows such as:



Source
F
p-value




Model
large
<0.001


Party ID
very large
<0.001


Region
smaller
0.04


Residual





The model row tests whether the full set of categories, taken together, explains variation in (Y).
The Party ID row tests whether party ID explains variation in (Y), after controlling for region.
The Region row tests whether region explains variation in (Y), after controlling for party ID.
If party ID has a very small p-value, then party ID matters even after controlling for region.
If region has a p-value of 0.04, then region is statistically significant at the 0.05 level, but we still need to ask whether the effect is substantively meaningful.
In the example, regional differences are small compared to party differences. With a large sample, even small differences may become statistically significant.
Statistical significance and substantive importance are not the same thing.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
24. Controlling for Variables in ANOVA
Two-way ANOVA introduces the idea of statistical control.
When we say:

Party ID matters after controlling for region,

we mean that differences across party categories remain even when we compare people within the same region category.
Likewise:

Region matters after controlling for party ID

means that regional differences remain even when we compare people within the same party category.
This is conceptually similar to controlled mean comparison tables. ANOVA gives us a formal significance test for those comparisons.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
25. The Research Question
Suppose we are interested in whether democracies have higher life expectancy than non-democracies.
The dependent variable is:

life expectancy

The categorical independent variables are:

regime type: democracy or non-democracy
GDP per capita category: low, middle, high

We know that democracies tend to be wealthier than non-democracies. Wealthier countries tend to have better health systems, infrastructure, nutrition, education, and public services.
So GDP per capita may confound the relationship between democracy and life expectancy.
A two-way ANOVA can help us ask:

Does regime type explain life expectancy after controlling for GDP category?
Does GDP category explain life expectancy after controlling for regime type?

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
26. Mean Comparison Table
Suppose the table is:



Regime Type
Low GDP
Middle GDP
High GDP
Overall




Non-democracy
57.8
68.7
73.5
64.5


Democracy
63.3
74.1
79.2
72.5



The table suggests that democracies have higher life expectancy within each income category.
It also suggests that higher-income countries have higher life expectancy within both regime categories.
A two-way ANOVA allows us to attach statistical significance to these patterns.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
27. Two-Way ANOVA Interpretation
Suppose ANOVA output shows:



Source
F
p-value




Model
62.32
<0.001


Democracy
21.81
<0.001


GDP category
65.33
<0.001



The model as a whole is statistically significant.
Democracy remains statistically significant after controlling for GDP per capita category.
GDP per capita category remains statistically significant after controlling for democracy.
A careful interpretation is:

We reject the null hypothesis that mean life expectancy is the same in democracies and non-democracies after controlling for GDP per capita category. We also reject the null hypothesis that mean life expectancy is the same across GDP categories after controlling for regime type.

This does not automatically prove that democracy causes higher life expectancy. It shows that the association remains after controlling for a coarse measure of country wealth.
Causality would require stronger design or additional controls.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
section: 28. ANOVA as a Special Case of Regression
text:
28. ANOVA as a Special Case of Regression
ANOVA can be understood as a special case of regression with categorical independent variables.
In regression, categorical variables can be represented using dummy variables. A regression model with categorical predictors can reproduce ANOVA results.
Regression is more flexible because it can include:

categorical variables
interval-level variables
multiple controls
interactions
nonlinear terms
complex model structures

For this reason, many applied researchers use regression rather than ANOVA.
Still, ANOVA is conceptually useful because it teaches us how variation in an outcome can be partitioned into explained and unexplained components.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
29. When Is ANOVA Useful?
ANOVA is useful when:

the dependent variable is interval-level
the independent variable or variables are categorical
we want to test whether group means differ
we have more than two group means
we want a broad test of whether categories explain variation in an outcome

Examples:

comparing test scores across schools
comparing outcomes across multiple treatment arms
comparing attitudes across party categories
comparing health outcomes across income groups
testing whether several group means are equal

ANOVA is less useful when:

we need to control for many variables
variables are continuous
the model requires more flexible specification
we care about estimating specific effect sizes
causal inference requires more advanced design

In those cases, regression is usually preferable.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
section: 30. ANOVA Tells Us Whether Means Differ
text:
30. ANOVA Tells Us Whether Means Differ
ANOVA tells us whether at least one group mean differs from the others.
It does not automatically tell us:

which groups differ
how large the differences are
whether the differences are substantively important
whether the relationship is causal

If ANOVA rejects the null hypothesis, researchers may conduct follow-up comparisons to determine which group means differ. These are sometimes called post-hoc tests.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
31. Statistical Versus Substantive Significance
As always, statistical significance is not the same as substantive importance.
With a very large sample, small differences in means may produce a statistically significant F statistic.
For example, regional differences in Obama thermometer scores may be statistically significant because the sample is large, even if the difference is only a few points on a 0-to-100 scale.
Researchers must inspect the group means and ask:

Are these differences meaningful in the real world?

ANOVA provides statistical evidence. Interpretation requires substantive judgment.

title: Chapter 12: ANOVA
text:
32. Causality
ANOVA does not establish causality by itself.
If the data come from a randomized experiment, group differences may support causal claims.
If the data are observational, group differences may reflect confounding, selection, or other alternative explanations.
For example, if democracies have higher life expectancy than non-democracies, the difference may reflect wealth, health systems, education, colonial history, conflict, geography, or other factors.
ANOVA can help describe and test associations. Causal inference requires research design and theory.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
section: 1. From Probability Theory to Chi-Square Tests
text:
1. From Probability Theory to Chi-Square Tests
Earlier, we defined independence using conditional probabilities.
Two events (A) and (B) are independent if:
[ P(A B) = P(A) ]
In words: knowing that (B) occurred does not change the probability of (A).
The same logic applies to categorical variables.
Suppose we have two categorical variables:

(Y): whether someone owns a gun
(X): whether someone is afraid to walk in their neighborhood at night

If these variables are independent, then the probability of owning a gun should be the same whether or not someone is afraid to walk in their neighborhood.
That is:
[ P( ) = P() ]
and:
[ P( ) = P() ]
If those conditional probabilities differ, the variables are not independent.
But sample data always contain random sampling error. We need to know whether the differences we observe are large enough to reject independence with statistical confidence.
That is what the chi-square test does.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
section: 2. Example: Gun Ownership and Neighborhood Fear
text:
2. Example: Gun Ownership and Neighborhood Fear
Suppose the General Social Survey asks two questions:

Do you own a gun?
Are you afraid to walk in your neighborhood at night?

The joint frequency distribution is:

Overall, the proportion who own a gun is:
[ = 0.3204 ]
So about 32.04 percent of respondents own a gun.
Among people who are afraid to walk in their neighborhood at night, the proportion who own a gun is:
[ = 0.2436 ]
Among people who are not afraid, the proportion who own a gun is:
[ = 0.3584 ]
These conditional probabilities differ from the unconditional probability of 0.3204.
That suggests the variables may not be independent.
But we need a statistical test to determine whether the deviation is large enough to reject the null hypothesis of independence.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
3. The Null Hypothesis of Independence
For a chi-square test of independence, the null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: X Y ]
The alternative hypothesis is:
[ H_A: X Y ]
In words:

The null hypothesis says the distribution of (Y) does not depend on (X).
The alternative hypothesis says the distribution of (Y) does depend on (X).

In the gun ownership example:
[ H_0: ]
[ H_A: ]

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
4. Expected Frequencies
The chi-square test compares observed frequencies to expected frequencies.
An observed frequency is the number we actually see in the data.
An expected frequency is the number we would expect to see if the null hypothesis of independence were true.
For example, we observed 133 people who both:

own a gun
are afraid to walk in their neighborhood at night

But if gun ownership and neighborhood fear were independent, how many would we expect in that cell?
The overall gun ownership rate is 0.3204. If independence holds, then 32.04 percent of people in every fear category should own a gun.
There are 546 people who are afraid. So the expected frequency is:
[ 546(0.3204) = 174.9 ]
So under independence, we would expect about 174.9 afraid respondents to own a gun.
We observed only 133.
That difference contributes to the chi-square statistic.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
5. Formula for Expected Frequencies
For any cell in a contingency table, the expected frequency is:
[ f_e = ]
where:

(f_e) is the expected frequency
(n) is the total sample size

For the “owns gun” and “afraid” cell:
[ f_e = ]
[ f_e = 174.9 ]
This formula works for every cell in the table.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
7. Contingency Tables and Cross-Tabs
A contingency table, also called a cross-tabulation or cross-tab, displays the joint distribution of two categorical variables.
Cross-tabs are useful for examining whether the distribution of one variable changes across categories of another variable.
When constructing these tables, it is often clearest to put:

the dependent variable (Y) in the rows
the independent variable (X) in the columns

This makes it easy to compare column percentages side by side.
For example:

(X=1)

Column percentages allow us to ask:

Does the distribution of (Y) change as we move across categories of (X)?

Using the gun ownership example:

If the variables were independent, both column percentages would equal the overall gun ownership rate:

They do not. The chi-square test tells us whether the observed deviation is statistically significant.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
9. Adding a Control Variable
We can add a control variable to a cross-tabulation.
Suppose we add whether someone hunts.
Hunting is likely related to gun ownership. Hunters are much more likely to own guns than non-hunters.
If we control for hunting, we compare gun ownership and neighborhood fear separately among:

non-hunters
hunters

This creates separate cross-tabs within each category of the control variable.
For example:










Owns Gun
Afraid, Non-Hunter
Not Afraid, Non-Hunter
Afraid, Hunter
Not Afraid, Hunter




Yes
%

%

%
%

If fear of walking at night is related to gun ownership only among non-hunters but not among hunters, then the control variable helps refine the relationship.
This is similar to controlled mean comparisons. We hold a third variable constant to see whether the relationship between (X) and (Y) persists.
However, cross-tabs can become unwieldy quickly when we add more variables or categories. This is one reason regression methods become useful.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
10. Assumptions of the Chi-Square Test
The chi-square test of independence requires:

A random or representative sample.
Two categorical variables.
Mutually exclusive categories.
Expected frequencies of at least 5 in every cell.

The expected frequency condition is important.
It refers to expected frequencies, not observed frequencies.
If expected frequencies are too small, the chi-square approximation may not be reliable. In that case, Fisher’s exact test may be preferable.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
11. The Chi-Square Statistic
The chi-square statistic is:
[ ^2 = ]
where:

(f_o) is the observed frequency
(f_e) is the expected frequency
the sum is taken across all cells in the table

The statistic measures how far observed frequencies are from expected frequencies.
If observed and expected frequencies are identical, then:
[ f_o - f_e = 0 ]
for every cell, and:
[ ^2 = 0 ]
As observed frequencies deviate more from expected frequencies, the chi-square statistic grows larger.

text:
13. Degrees of Freedom
For a chi-square test of independence:
[ df = (r-1)(c-1) ]
where:

(r) is the number of rows
(c) is the number of columns

Do not count total rows or total columns.
In the gun ownership example, there are:

2 rows
2 columns

So:
[ df = (2-1)(2-1) ]
[ df = 1 ]

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
14. The Chi-Square Distribution
The chi-square distribution is different from the normal and t distributions.
It starts at 0 and extends to positive infinity.
A chi-square statistic can never be negative.
A chi-square test is therefore a one-sided test. We reject the null hypothesis only for large values of (^2).
For (= 0.05) and (df = 1), the critical value is:
[ ^2 = 3.84 ]
Our test statistic is:
[ ^2 = 22.2 ]
Since:
[ 22.2 > 3.84 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.
Gun ownership and fear of walking in one’s neighborhood at night are not independent.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
15. p-Value for the Chi-Square Test
The p-value is the area in the upper tail of the chi-square distribution beyond the observed chi-square statistic.
Unlike two-tailed t-tests and z-tests, we do not double the p-value.
For the gun ownership example, the test statistic is so large that:
[ p < 0.001 ]
Since:
[ p < 0.05 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
section: 16. Running the Chi-Square Test in Software
text:
16. Running the Chi-Square Test in Software
In Stata, the command structure is:
tab yvar xvar, col chi
This produces a cross-tabulation with column percentages and a chi-square test.
In R, one common workflow is:
mytable <- table(yvar, xvar)
chisq.test(mytable, correct = FALSE)
The argument correct = FALSE avoids a small-sample correction that is not needed for large samples.
Software reports:

chi-square statistic
degrees of freedom
p-value

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
17. Revisiting the Graduation Program
Earlier, we analyzed a difference of proportions for a program intended to increase high school graduation.
The data were:



Group
Graduated
Did Not Graduate
Total




Program
83
10
93


No Program
102
19
121


Total
185
29
214



The graduation rates were:
[ = 0.892 ]
and:
[ = 0.843 ]
The difference was not statistically significant using a z-test.
We can also analyze this as a chi-square test because it is a 2-by-2 table.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
18. Expected Frequencies
The overall graduation rate is:
[ = 0.864 ]
If program participation and graduation were independent, we would expect 86.4 percent of each group to graduate.
The expected frequencies are approximately:



Group
Graduated Expected
Did Not Graduate Expected




Program
80.4
12.6


No Program
104.6
16.4



All expected frequencies exceed 5, so the chi-square test is appropriate.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
19. Chi-Square Result
Using the formula:
[ ^2 = ]
we obtain:
[ ^2 = 1.10 ]
Degrees of freedom:
[ df = (2-1)(2-1)=1 ]
The critical value at (= 0.05) is:
[ 3.84 ]
Since:
[ 1.10 < 3.84 ]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
The chi-square test reaches the same conclusion as the difference-of-proportions z-test.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
section: 20. Relationship Between z and Chi-Square in 2-by-2 Tables
text:
20. Relationship Between z and Chi-Square in 2-by-2 Tables
For a 2-by-2 table, the chi-square statistic with one degree of freedom is equal to the square of the z-statistic from the difference-of-proportions test:
[ ^2 = z^2 ]
In the graduation example, the z-statistic was:
[ z = 1.049 ]
Squaring it:
[ 1.049^2 ]
which equals the chi-square statistic.
This relationship holds only for 2-by-2 tables.
Once one of the variables has more than two categories, the chi-square test no longer reduces to a z-test.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
21. The Research Question
Suppose we want to analyze satisfaction with health insurance plans after implementation of the Affordable Care Act.
The dependent variable is satisfaction with one’s health plan:

satisfied
neutral
dissatisfied

The independent variable is type of health insurance:

ACA exchange plan
other health insurance

The question is:

Is satisfaction with one’s health insurance independent of plan type?

The null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: ]
The alternative is:
[ H_A: ]

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
22. Observed Frequencies and Column Percentages
Suppose the data are:



Satisfaction
ACA Plan
Other Plan
Total




Satisfied
772
5,096
5,868


Neutral
205
895
1,100


Dissatisfied
108
386
494


Total
1,085
6,377
7,462



Column percentages show:



Satisfaction
ACA Plan
Other Plan
Overall




Satisfied
71.2%
79.9%
78.6%


Neutral
18.9%
14.0%
14.7%


Dissatisfied
10.0%
6.1%
6.7%



People with ACA exchange plans are less likely to report being satisfied and more likely to report being neutral or dissatisfied.
Now we test whether this relationship is statistically significant.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
23. Expected Frequencies
Under independence, the satisfaction distribution should be the same for ACA plans and other plans.
For example, 78.6 percent of all respondents are satisfied. If plan type does not matter, then 78.6 percent of ACA respondents and 78.6 percent of other-plan respondents should be satisfied.
Using:
[ f_e = ]
the expected frequencies are approximately:



Satisfaction
ACA Expected
Other Expected




Satisfied
853.2
5,014.8


Neutral
159.9
940.1


Dissatisfied
71.9
422.1



All expected frequencies exceed 5, so the chi-square test is appropriate.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
24. Chi-Square Statistic
For each cell:
[ ]
The contributions are approximately:



Satisfaction
ACA Contribution
Other Contribution




Satisfied
7.7
1.3


Neutral
12.7
2.2


Dissatisfied
18.1
3.1



Adding all cell contributions gives:
[ ^2 ]
Using the lecture’s rounded values, the statistic was reported around:
[ ^2 ]
Small differences can arise from rounding or slightly different underlying cell values. The substantive conclusion is the same: the statistic is very large.
Degrees of freedom:
[ df = (3-1)(2-1) ]
[ df = 2 ]
At (= 0.05), the critical value for (df=2) is:
[ 5.99 ]
Since:
[ 50.4 > 5.99 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.
Satisfaction and plan type are not independent.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
25. Interpreting the Result
The chi-square test tells us that satisfaction differs by plan type.
But the chi-square statistic itself does not tell us the direction of the relationship.
To interpret direction, we must examine the table.
ACA exchange plan respondents are:

less likely to be satisfied
more likely to be neutral
more likely to be dissatisfied

The chi-square test tells us the relationship is statistically significant. The table tells us what the relationship looks like.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
26. Residuals
A residual in a chi-square table is:
[ f_o - f_e ]
It tells us whether a cell contains more or fewer cases than expected under independence.
A positive residual means:
[ f_o > f_e ]
There are more cases in that cell than expected.
A negative residual means:
[ f_o < f_e ]
There are fewer cases in that cell than expected.
Residuals help us understand the direction of the relationship.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
27. Residuals in the ACA Example
For the ACA satisfaction example:



Satisfaction
ACA Residual
Other Residual




Satisfied
negative
positive


Neutral
positive
negative


Dissatisfied
positive
negative



This means:

fewer ACA plan respondents are satisfied than expected
more ACA plan respondents are neutral than expected
more ACA plan respondents are dissatisfied than expected

The opposite pattern appears among respondents with other health insurance.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
28. Standardized Residuals
A raw residual can be hard to interpret because its size depends on the sample size and expected frequency.
We can convert residuals into standardized residuals, which function like z-statistics.
A standardized residual is:
[ z = { } ]
where:

(p_{row}) is the row proportion
(p_{column}) is the column proportion

Large positive or negative standardized residuals indicate cells that contribute strongly to the chi-square statistic.
A standardized residual beyond approximately () suggests that the cell deviation is large relative to what we would expect under independence.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
29. Example: Standardized Residual
Suppose the ACA-satisfied cell has:
[ f_o = 772 ]
[ f_e = 853.2 ]
The residual is:
[ 772 - 853.2 = -81.2 ]
If the standardized residual is:
[ z = -6.50 ]
that is a very large negative deviation.
This means the ACA-satisfied cell has far fewer cases than expected under independence.
The result is not merely statistically significant overall. This specific cell is contributing strongly to the relationship.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
section: 30. Limits of Chi-Square for Ordinal Data
text:
30. Limits of Chi-Square for Ordinal Data
The chi-square test treats categories as nominal.
It does not care whether categories have a meaningful order.
For example, if a variable has categories:

decrease
keep the same
increase

the chi-square test treats them simply as three categories. It does not know that “increase” is higher than “keep the same” or that “decrease” is lower.
This is a limitation when variables are ordinal.
If both variables are ordinal, we may want to know whether the relationship is positive or negative.
The chi-square test does not provide that.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
31. Positive and Negative Relationships
A positive relationship between two ordinal variables means higher values of one variable tend to be associated with higher values of the other variable.
A negative relationship means higher values of one variable tend to be associated with lower values of the other variable.
For example, suppose one variable measures support for legalizing marijuana:

oppose
neutral
favor

and another measures whether gun laws should be more restrictive:

make it more difficult to buy a gun
keep laws the same
make it easier to buy a gun

If people who favor marijuana legalization also tend to favor more restrictive gun laws, the relationship may be negative depending on how the categories are ordered.
Direction depends on category coding.
Always check which direction the categories move.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
32. Gamma and Kendall’s Tau-b
For ordinal variables, we can use measures such as:

Goodman and Kruskal’s gamma
Kendall’s tau-b

These statistics can be positive, negative, or close to zero.
Interpretation:

positive value: variables tend to move in the same direction
negative value: variables tend to move in opposite directions
near zero: little ordinal association

These statistics supplement the chi-square test by describing the direction of the ordinal relationship.
For example, if gamma is:
[ -0.35 ]
then the relationship is negative.
If gamma is:
[ 0.42 ]
then the relationship is positive.
These values also have standard errors, allowing assessment of whether they are meaningfully different from zero.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
section: 33. Example: Spending on Schools and Social Security
text:
33. Example: Spending on Schools and Social Security
Suppose respondents are asked whether federal spending on public schools should:

decrease
stay the same
increase

and whether spending on Social Security should:

decrease
stay the same
increase

We might expect a positive relationship. People who favor more spending in one area may also favor more spending in another.
If gamma and Kendall’s tau-b are positive and large relative to their standard errors, that supports the interpretation of a positive ordinal relationship.
The chi-square test tells us whether the variables are independent.
Gamma and tau-b help tell us the direction.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
34. When Chi-Square Is Not Appropriate
The chi-square test requires expected frequencies of at least 5 in every cell.
If this condition fails, the chi-square approximation may be unreliable.
In such cases, use Fisher’s exact test.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
35. Fisher’s Exact Test
Fisher’s exact test evaluates association between categorical variables by calculating exact probabilities rather than relying on the chi-square approximation.
It is especially useful when sample sizes are small.
The test considers all possible tables with the same row and column totals and asks:

How many of those possible tables are as extreme as, or more extreme than, the observed table?

The result is an exact p-value.
Fisher’s exact test can be used for 2-by-2 tables and, in some software, for larger tables. However, for large tables with many categories, the computation may become memory intensive.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
36. Fisher’s Exact Test in Software
In Stata:
tab yvar xvar, exact
or:
tab yvar xvar, col chi exact
In R:
mytable <- table(yvar, xvar)
fisher.test(mytable)
Fisher’s exact test reports a p-value directly.
If expected cell counts are too small for chi-square, Fisher’s exact test is the safer option.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
37. Direction
The chi-square statistic does not tell us the direction of the relationship.
It tells us whether variables are independent, not how they are related.
To understand direction, examine:

column percentages
row percentages
residuals
standardized residuals
ordinal association measures, if appropriate

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
38. Magnitude
The chi-square statistic does not directly tell us the substantive magnitude of the relationship.
With a large enough sample, even small differences can become statistically significant.
For example, a difference between 51 percent and 53 percent may be statistically significant in a huge sample, but substantively small.
Always distinguish statistical significance from substantive significance.

title: Chapter 11: Association Between Categorical Variables
text:
39. Category Choices
Chi-square results can depend on how categories are defined.
If researchers combine or split categories differently, the table changes, and the chi-square result may change.
For example, a political scientist might classify regimes as:

democracy
non-democracy

or instead as:

non-democracy
presidential democracy
parliamentary democracy

The chi-square statistic may differ across these choices.
Researchers should be transparent about categorization decisions and check whether results are sensitive to alternative classifications.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
1. From Correlation to Regression
Correlation analysis measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two interval-level variables.
The most common correlation coefficient, Pearson’s (r), ranges from:
[ -1 r ]
A value of (r = 1) means perfect positive linear association.
A value of (r = -1) means perfect negative linear association.
A value of (r = 0) means no linear association.
Correlation is unit-free. It does not depend on the units of (X) or (Y). It tells us how linear the relationship is, but not how steep the relationship is.
Linear regression also studies linear relationships, but it estimates a line:
[ Y = a + bX ]
The coefficient (b) is the slope. It tells us how much (Y) changes when (X) increases by one unit.
That is the key advance.
Correlation tells us whether two variables move together.
Regression tells us how much one variable changes when the other changes.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
2. What Regression Adds
Regression adds two major things beyond correlation.
First, regression estimates the magnitude of the relationship.
For example, suppose we regress infant mortality on access to clean water. Regression can tell us:

For each one-percentage-point increase in the population with access to an improved water source, infant mortality is predicted to fall by 1.5 deaths per 1,000 births.

Correlation cannot give us that statement.
Second, regression allows us to include multiple predictors.
For example, we may want to estimate the relationship between water access and infant mortality while controlling for GDP per capita, population density, democracy, education, or health spending.
With multiple regression, we can estimate the association between one predictor and the outcome while holding other predictors constant.
That is why regression becomes so powerful.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
3. The Basic Linear Function
The basic linear function is:
[ Y = a + bX ]
where:

(Y) is the outcome variable
(X) is the predictor variable
(a) is the intercept
(b) is the slope

The intercept is the predicted value of (Y) when (X = 0).
The slope is the predicted change in (Y) for a one-unit increase in (X).
If:
[ b > 0 ]
then the relationship is positive.
If:
[ b < 0 ]
then the relationship is negative.
If:
[ b = 0 ]
then (X) does not predict (Y) linearly.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
4. The Intercept
The intercept tells us where the regression line crosses the (Y)-axis.
When (X = 0), the term (bX) becomes zero:
[ Y = a + b(0) ]
[ Y = a ]
So (a) is the predicted value of (Y) when (X = 0).
Sometimes this is substantively meaningful. Sometimes it is not.
For example, if we predict test scores using attendance rate, the intercept is the predicted test score for a student with 0 percent attendance. That may not be very meaningful if no student in the data has attendance close to 0.
The intercept is always mathematically necessary, but it is not always substantively interesting.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
5. The Slope
The slope tells us how much (Y) is predicted to change when (X) increases by one unit.
For example, suppose:
[ = 10.81 + 0.81X ]
Then each one-unit increase in (X) is associated with a predicted 0.81-unit increase in (Y).
If:
[ = 85.66 - 0.68X ]
then each one-unit increase in (X) is associated with a predicted 0.68-unit decrease in (Y).
The slope is the rise over the run.
The “run” is the change in (X).
The “rise” is the change in (Y).
The slope is measured in the units of (Y) per one unit of (X).

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
6. Predicted Values
Regression produces predicted values.
We write a predicted value as:
[ ]
The hat indicates that this is not the actual observed value of (Y). It is the value predicted by the model.
For a simple linear regression:
[ _i = _0 + _1X_i ]
where:

(_0) is the estimated intercept
(_1) is the estimated slope
(X_i) is the value of (X) for case (i)

Each predicted value lies on the regression line.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
7. Residuals
The model usually does not predict (Y) perfectly.
The difference between the observed value and the predicted value is called the residual:
[ _i = Y_i - _i ]
A residual is a prediction error.
If:
[ _i > 0 ]
then the model underpredicted (Y).
If:
[ _i < 0 ]
then the model overpredicted (Y).
Residuals are vertical distances between observed points and the regression line.
Regression chooses the line that minimizes the sum of squared residuals.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
8. The Population Regression Model
In statistical notation, the simple linear regression model is:
[ Y_i = _0 + _1X_i + u_i ]
where:

(Y_i) is the dependent variable for case (i)
(X_i) is the independent variable for case (i)
(_0) is the true intercept
(_1) is the true slope
(u_i) is the stochastic or random component

The term (u_i) represents all the factors that affect (Y_i) but are not included in the model.
It captures the idiosyncratic, unpredictable, or unmodeled part of (Y).

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
9. The Estimated Regression Model
Because we do not know the true population parameters, we estimate them using sample data.
The estimated model is:
[ _i = _0 + _1X_i ]
The estimated residual is:
[ _i = Y_i - _i ]
We use the sample to estimate:

(_0) with (_0)
(_1) with (_1)

The estimated line is the line of best fit.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
10. Ordinary Least Squares
The most common method for estimating a linear regression is ordinary least squares, or OLS.
OLS chooses the intercept and slope that minimize the sum of squared residuals:
[ SSE = _{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - _i)^2 ]
Substitute the regression equation:
[ SSE = _{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - _0 - _1X_i)^2 ]
OLS finds the values of (_0) and (_1) that make this quantity as small as possible.
Why square the residuals?
Because residuals can be positive or negative. If we simply added them, they would cancel out. Squaring makes them positive and gives larger errors more weight.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
11. The OLS Slope
The OLS estimate of the slope is:
[ _1 = { (X_i-{X})^2 } ]
The numerator measures covariation between (X) and (Y).
The denominator measures variation in (X).
If (X) and (Y) move together, the numerator is positive, producing a positive slope.
If (X) and (Y) move in opposite directions, the numerator is negative, producing a negative slope.
If (X) and (Y) do not covary, the numerator is close to zero, and the slope is close to zero.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
12. The OLS Intercept
Once we have the slope, the intercept is:
[ _0 = {Y} - _1{X} ]
This ensures that the regression line passes through the point:
[ ({X}, {Y}) ]
That is, the line passes through the mean of (X) and the mean of (Y).

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
13. Regression and Correlation
The formulas for Pearson’s (r) and the OLS slope are related.
Pearson’s (r) is:
[ r = { } ]
The OLS slope is:
[ _1 = { (X_i-{X})^2 } ]
Both use the same numerator: covariation between (X) and (Y).
They differ in the denominator.
Pearson’s (r) standardizes by variation in both (X) and (Y), making it unit-free and bounded between -1 and 1.
The OLS slope divides by variation in (X), leaving the result in the units of (Y) per unit of (X).

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
14. The Research Question
Suppose we want to explain variation in illiteracy across countries.
The dependent variable is:

illiteracy rate, measured as the percentage of the population that cannot read and write

The independent variable is:

democracy, measured on a scale from 0 to 10

A score of 0 means not democratic.
A score of 10 means highly democratic.
The theory is that democratic governments may be more responsive to citizens and therefore more likely to provide education and public services, reducing illiteracy.
The model is:
[ Illiteracy_i = _0 + _1 Democracy_i + u_i ]

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
15. Regression Results
Suppose the estimated regression is:
[ = 37.1 - 2.9(Democracy) ]
The intercept is:
[ 37.1 ]
The slope is:
[ -2.9 ]
Interpretation of the slope:

For each one-point increase on the democracy scale, the predicted illiteracy rate falls by 2.9 percentage points.

Interpretation of the intercept:

For a country with a democracy score of 0, the predicted illiteracy rate is 37.1 percent.

This assumes that a democracy score of 0 is meaningful and within the range of the data.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
16. Predictions
For a country with a democracy score of 3:
[ = 37.1 - 2.9(3) ]

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
17. Causal Language
Be careful.
The regression shows an association. It does not automatically prove causation.
A careful interpretation says:

Higher democracy scores are associated with lower illiteracy rates.

or:

A one-point increase in democracy is associated with a predicted 2.9-percentage-point decrease in illiteracy.

Avoid saying:

A one-point increase in democracy causes illiteracy to fall by 2.9 percentage points.

unless the research design supports a causal claim.
Other factors may matter, such as GDP per capita, colonial history, state capacity, conflict, or public health systems.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
18. Sampling Distributions of Regression Coefficients
Regression coefficients are sample statistics.
If we drew a different sample, we would get different estimates of the slope and intercept.
Therefore, (_1) has a sampling distribution.
The standard deviation of that sampling distribution is the standard error of (_1).
The standard error tells us how much the estimated slope typically varies from the true slope across repeated samples.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
19. Standard Error of the Slope
The true standard error of (_1) depends on:

The amount of unexplained variation in (Y)
The amount of variation in (X)

A simplified expression is:
[ SE_{_1} = ]
where:

(_u^2) is the variance of the stochastic component
((X_i-{X})^2) is variation in (X)

More unexplained variation in (Y) increases the standard error.
More variation in (X) decreases the standard error.
This makes intuitive sense. If (X) varies widely, it is easier to estimate the slope. If all values of (X) are clustered closely together, estimating the slope is unstable.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
20. t-Test for a Regression Coefficient
The usual null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: _1 = 0 ]
The alternative is:
[ H_A: _1 ]
The t-statistic is:
[ t = {SE_{_1}} ]
or simply:
[ t = {SE_{_1}} ]
Degrees of freedom are:
[ df = n-k ]
where (k) is the number of estimated coefficients, including the intercept.
For simple bivariate regression, we estimate two coefficients:

intercept
slope

So:
[ df = n-2 ]

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
section: 21. Confidence Interval for a Regression Coefficient
text:
21. Confidence Interval for a Regression Coefficient
A confidence interval for a coefficient is:
[ _1 t^*SE_{_1} ]
The t critical value depends on:

confidence level
degrees of freedom

For large samples, the 95 percent critical value is approximately:
[ 1.96 ]
For smaller samples, use the t-distribution.
If the confidence interval for (_1) does not include 0, then the coefficient is statistically significant at the corresponding level.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
22. Regression Output
Regression software usually reports:

coefficient estimate
standard error
t-statistic
p-value
sometimes confidence interval

For example:



Variable
Coefficient
Standard Error
t
p-value




Democracy
-2.90
0.60
-4.87
<0.001


Constant
37.10
4.20
8.83
<0.001



Interpretation:

The coefficient on democracy is negative.
The t-statistic is large in magnitude.
The p-value is very small.
We reject the null hypothesis that the true coefficient on democracy is 0.

There is statistically significant evidence of a negative association between democracy and illiteracy.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
23. Total, Explained, and Unexplained Variation
Regression partitions variation in (Y) into explained and unexplained parts.
The total sum of squares is:
[ TSS = (Y_i-{Y})^2 ]
This is total variation in (Y).
The explained sum of squares is:
[ ESS = (_i-{Y})^2 ]
This is the variation explained by the regression model.
The sum of squared errors is:
[ SSE = (Y_i-_i)^2 ]
This is unexplained variation, or residual variation.
These quantities are related:
[ TSS = ESS + SSE ]

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
24. R-Squared
The (R^2) statistic measures the proportion of variation in (Y) explained by the model.
[ R^2 = ]
Equivalently:
[ R^2 = 1- ]
(R^2) ranges from 0 to 1.
If:
[ R^2 = 0 ]
the model explains none of the variation in (Y).
If:
[ R^2 = 1 ]
the model explains all variation in (Y).
For example, if:
[ R^2 = 0.72 ]
then the model explains 72 percent of the variation in (Y).

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
25. Interpreting R-Squared
Suppose we regress infant mortality on access to improved water sources and obtain:
[ R^2 = 0.72 ]
A substantive interpretation is:

Access to an improved water source explains about 72 percent of the variation in infant mortality across countries in this sample.

More carefully:

According to this model, variation in access to improved water sources is associated with about 72 percent of the cross-national variation in infant mortality.

The second version avoids causal overclaiming.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
26. Adjusted R-Squared
Adding more independent variables generally increases (R^2), even if the variables add little real explanatory value.
The adjusted (R^2) penalizes for adding variables.
If (R^2) and adjusted (R^2) differ meaningfully, use adjusted (R^2) when comparing model fit.
In simple bivariate regression, they are often very similar.
In multiple regression, adjusted (R^2) is usually more informative.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
27. Standard Error of the Estimate
Regression output may report something called:

root MSE
residual standard error
standard error of the estimate

These refer to the typical prediction error.
It is the average size of the residual, in the scale of (Y).
For example, if the standard error of the estimate is:
[ 18.8 ]
in a model predicting infant mortality, then:

The model’s predicted infant mortality rate typically misses the actual rate by about 18.8 deaths per 1,000 births.

This is often more intuitive than (R^2) because it is in the units of the dependent variable.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
28. The F-Test for the Model
Regression output also reports an F-statistic.
In bivariate regression, the F-test asks whether the model as a whole explains a statistically significant amount of variation in (Y).
Because the model has only one independent variable, the F-test is essentially equivalent to the t-test for that variable.
In multiple regression, the F-test becomes more useful. It asks whether the independent variables, taken together, explain statistically significant variation in (Y).
The null hypothesis for the model F-test is:
[ H_0: _1 = _2 = = _k = 0 ]
If the p-value for the F-test is small, we reject that null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
29. Dummy Variables
A dichotomous independent variable has two values, usually coded:
[ 1 = ]
[ 0 = ]
These are often called dummy variables.
Examples:

program participant = 1, nonparticipant = 0
retired = 1, not retired = 0
Democrat = 1, not Democrat = 0
religion important = 1, religion not important = 0

Regression works with dummy variables because 0 and 1 can be included in the linear equation.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
30. Regression with a Dummy Variable
Suppose the model is:
[ Y_i = _0 + _1D_i + u_i ]
where (D_i) is a dummy variable.
If:
[ D_i = 0 ]
then:
[ _i = _0 ]
If:
[ D_i = 1 ]
then:
[ _i = _0 + _1 ]
So (_0) is the predicted mean for the reference group.
(_1) is the difference between the two group means.
This is equivalent to a difference-of-means test.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
section: 31. Example: Religion and Obama Thermometer Scores
text:
31. Example: Religion and Obama Thermometer Scores
Suppose we regress Obama thermometer scores on whether religion is important in a respondent’s life.
The model is:
[ = 63.92 - 4.71(ReligionImportant) ]
where:
[ ReligionImportant = 1 ]
if the respondent says religion is important, and:
[ ReligionImportant = 0 ]
otherwise.
For respondents who say religion is not important:
[ = 63.92 ]
For respondents who say religion is important:
[ = 63.92 - 4.71 ]
[ = 59.21 ]
Interpretation:

Respondents who say religion is important are predicted to rate Obama about 4.71 thermometer points lower than respondents who say religion is not important.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
32. Changing the Reference Category
If we reverse the coding, the interpretation changes but the substantive information does not.
Suppose:
[ ReligionNotImportant = 1 ]
and:
[ ReligionNotImportant = 0 ]
for those who say religion is important.
The model may become:
[ = 59.21 + 4.71(ReligionNotImportant) ]
Now the intercept is the predicted value for people who say religion is important.
The coefficient says people who say religion is not important are predicted to rate Obama 4.71 points higher.
Same relationship, different reference category.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
33. Why Dummy Variables Matter
Dummy variables become especially useful in multiple regression.
A simple difference-of-means test compares two groups without controlling for anything else.
Regression allows us to compare groups while controlling for other variables.
For example, we might estimate the association between religious importance and Obama thermometer scores while controlling for:

party identification
age
race
income
education
region

That is where regression becomes much more powerful than a simple t-test.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
34. The Multiple Regression Model
A multiple regression model includes more than one independent variable:
[ Y_i = _0 + 1X{1i} + 2X{2i} + 3X{3i} + u_i ]
Each coefficient represents the association between that predictor and (Y), holding the other predictors constant.
For example:
[ _1 ]
is the predicted change in (Y) for a one-unit increase in (X_1), holding (X_2) and (X_3) constant.
This is the key interpretation of multiple regression:

The effect of one variable, controlling for the others.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
section: 35. Example: Infant Mortality, Water, and GDP
text:
35. Example: Infant Mortality, Water, and GDP
Suppose we first estimate:
[ InfantMortality = _0 + _1 Water + u ]
and find:
[ _1 = -1.54 ]
This suggests that each one-percentage-point increase in access to improved water is associated with 1.54 fewer infant deaths per 1,000 births.
Now add GDP per capita:
[ InfantMortality = _0 + _1 Water + _2 (GDPpc) + u ]
Suppose the coefficient on water changes to:
[ _1 = -0.80 ]
This suggests the original coefficient was partly capturing the effect of wealth. Wealthier countries tend to have better water access and lower infant mortality.
Leaving GDP out produced omitted variable bias.
After controlling for GDP, the estimated association between water and infant mortality becomes smaller.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
36. Interpretation in Multiple Regression
Suppose the model is:
[ InfantMortality = 170.3 - 0.80(Water) - 12.4(GDPpc) ]
Interpretation of water:

Holding GDP per capita constant, each one-percentage-point increase in access to improved water is associated with 0.80 fewer infant deaths per 1,000 births.

Interpretation of log GDP per capita:

Holding water access constant, a one-unit increase in log GDP per capita is associated with 12.4 fewer infant deaths per 1,000 births.

When predictors are logged, interpretation becomes more complicated. Roughly, a one-unit increase in the natural log of a variable corresponds to multiplying the original variable by about 2.72.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
37. Linear Regression Assumes Linearity
Linear regression assumes that the relationship between (X) and (Y) can be reasonably represented by a straight line.
But many real relationships are nonlinear.
For example:

GDP per capita and infant mortality
population and disease spread
age and income
education and earnings
pollution exposure and health
program dosage and outcomes

If the relationship is nonlinear, a linear model may fit poorly or produce misleading predictions.
Always inspect scatterplots when possible.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
38. Exponential Relationships
An exponential relationship occurs when the effect of (X) on (Y) grows as (X) increases.
A linear model may underpredict at low values, overpredict in the middle, and underpredict again at high values.
One solution is to transform the variables.
For exponential relationships, taking the natural log of (Y), (X), or both can make the relationship more linear.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
39. Log Transformations
Common log models include:







Model
Approximate Interpretation




(Y) on (X)
One-unit increase in (X) changes (Y) by () units


((Y)) on (X)
One-unit increase in (X) changes (Y) by about (100) percent


(Y) on ((X))
One percent increase in (X) changes (Y) by about (/100) units


((Y)) on ((X))
One percent increase in (X) changes (Y) by about () percent



These are approximations but useful ones.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
40. Example: GDP and Infant Mortality
Suppose we regress infant mortality on GDP per capita in dollars and find a statistically significant relationship. But the scatterplot shows a strong nonlinear pattern.
At low levels of GDP, small increases are associated with large reductions in infant mortality.
At high levels of GDP, additional income has much smaller effects.
A log-log model may fit better:
[ (InfantMortality) = _0 + _1(GDPpc) + u ]
Suppose:
[ _1 = -0.69 ]
Interpretation:

A 1 percent increase in GDP per capita is associated with approximately a 0.69 percent decrease in infant mortality.

The log transformation often produces a better-fitting and more substantively reasonable model.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
41. Assumption 1: Conditional Mean Zero
The first assumption is:
[ E(u_i X_i) = 0 ]
This means that, after accounting for (X), the expected value of the stochastic component is zero.
In plain language:

There is nothing systematic left in the error term that is related to (X).

If this assumption is violated, we may have omitted variable bias.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
42. Omitted Variable Bias
Suppose the true model is:
[ Y = _0 + _1X + _2Z + u ]
but we estimate:
[ Y = _0 + _1X + u ]
If (Z) affects (Y) and is correlated with (X), then the coefficient on (X) will be biased.
Why?
Because (X) partly stands in for (Z). It captures some of (Z)’s explanatory power.
Solution:

identify the omitted variable
measure it
include it in the model

This is often difficult because important variables may be unknown or unmeasured.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
section: 43. Assumption 2: Independent and Identically Distributed Data
text:
43. Assumption 2: Independent and Identically Distributed Data
The second assumption is that observations are independently and identically distributed, often abbreviated IID.
This means:

Observations are independent of one another.
Observations come from the same underlying distribution.

Violations occur when observations are connected.
Examples:

students nested in classrooms
students nested in schools
people in the same household
repeated observations over time
countries in the same regional conflict
workers in the same firm

When observations are connected, residuals may be correlated.
This usually biases standard errors.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
44. Solutions to Non-Independence
If observations are clustered, use clustered standard errors.
For example, if students are nested in schools, cluster standard errors by school.
If observations are repeated over time, use standard errors robust to serial correlation, such as Newey-West standard errors.
Sometimes the model itself should also be changed. For example, we might include group-level variables or fixed effects.
The key idea:

If observations are not independent, the usual standard errors are not trustworthy.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
45. Assumption 3: Large Outliers Are Unlikely
OLS can be sensitive to outliers.
An outlier is especially influential when:

It has an unusual value of (Y) given (X).
It has an unusual value of (X), far from the mean of (X).

Outliers far from the center of (X) can pull the regression line toward themselves.
Before dropping an outlier, ask:

Is it a data entry error?
Is it measured incorrectly?
Does it belong in the population of interest?
Does it reveal an omitted variable?
Does the model need to be respecified?
Would a transformation help?

Do not drop observations merely because they are inconvenient.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
46. Example: Population Density and Assaults
Suppose we regress state assault rates on population density.
Including the District of Columbia may produce a strong positive relationship because DC has extremely high population density and a high assault rate.
But DC is not a state in the same way as the others. It is an urban federal district with no rural territory and a unique legal structure.
Dropping DC may be justified if the population of interest is the 50 states.
When DC is removed, the relationship may change dramatically, perhaps even becoming negative.
This illustrates how influential outliers can affect regression estimates.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
47. Assumption 4: Homoscedasticity
The fourth assumption is homoscedasticity.
This means the variance of the error term is constant across values of (X).
In symbols:
[ Var(u_i X_i) = ^2 ]
The opposite is heteroscedasticity.
Heteroscedasticity occurs when the variance of errors changes across values of (X).
For example, prediction errors may be small at low values of (X) and large at high values of (X).

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
48. Consequences of Heteroscedasticity
If heteroscedasticity is present, OLS coefficients remain unbiased under the other assumptions.
But the usual standard errors are wrong.
That affects:

t-statistics
p-values
confidence intervals
conclusions about statistical significance

Solution:
Use heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors.
These are often called robust standard errors.
There are different formulas, such as HC1 and HC3. HC3 is more conservative and often preferred in smaller samples.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
49. The Model
Suppose we predict student test scores using attendance rate.
The dependent variable is:

state test score

The independent variable is:

attendance rate, measured as percent of school days attended

There are 1,440 students in 19 schools.
Because students are nested within schools, observations are not fully independent. Students in the same school may share teachers, administrators, resources, peer environments, and neighborhood contexts.
Therefore, clustered standard errors by school may be appropriate.

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
50. Interpreting Results
Suppose the estimated model is:
[ = 138.53 + 1.36(AttendanceRate) ]
Interpretation of the intercept:

A student with 0 percent attendance is predicted to score 138.53 on the state test.

This may not be substantively realistic if no student has attendance near 0.
Interpretation of the slope:

Each one-percentage-point increase in attendance is associated with a predicted 1.36-point increase in the test score.

If a student has 80 percent attendance:
[ = 138.53 + 1.36(80) ]

title: Chapter 14: Linear Regression
text:
51. Interpreting R-Squared and Prediction Error
Suppose:
[ R^2 = 0.052 ]
Interpretation:

Attendance rate explains about 5.2 percent of the variation across students in test scores.

Suppose the residual standard error is:
[ 39.2 ]
Interpretation:

The model’s predicted test scores typically miss actual test scores by about 39.2 points.

This is the standard error of the estimate.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
1. Causality
A causal relationship exists when changes in one variable produce changes in another variable.
Suppose we have two variables:
[ X Y ]
The arrow indicates that (X) causes (Y), or that changes in (X) lead to changes in (Y).
In this framework, (Y) is the dependent variable. It is the outcome we care about. Its value depends, at least in part, on another variable.
(X) is the independent variable. It is the variable we think may be doing the causing. It is the predictor, treatment, intervention, or explanatory variable.
For example:

A job training program may affect employment.
A vaccine education program may affect vaccination rates.
A new gasoline blend may affect air quality.
Class size may affect student test scores.
Exposure to lead may affect child development.

In each case, we are interested in whether changes in (X) produce changes in (Y).
Researchers often ask:

What is your dependent variable?

This means:

What outcome are you trying to explain?

They may also ask:

What is your independent variable?

This means:

What factor do you think affects that outcome?

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
2. Conditions for Causality
For a causal relationship to exist, three basic conditions must hold.
1. Covariation
The independent variable and dependent variable must move together.
If (X) changes but (Y) never changes, then (X) cannot be causing (Y).
For example, if a new education program is introduced but test scores remain exactly the same for everyone, there is no evidence that the program affected test scores.
Covariation does not prove causation, but causation requires covariation.
2. Temporal Ordering
The cause must come before the effect.
If (X) causes (Y), then (X) must occur or change before (Y) changes.
For example, if we argue that a vaccine education program affects later vaccination behavior, the education program must occur before the vaccination decision.
If (Y) changes before (X), then (X) cannot be the cause of that change in (Y).
3. Elimination of Alternative Explanations
There must not be another variable that explains the relationship between (X) and (Y).
This is often the hardest condition to satisfy.
Suppose we observe that students in smaller classes have lower test scores. It might be tempting to conclude that smaller classes cause worse performance. But perhaps struggling students are intentionally assigned to smaller classes so that they can receive more attention. In that case, the apparent relationship between class size and test scores reflects the assignment process, not the causal effect of class size.
To make a causal claim, we must rule out plausible alternative explanations.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
3. Correlation Is Not Causation
A correlation between two variables does not necessarily imply a causal relationship.
Two variables may be associated for many reasons. One may cause the other, but other possibilities exist.
There may be:

a third variable causing both
reverse causality
simultaneity
selection effects
measurement problems
random coincidence
omitted variables
confounding factors

The challenge of causal inference is to determine whether an observed association reflects a real causal relationship.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
4. Spurious Relationships
A relationship is spurious when two variables appear to be related, but the relationship is actually caused by some third factor.
Consider a simple example.
A child notices that balloons with strings float, while balloons without strings do not. The child asks:

Why do balloons with strings float?

The string seems associated with floating. But the string is not causing the balloon to float. The real cause is helium. Balloons filled with helium float, and people attach strings to helium balloons so they can hold on to them.
The relationship between strings and floating is spurious. Helium causes both:

the balloon floating
the need for a string

The child observed a real association but misunderstood the causal mechanism.
This is a simple example, but the same logic appears constantly in social science and policy analysis. We may observe a relationship between (X) and (Y), but a third factor (Z) may be responsible for the apparent association.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
5. Reverse Causality and Simultaneity
Sometimes the causal direction may be reversed.
We may think:
[ X Y ]
but in reality:
[ Y X ]
For example, suppose we observe that people who visit doctors more often are less healthy. Does visiting the doctor make people sick? Probably not. More likely, being sick causes people to visit doctors more often.
That is reverse causality.
Another possibility is simultaneity, where two variables move together as part of a broader process, rather than one clearly causing the other.
Economic indicators provide an example. During an economic expansion, employment rises, consumer confidence increases, incomes grow, and spending increases. These variables move together, but that does not mean any one of them alone is causing all the others. They may be jointly shaped by broader economic conditions.
As the saying goes, a rising tide lifts all boats. The boats are not causing each other to rise; the tide is.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
section: 6. Example: Class Size and Test Scores
text:
6. Example: Class Size and Test Scores
Research sometimes finds that children in smaller classrooms perform worse on standardized tests, even after controlling for demographic factors.
At first, this seems surprising. Many people expect smaller classes to improve learning.
But there may be a selection process at work. Students who are already struggling may be placed into smaller classes to receive more support. If so, smaller classes are not causing lower test scores. Instead, lower-performing students are being assigned to smaller classes.
The assignment process confounds the relationship between class size and test scores.
This example illustrates why causal inference is difficult. We cannot simply compare outcomes across groups and assume the group difference is caused by the variable that defines the groups.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
7. Controlling for Confounding Factors
A confounding factor is a variable that affects the outcome and is also related to the independent variable.
Confounders make it difficult to isolate the causal effect of (X) on (Y).
To address confounding, researchers try to control for other factors.
To control for a variable means to compare cases that are similar on that variable. If we hold a third factor constant and still observe a relationship between (X) and (Y), then that third factor cannot fully explain the relationship.
For example, suppose we want to know whether education affects income. Age may be a confounder because older people tend to have more education and also more work experience. If we compare people with different levels of education but similar ages, we are controlling for age.
In practice, controlling for confounders can be difficult. We may not have data on the relevant variables. Worse, we may not even know what all the relevant confounders are.
This is one reason experiments are so powerful.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
8. Theory and Causal Mechanisms
Statistical evidence is more persuasive when paired with a strong theory.
A causal mechanism explains how or why (X) affects (Y).
For example, if we claim that a vaccine education program increases vaccination rates, we should be able to explain the mechanism:

Parents receive accurate information.
The information reduces uncertainty or misinformation.
Parents become more confident in vaccine safety.
Parents are more likely to vaccinate their children.

If we observe a statistical relationship but cannot explain why it should exist, we should be cautious. The relationship may be spurious or accidental.
This is especially important when interpreting studies reported in the media. A study may find an association between a food, behavior, or lifestyle factor and some health outcome. But if researchers cannot explain a plausible biological or social mechanism, we should not leap too quickly to causal conclusions.
A statistically significant association is not enough. Causal claims require theory, design, and evidence.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
9. Experiments as the Gold Standard
Experiments are often considered the strongest design for identifying causal effects.
In an experiment, researchers control the assignment of the independent variable. Some cases receive the treatment, while others remain in a control condition.
A common experimental design is the randomized controlled trial, or RCT.
In an RCT:

Researchers identify a sample.
Cases are randomly assigned to treatment or control.
The treatment group receives the intervention.
The control group does not.
Outcomes are compared across groups.

The key feature is random assignment.
Random assignment helps ensure that the treatment and control groups are similar in all respects except for the treatment itself.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
10. Why Experiments Help Identify Causality
Experiments help satisfy the three conditions for causality.
Covariation
We can compare outcomes between treated and untreated groups to see whether (Y) differs when (X) differs.
Temporal Ordering
The researcher controls when the treatment is administered, so the treatment occurs before the outcome is measured.
Elimination of Alternative Explanations
Random assignment distributes confounding factors across treatment and control groups. On average, the groups should be similar in both observed and unobserved characteristics.
