This is the major advantage of randomization. It can balance even factors the researcher does not know about or cannot measure.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
11. Treatment and Control Groups
An experiment divides cases into groups.
The treatment group receives the intervention.
The control group does not receive the intervention or receives a baseline condition, such as a placebo.
For example, in a drug trial:

The treatment group receives the medication.
The control group receives a placebo.

In a policy evaluation:

The treatment group receives the program.
The control group does not.

The goal is for the control group to represent what would have happened to the treatment group if it had not received the treatment.
Likewise, the treatment group represents what would have happened to the control group if it had received the treatment.
Randomization makes this comparison credible.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
12. Randomization
Randomization means assigning cases to treatment or control by chance.
If randomization works, the treatment and control groups should be similar on average.
They should have similar distributions of:

age
gender
income
prior achievement
health status
motivation
family background
political attitudes
any other factor that might matter

Some differences may still occur by random chance, especially in small samples. But randomization prevents systematic selection into treatment.
This is crucial because selection can otherwise bias causal estimates.
For example, suppose people who choose to participate in a job training program are more motivated than those who do not. If we compare participants to nonparticipants, differences in employment may reflect motivation rather than the program. Random assignment avoids this problem by assigning participation independently of motivation.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
13. Balance
After random assignment, researchers often check whether the treatment and control groups are balanced.
A balance check compares the groups on characteristics measured before treatment.
For example, researchers may compare average age, income, prior test scores, or baseline health across treatment and control groups.
If randomization worked well, the groups should look similar.
Perfect balance is not guaranteed, especially in small samples. Randomization can fail by chance. If researchers notice serious imbalance before the experiment begins, they may be able to re-randomize.
Once the experiment has already occurred, imbalance becomes harder to fix. Researchers may then use statistical controls, regression, or matching methods to adjust for differences.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
14. Potential Outcomes
The potential outcomes framework provides a formal way to think about causal effects.
For each person or case (i), there are two potential outcomes:
[ Y_i(1) ]
and:
[ Y_i(0) ]
where:

(Y_i(1)) is the outcome for case (i) if treated.
(Y_i(0)) is the outcome for case (i) if not treated.

For example, suppose the outcome is employment after a job training program.
For each person:

(Y_i(1)): employment outcome if the person receives training
(Y_i(0)): employment outcome if the person does not receive training

The individual treatment effect is:
[ Y_i(1) - Y_i(0) ]
This is the causal effect of treatment for that individual.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
section: 15. The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
text:
15. The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference
The problem is that we can never observe both potential outcomes for the same person at the same time.
A person either receives the treatment or does not.
If a person receives treatment, we observe:
[ Y_i(1) ]
but we do not observe:
[ Y_i(0) ]
If a person does not receive treatment, we observe:
[ Y_i(0) ]
but we do not observe:
[ Y_i(1) ]
The missing potential outcome is the counterfactual.
This is called the fundamental problem of causal inference.
To know the exact treatment effect for one individual, we would need to observe what happened to that person under treatment and what would have happened to that same person without treatment. But we cannot observe both.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
16. Average Treatment Effects
Because we cannot observe individual treatment effects directly, we often estimate average treatment effects.
The average treatment effect, or ATE, is:
[ ATE = E[Y(1) - Y(0)] ]
This is the expected difference between outcomes under treatment and outcomes under control.
Equivalently, under random assignment:
[ ATE = E[Y(1)] - E[Y(0)] ]
Randomization allows us to estimate this using observed group means:
[ = {Y}{treatment} - {Y}{control} ]
That is, we compare the mean outcome in the treatment group to the mean outcome in the control group.
Randomization makes this comparison valid because the groups are similar on average except for treatment status.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
17. Why Randomization Solves the Problem
Randomization does not allow us to observe both potential outcomes for the same person.
But it does allow us to compare groups that are equivalent on average.
The control group provides an estimate of what would have happened to the treatment group if it had not been treated.
The treatment group provides an estimate of what would have happened to the control group if it had been treated.
Thus, while individual treatment effects remain unobservable, the average treatment effect can be estimated.
This is the logic behind experimental causal inference.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
18. Difference of Means
The simplest way to estimate an experimental treatment effect is a difference of means:
[ {Y}_T - {Y}_C ]
where:

({Y}_T) is the mean outcome in the treatment group
({Y}_C) is the mean outcome in the control group

The null hypothesis is usually:
[ H_0: _T - _C = 0 ]
That is, the treatment has no effect.
The alternative hypothesis is:
[ H_A: _T - _C ]
or, in a directional test:
[ H_A: _T - _C > 0 ]
or:
[ H_A: _T - _C < 0 ]
The statistical question becomes:

Is the observed difference between the two group means large enough, relative to its standard error, to reject the null hypothesis?

We will develop the formal difference-of-means test next.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
19. Internal Validity
Internal validity refers to whether the experiment successfully identifies the causal effect within the study.
An experiment has strong internal validity if differences in outcomes between the treatment and control groups can be attributed to the treatment.
In other words:

Did the experiment rule out alternative explanations?

Strong internal validity requires that:

treatment assignment is random
treatment and control groups are comparable
the treatment is implemented as intended
outcomes are measured consistently
there is no contamination between groups
attrition does not differ systematically across groups
participants do not change behavior simply because they know they are being studied, unless that is part of the treatment

If internal validity is weak, then the estimated treatment effect may be biased.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
20. External Validity
External validity refers to whether the results generalize beyond the study setting.
An experiment may have strong internal validity but weak external validity.
For example, suppose a chemical is tested on mice in a laboratory. The experiment may be carefully controlled, with strong internal validity. But the results may not generalize perfectly to humans exposed to lower levels of the chemical in real-world conditions.
External validity asks:

Does this result apply to other populations?
Does it apply in other places?
Does it apply at other times?
Does it apply under real-world conditions?
Would the effect remain if the program were scaled up?

Policy researchers care deeply about external validity because policy decisions are usually made for broader populations than the experimental sample.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
21. Internal and External Validity Tradeoffs
Sometimes internal and external validity are in tension.
A tightly controlled laboratory experiment may have strong internal validity because the researcher controls almost everything. But it may have weaker external validity because the setting is artificial.
A real-world field experiment may have stronger external validity because it occurs in natural conditions. But it may face more threats to internal validity because the researcher has less control.
Good research design tries to maximize both, but tradeoffs are common.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
22. The Study
Consider an experiment on teacher absenteeism in rural India.
Public sector absenteeism can be a major problem in some contexts. Teachers may not show up for work, which can reduce instructional time and student learning.
Researchers studied whether incentives could reduce teacher absenteeism.
The study took place in rural villages near Udaipur, in Rajasthan, India. Researchers worked with a local NGO and identified 120 schools.
The schools were randomly assigned:

60 schools to the treatment group
60 schools to the control group

Teachers in treatment schools received digital cameras and were asked to document their attendance. They took photos when they arrived and when they left. They could also take photos during the school day.
Teachers were paid bonuses based on verified attendance. If they were present for at least five hours, they received additional pay.
The control schools did not receive this intervention.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
23. Results
The study found that absenteeism was lower in treatment schools.

Treatment schools: 22 percent absenteeism
Control schools: 42 percent absenteeism

The researchers also examined student outcomes. Students in treatment schools scored approximately 0.15 to 0.17 standard deviations higher on language and math tests.
The study therefore suggested that attendance incentives, combined with monitoring, reduced absenteeism and improved student learning.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
24. Internal Validity Questions
The study has important strengths for internal validity.
Schools were randomly assigned to treatment and control groups. This helps ensure that treatment and control schools were similar on average before the intervention.
However, several internal validity questions remain.
Did Randomization Work?
Even with random assignment, groups may differ by chance. Researchers should check whether treatment and control schools were balanced on pre-treatment characteristics.
Was There Spillover?
Teachers in control schools may have learned about the treatment and changed their behavior. If so, the control group may no longer represent the no-treatment condition.
Was the Treatment Actually the Incentive?
The intervention included both monitoring and financial incentives. If absenteeism declined, was it because of the bonus, because teachers knew they were being watched, or because of both?
Did Awareness of Being Studied Matter?
Teachers in the treatment group knew they were being monitored. They may have changed behavior because they were part of a study. This is related to the Hawthorne effect, where people alter behavior because they know they are being observed.
Were Outcomes Measured Consistently?
If student test scores were measured differently across schools, or if treatment schools had stronger incentives to present better outcomes, that could threaten internal validity.
These concerns do not necessarily invalidate the study, but they illustrate the kinds of questions researchers must ask.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
25. External Validity Questions
External validity asks whether the findings generalize beyond the study.
Several questions arise.
Would the Program Work Elsewhere?
The study was conducted in one region of India. Would the same incentive system work in urban schools, other Indian states, or other countries?
Would It Work Where Salaries Are Higher?
If teachers are already well paid, financial incentives may have a weaker effect.
Would It Work at Scale?
A pilot program may work under close supervision by researchers and an NGO. A statewide or national program may be harder to monitor and implement.
Would Teachers Adapt?
Over time, teachers may learn ways to comply superficially without improving instruction. The short-term effect may differ from the long-term effect.
Are the Schools Representative?
If the schools in the study differ from other schools, the results may not generalize.
These are external validity questions. They concern whether the result applies beyond the specific experimental setting.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
26. When Experiments Are Not Possible
Experiments are powerful, but they are not always possible.
Sometimes experiments are unethical.
For example, we cannot randomly assign children to drink lead-contaminated water to study the effect of lead exposure.
Sometimes experiments are impractical.
We cannot randomly assign people to different childhood family backgrounds, neighborhoods, political systems, or natural disasters.
Sometimes experiments are too expensive or politically impossible.
In these cases, researchers rely on observational data.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
27. Observational Data
In an observational study, researchers observe data generated by the world rather than assigning treatment themselves.
For example, researchers may compare:

children exposed to lead versus children not exposed to lead
students in small classes versus large classes
workers who joined a training program versus those who did not
counties that adopted a policy versus counties that did not
people with more education versus less education

The problem is that treatment status is not randomly assigned.
People or places in one group may differ systematically from those in another group.
Those differences may explain the outcome.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
28. The Goal in Observational Studies
Even without randomization, the goal remains the same:

Compare groups that are as similar as possible except for the independent variable of interest.

If we want to estimate the effect of a savings program, we want treatment and comparison groups that are similar in income, age, gender, education, prior savings behavior, and other relevant factors.
If we want to estimate the effect of lead exposure, we want exposed and unexposed children who are otherwise similar in family background, neighborhood, school quality, health status, and other factors.
The challenge is that without random assignment, similarity is not guaranteed.
Researchers must try to construct credible comparisons using statistical methods and research design.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
29. Threats in Observational Studies
Observational studies face several threats.
Selection Bias
People who receive a treatment may differ from those who do not.
For example, people who enroll in a job training program may be more motivated than those who do not.
Confounding
A third variable may affect both treatment and outcome.
For example, family income may affect both neighborhood lead exposure and child development outcomes.
Reverse Causality
The assumed outcome may actually influence the treatment.
For example, poor health may cause more doctor visits, not the reverse.
Omitted Variables
Important variables may be missing from the data.
For example, motivation, family support, stress, or teacher quality may be difficult to measure.
Unobserved Heterogeneity
Groups may differ in ways researchers cannot observe.
This is one of the hardest problems in causal inference.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
30. Statistical Controls
One strategy in observational studies is to use statistical controls.
For example, suppose we compare savings outcomes between people who participated in a savings program and those who did not. If the treatment group has higher income on average, income may confound the analysis.
We can control for income using regression or matching.
The goal is to compare people with similar income levels but different treatment status.
Statistical controls help, but they only work for variables we observe and measure well.
They cannot control for variables that are missing, unknown, or poorly measured.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
31. Matching
Another strategy is matching.
Matching pairs treated cases with similar untreated cases.
For example, a woman who participated in a savings program might be matched with a woman of similar age, income, education, family status, and prior savings behavior who did not participate.
The goal is to approximate an experimental comparison by making the treated and comparison groups as similar as possible.
Matching can improve causal inference, but it still depends on observed variables. If treated and untreated cases differ on unobserved factors, bias may remain.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
32. Quasi-Experiments
A quasi-experiment is a research design that attempts to approximate an experiment without random assignment.
Examples include:

natural experiments
regression discontinuity designs
difference-in-differences designs
instrumental variables
matching designs

These methods are designed to create or exploit comparison groups that approximate random assignment.
Quasi-experiments are especially important in policy research because true experiments are often unavailable.
You will encounter many of these designs in future program evaluation and econometrics courses.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
33. Comparing Means Across Groups
To evaluate causal effects, we often compare mean outcomes across groups.
For example:
[ {Y}_T - {Y}_C ]
where:

({Y}_T) is the mean outcome in the treatment group
({Y}_C) is the mean outcome in the control group

If the treatment has no effect, we expect:
[ _T - _C = 0 ]
So the null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: _T - _C = 0 ]
If the observed difference in sample means is large relative to its standard error, we may reject the null hypothesis.
This is the basis of the difference-of-means test.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
34. Comparing Proportions Across Groups
Sometimes the outcome is binary.
For example:

vaccinated or not vaccinated
employed or not employed
graduated or did not graduate
voted or did not vote

Then we compare proportions:
[ _T - _C ]
The null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: _T - _C = 0 ]
or:
[ H_0: _T = _C ]
If the difference in sample proportions is large relative to its standard error, we may reject the null hypothesis.
This is the basis of the difference-of-proportions test.

title: Chapter 9: Experiments and Causality
text:
35. Mean Comparison Tables
A simple way to begin comparing groups is with a mean comparison table.
For example:



Group
Mean Outcome




Treatment
82.4


Control
76.1


Difference
6.3



This table shows that the treatment group has a higher mean outcome than the control group.
But the table alone does not tell us whether the difference is statistically significant.
To answer that, we need a standard error and a significance test.
The next step is to ask:

Is the observed difference large enough relative to sampling variability that we can reject the null hypothesis of no difference?

That is where two-sample inference begins.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 1. The Big Idea of Statistical Inference
text:
1. The Big Idea of Statistical Inference
Statistical inference is the process of using sample data to make claims about a population.
We usually cannot observe the full population. Instead, we collect a sample and calculate sample statistics.
For example:

We use a sample mean ({Y}) to estimate a population mean ().
We use a sample proportion () to estimate a population proportion ().
We use a sample standard deviation (s) to estimate a population standard deviation ().

These sample statistics are point estimates.
A point estimate is a single-number estimate of a population parameter.
For example, suppose the Current Population Survey estimates that the unemployment rate in the United States is 4.3 percent. That 4.3 percent is a point estimate. It is based on sample data, not a complete count of every person in the country.
Because it comes from a sample, it is probably not exactly equal to the true unemployment rate. It is an estimate.
The natural question is:

How precise is that estimate?

That is what confidence intervals help us communicate.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 2. What the Central Limit Theorem Gives Us
text:
2. What the Central Limit Theorem Gives Us
The Central Limit Theorem tells us that, under appropriate conditions, the sampling distribution of a sample mean is approximately normal.
For a random sample of size (n), drawn from a population with mean () and standard deviation (), the sample mean ({Y}) has a sampling distribution that is:

Approximately normal under the right conditions.
Centered at the true population mean ().
Has standard deviation:

[ _{{Y}} = ]
This standard deviation of the sampling distribution is called the standard error.
The standard error tells us how much sample means typically vary from the true population mean.
So, for a sample mean:
[ SE_{{Y}} = ]
if () is known.
Usually, () is not known, so we estimate it using the sample standard deviation (s):
[ _{{Y}} = ]
This is the estimated standard error.
The Central Limit Theorem gives us the foundation for confidence intervals because it tells us the shape, center, and spread of the sampling distribution.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
3. Sampling Error
A sample statistic usually differs from the population parameter.
The difference is called sampling error.
For a sample mean, sampling error is:
[ {Y} - ]
For a sample proportion, sampling error is:
[ - ]
We usually do not know the actual sampling error because we do not know the population parameter. If we knew () or (), we would not need to estimate it.
But we can estimate the typical size of sampling error using the standard error.
The standard error tells us, on average across repeated samples, how much a sample statistic tends to miss the true population parameter.
That is the key to inference.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
4. Point Estimates
A point estimate is a single value calculated from sample data and used to estimate a population parameter.
Examples include:
[ {Y} ]
as an estimate of:
[ ]
and:
[ ]
as an estimate of:
[ ]
For example:

A survey estimates unemployment at 4.3 percent.
A sample estimates average years of education as 10 years.
A poll estimates candidate support at 47 percent.
A survey estimates dog ownership at 44 percent.

Each of these is a point estimate.
Point estimates are useful, but they are incomplete. They do not tell us how uncertain we are.
A point estimate of 47 percent with a large sample may be very precise. A point estimate of 47 percent with a tiny sample may be extremely uncertain.
That is why we need interval estimates.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
5. Interval Estimates
An interval estimate gives a range around a point estimate.
The most familiar interval estimate is the confidence interval.
In everyday reporting, you may hear the phrase margin of error. For example:

Candidate A has 47 percent support, with a margin of error of plus or minus 2 percentage points.

That means the point estimate is 47 percent, and the interval estimate extends 2 percentage points in each direction.
So the interval runs from:
[ 47 - 2 = 45 ]
to:
[ 47 + 2 = 49 ]
The margin of error communicates the precision of the estimate.
A smaller margin of error means the estimate is more precise.
A larger margin of error means the estimate is less precise.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
6. Example: The Unemployment Rate
Suppose the Bureau of Labor Statistics reports that the unemployment rate is 4.3 percent.
That number comes from survey data, specifically the Current Population Survey. Because it is based on a sample, it has sampling error.
Suppose the technical documentation reports that the margin of error for a 90 percent confidence interval is plus or minus 0.2 percentage points.
Then the interval is:
[ 4.3 ]
or:
[ 4.1 4.5 ]
This interval communicates uncertainty around the point estimate.
The reported unemployment rate is 4.3 percent, but the survey procedure has a degree of imprecision. The confidence interval expresses that imprecision.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
7. What Is a Confidence Interval?
A confidence interval is a numerical interval constructed around a point estimate using a procedure that captures the true population parameter a specified percentage of the time across repeated random samples.
That definition is careful for a reason.
A 95 percent confidence interval does not mean:

There is a 95 percent chance that the true population parameter is inside this specific interval.

That is the tempting interpretation, but it is not technically correct.
Instead, a 95 percent confidence interval means:

If we repeatedly drew random samples and constructed confidence intervals using this same procedure, 95 percent of those intervals would contain the true population parameter.

The confidence level describes the long-run success rate of the procedure.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 8. The General Form of a Confidence Interval
text:
8. The General Form of a Confidence Interval
All confidence intervals have the same basic structure:
[ ]
The margin of error has two parts:
[ = ]
So:
[ = ()() ]
The standard error tells us how much random sampling error we expect.
The critical value tells us how far out we need to go in the sampling distribution to achieve the desired level of confidence.
For a 95 percent confidence interval using the standard normal distribution, the critical value is:
[ 1.96 ]
because 95 percent of the standard normal distribution lies between:
[ -1.96 ]
and:
[ 1.96 ]

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
9. Confidence Levels
The confidence level is the long-run percentage of intervals that would contain the true population parameter if we repeated the sampling process many times.
Common confidence levels include:








Confidence Level
Alpha ()
Two-Tailed Critical Value, Normal Approximation




90%
0.10
1.64 or 1.645


95%
0.05
1.96


99%
0.01
2.58 or 2.576



The higher the confidence level, the wider the interval.
Why?
Because to be more confident, we must cover more of the sampling distribution.
A 90 percent confidence interval is narrower than a 95 percent confidence interval.
A 99 percent confidence interval is wider than a 95 percent confidence interval.
There is a tradeoff:

Higher confidence means a wider interval.
Lower confidence means a narrower interval.
Wider intervals are less precise.
Narrower intervals are more precise but less confident.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
10. Alpha
The symbol (), called alpha, represents the probability that the confidence interval procedure fails to capture the true parameter.
It is the complement of the confidence level.
For a 95 percent confidence interval:
[ = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05 ]
For a 90 percent confidence interval:
[ = 1 - 0.90 = 0.10 ]
For a 99 percent confidence interval:
[ = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01 ]
In a two-sided confidence interval, () is split between the two tails of the sampling distribution.
For a 95 percent confidence interval:
[ = 0.05 ]
so each tail contains:
[ = 0.025 ]
That is why the critical values are:
[ -1.96 ]
and:
[ 1.96 ]
Those values leave 2.5 percent in the lower tail and 2.5 percent in the upper tail.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
11. Critical Values
A critical value is the value from a probability distribution that corresponds to a desired confidence level.
For a 95 percent confidence interval using the standard normal distribution, the critical value is:
[ z^* = 1.96 ]
For a 90 percent confidence interval:
[ z^* ]
For a 99 percent confidence interval:
[ z^* ]
The critical value tells us how many standard errors to move away from the point estimate.
For a 95 percent confidence interval:
[ (SE) ]
For a 99 percent confidence interval:
[ (SE) ]
A larger critical value produces a wider interval.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 12. What Confidence Intervals Do Not Mean
text:
12. What Confidence Intervals Do Not Mean
A confidence interval is often misinterpreted.
Suppose we calculate a 95 percent confidence interval for a mean and get:
[ 9.752 10.248 ]
It is tempting to say:

There is a 95 percent chance that the true population mean lies between 9.752 and 10.248.

But that is not technically correct.
The true population mean is fixed. It is a number. We do not know it, but it is not random.
The interval is random before we collect the sample because the sample is random. Once we collect the sample and calculate the interval, the interval is fixed too.
At that point, the true mean either is inside the interval or it is not.
So the probability is not 0.95 for this specific interval. It is either 1 or 0. We just do not know which.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
13. The Ring Toss Analogy
A useful analogy is a ring toss.
Imagine the true population mean is a stake in the ground. We cannot see the stake. We are blindfolded.
Our statistical procedure is like tossing a ring toward the stake.
A 95 percent confidence procedure means that, in the long run, 95 percent of the rings we toss will land around the stake.
But once a ring has landed, it either surrounds the stake or it does not. It no longer makes sense to say there is a 95 percent chance that this particular ring surrounds the stake.
The probability describes the procedure before the toss, not the ring after it lands.
The same is true for confidence intervals.
Before collecting data, there is a 95 percent chance that the interval we will construct will contain the true parameter.
After collecting data, the interval either contains the true parameter or it does not.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
14. Correct Interpretations
A good interpretation of a 95 percent confidence interval is:

Across repeated random samples, 95 percent of confidence intervals constructed using this procedure would contain the true population mean.

Or:

We used a procedure that, in repeated sampling, produces intervals containing the true population mean 95 percent of the time.

Then, if useful, we can state the interval:

In this case, the interval ranges from 9.752 to 10.248.

What we should avoid is language like:

There is a 95 percent probability that the true mean is between 9.752 and 10.248.

or:

The true mean falls between 9.752 and 10.248 with probability 0.95.

Those statements incorrectly treat the population parameter as random after the interval has already been constructed.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 15. Confidence Interval for a Mean: Ideal Case
text:
15. Confidence Interval for a Mean: Ideal Case
Suppose we want to estimate a population mean ().
If we knew the population standard deviation (), then the standard error of the sample mean would be:
[ SE_{{Y}} = ]
A confidence interval for the mean would be:
[ {Y} z^*() ]
where:

({Y}) is the sample mean
(z^*) is the critical value from the standard normal distribution
() is the population standard deviation
(n) is the sample size

This ideal case is useful theoretically, but it is uncommon in practice because we usually do not know ().

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 16. Confidence Interval for a Mean: Practical Case
text:
16. Confidence Interval for a Mean: Practical Case
Usually, we do not know the population standard deviation ().
Instead, we estimate it using the sample standard deviation (s).
The estimated standard error is:
[ _{{Y}} = ]
Because we estimate (), we introduce additional uncertainty. To account for this, we use the t-distribution rather than the standard normal distribution.
The confidence interval for a mean is:
[ {Y} t^*() ]
where:

({Y}) is the sample mean
(t^*) is the critical value from the t-distribution
(s) is the sample standard deviation
(n) is the sample size

The quantity:
[ t^*() ]
is the margin of error.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
17. Why the t-Distribution?
The t-distribution looks similar to the normal distribution, but it has more area in the tails.
That extra tail area reflects additional uncertainty from estimating () with (s).
When sample size is small, (s) may be a noisy estimate of (). The t-distribution accounts for that by producing wider intervals.
As sample size increases, the t-distribution becomes nearly identical to the standard normal distribution.
For large samples, using (t) or (z) makes almost no difference.
For small samples, using (t) matters.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
18. Degrees of Freedom
The t-distribution depends on degrees of freedom.
For a one-sample confidence interval for a mean, the degrees of freedom are:
[ df = n - 1 ]
The smaller the degrees of freedom, the heavier the tails of the t-distribution.
As degrees of freedom increase, the t-distribution approaches the standard normal distribution.
In practice:

With small samples, use the t-distribution.
With large samples, the t-distribution and normal distribution are nearly the same.
For means, using the t-distribution is generally safe.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 19. Conditions for a Confidence Interval for a Mean
text:
19. Conditions for a Confidence Interval for a Mean
To construct a confidence interval for a mean using the usual t procedure, we need appropriate conditions.
The sample should be random.
The observations should be independent or approximately independent.
The sampling distribution of the mean should be approximately normal.
That last condition is satisfied if either:

The population distribution is approximately normal, or
The sample size is large enough, usually (n ).

If the sample size is less than 30 and the population distribution is strongly skewed or otherwise non-normal, the usual t procedure may not be appropriate.
The t-distribution adjusts for estimating (). It does not fix a non-normal sampling distribution caused by a small sample from a non-normal population.
That distinction is important.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 20. Example: Confidence Interval for Mean Years of Education
text:
20. Example: Confidence Interval for Mean Years of Education
Suppose we survey:
[ n = 1000 ]
people and measure years of education.
The sample mean is:
[ {Y} = 10 ]
The sample standard deviation is:
[ s = 4 ]
We want a 95 percent confidence interval for the population mean years of education.
The formula is:
[ {Y} t^*() ]
Because (n = 1000), the t critical value for a 95 percent confidence interval is essentially the same as the z critical value:
[ t^* ]
Now calculate the estimated standard error:
[ = ]
[ ]
[ ]
Now calculate the margin of error:
[ 1.96(0.1265) ]
So the confidence interval is:
[ 10 ]
Lower bound:
[ 10 - 0.248 = 9.752 ]
Upper bound:
[ 10 + 0.248 = 10.248 ]
The 95 percent confidence interval is:
[ 9.752 10.248 ]
A correct interpretation is:

We used a procedure that, across repeated random samples, would produce confidence intervals containing the true population mean 95 percent of the time. In this sample, the resulting interval ranges from 9.752 to 10.248 years of education.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
21. Population and Sample Proportions
A population proportion is the proportion of cases in the population that fall into a category.
We represent the population proportion as:
[ ]
Examples include:

the proportion of voters who support Candidate A
the proportion of students who graduate from high school
the proportion of households that own a dog
the proportion of adults who are unemployed

The sample proportion is written:
[ ]
The hat indicates that () estimates ().
If 44 percent of survey respondents own a dog, then:
[ = 0.44 ]

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 22. Proportions as Means of Zeros and Ones
text:
22. Proportions as Means of Zeros and Ones
A sample proportion can be understood as the mean of a binary variable.
Suppose we code dog ownership as:
[ 1 = ]
[ 0 = ]
If 44 percent of people in the sample own a dog, then the mean of this 0/1 variable is:
[ 0.44 ]
That is the sample proportion.
Because proportions are means of binary variables, the Central Limit Theorem applies to proportions as well. However, because binary variables are discrete and bounded by 0 and 1, the conditions are somewhat different.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
23. Standard Error for a Proportion
The true standard error of a sample proportion is:
[ SE_{} = ]
where:

() is the population proportion
(n) is the sample size

But we usually do not know (), so we estimate it with ().
The estimated standard error is:
[ _{} = ]
This tells us how much sample proportions typically vary from the true population proportion.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 24. Why We Use z for Proportions
text:
24. Why We Use z for Proportions
For proportions, we do not use the t-distribution.
This is important.
For means, we use the t-distribution because we estimate the population standard deviation () with the sample standard deviation (s).
For proportions, the relevant distribution is fundamentally connected to the binomial distribution. The binomial distribution gives the exact probabilities of different numbers of successes in a fixed number of trials.
When sample size is large enough, the binomial distribution can be approximated by the normal distribution. Then we use the z-distribution.
So for proportions:

Small samples: use the binomial distribution.
Large samples: use the normal approximation with z.
Do not use the t-distribution for proportions.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 25. Conditions for the Normal Approximation for Proportions
text:
25. Conditions for the Normal Approximation for Proportions
To use the normal approximation for a confidence interval for a proportion, the sample size must be large enough.
A useful rule of thumb is that each category should have at least about 15 cases.
That is:
[ n ]
and:
[ n(1-) ]
In words:

At least 15 cases should be in the category of interest.
At least 15 cases should be outside the category of interest.

This condition helps ensure that the sampling distribution of the sample proportion is approximately normal.
If the sample size is too small, or if the proportion is too close to 0 or 1, the normal approximation may be poor. In that case, exact binomial methods or other specialized methods are preferable.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 26. Confidence Interval Formula for a Proportion
text:
26. Confidence Interval Formula for a Proportion
The confidence interval for a proportion is:
[ z^* ]
where:

() is the sample proportion
(z^*) is the critical value from the standard normal distribution
(n) is the sample size

The margin of error is:
[ z^* ]
For a 95 percent confidence interval:
[ z^* = 1.96 ]
For a 99 percent confidence interval:
[ z^* ]

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
27. Example: Dog Ownership
Suppose a survey asks Americans whether they own a dog.
The sample size is:
[ n = 2053 ]
The sample proportion who say they own a dog is:
[ = 0.44 ]
We want a 99 percent confidence interval for the population proportion.
For a 99 percent confidence interval:
[ z^* = 2.58 ]
The formula is:
[ z^* ]
Substitute values:
[ 0.44 ]
Calculate inside the square root:
[ 0.44(0.56) = 0.2464 ]
[ ]
Take the square root:
[ ]
Now multiply by the critical value:
[ 2.58(0.01095) ]
The margin of error is approximately:
[ 0.03 ]
So the confidence interval is approximately:
[ 0.44 ]
Lower bound:
[ 0.44 - 0.03 = 0.41 ]
Upper bound:
[ 0.44 + 0.03 = 0.47 ]
The 99 percent confidence interval is approximately:
[ 0.41 0.47 ]
A correct interpretation is:

We used a procedure that, across repeated random samples, would produce confidence intervals containing the true population proportion 99 percent of the time. In this sample, the resulting interval ranges from approximately 0.41 to 0.47.

Or, in more ordinary language:

The estimated proportion of Americans who own a dog is 0.44, with a 99 percent confidence interval from about 0.41 to 0.47.

That wording is acceptable as long as we do not say there is a 99 percent probability that the true proportion lies in this specific interval.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 28. Symmetric and Asymmetric Intervals for Proportions
text:
28. Symmetric and Asymmetric Intervals for Proportions
The textbook confidence interval for a proportion uses the normal approximation:
[ z^* ]
This produces a symmetric interval around ().
That is usually fine when:

the sample size is large
the sample proportion is not too close to 0 or 1

But proportions are bounded:
[ 0 ]
If () is close to 0 or 1, a symmetric normal interval can behave poorly. It may extend below 0 or above 1, which is impossible for a proportion.
For example, if ( = 0.96), a symmetric confidence interval might try to extend above 1. That does not make sense.
Software often uses better methods in these cases, such as:

exact binomial intervals
Wilson intervals
other adjusted methods

These intervals may not be symmetric, but they better respect the actual probability structure of proportions.
For hand calculations in this course, the normal approximation is useful and important. But in applied work, software may use more accurate methods.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:
29. Means Versus Proportions
Confidence intervals for means and proportions follow the same general logic:
[ ]
But the details differ.
For a mean:
[ {Y} t^*() ]
For a proportion:
[ z^* ]
The differences are important.
For means, we usually use the t-distribution because we estimate the population standard deviation with (s).
For proportions, we use the z-distribution when the sample size is large enough, or exact binomial methods when it is not.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
text:

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 31. What Makes a Confidence Interval Wider?
text:
31. What Makes a Confidence Interval Wider?
A confidence interval becomes wider when:

The confidence level increases.
The standard error increases.
The sample size decreases.
The sample or population is more variable.

For means, the standard error is:
[ ]
So a larger (s) means a wider interval.
A larger (n) means a narrower interval.
For proportions, the standard error is:
[ ]
Again, a larger (n) produces a narrower interval.
The confidence level also matters. A 99 percent confidence interval is wider than a 95 percent confidence interval because it uses a larger critical value.

title: Chapter 7: Statistical Inference
section: 32. What Makes a Confidence Interval Narrower?
text:
32. What Makes a Confidence Interval Narrower?
A confidence interval becomes narrower when:

The confidence level decreases.
The standard error decreases.
The sample size increases.
The data are less variable.

Larger samples give more precise estimates because they reduce standard error.
This is one of the most important practical lessons in statistics:

Precision improves with sample size, but at a diminishing rate because standard error decreases with (), not (n).

For example, quadrupling the sample size cuts the standard error in half.
That is useful, but it also means precision becomes increasingly expensive to improve.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
1. Populations and Samples
A population is the full set of cases we want to learn about. The population might consist of people, organizations, countries, schools, legislative districts, elections, policy programs, or almost anything else.
Examples of populations include:

all registered voters in the United States

all adults living in Michigan

all public schools in a state

all countries in the world

all members of a particular Congress

all students eligible for a new educational program

all households in a city

The population is the group about which we want to draw conclusions.
A sample is a subset of the population. Because we usually cannot collect data from every member of the population, we collect data from a sample and use it to estimate what is true of the population.
For example, suppose we want to know the average age of students in a large lecture course. The population is all students in the course. If we randomly select ten students and calculate their average age, those ten students are our sample. Their average age is our estimate of the average age in the full class.
The same logic applies at much larger scales. A national election poll may survey 1,000 adults in order to estimate the preferences of millions of voters.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
2. Parameters and Statistics
When we describe a population, we refer to parameters.
A parameter is a true characteristic of a population. For example:

the average income of all households in the United States

the percentage of voters who support a candidate

the median age of all residents in Michigan

the standard deviation of test scores among all students in a school district

Parameters are usually unknown. They are the values we would observe if we could measure the entire population.
When we describe a sample, we refer to statistics.
A statistic is a characteristic calculated from a sample. For example:

the average income among surveyed households

the percentage of respondents who support a candidate

the median age of people in a survey

the standard deviation of test scores in a sampled group of students

The statistic is known, because we can calculate it directly from the sample. The parameter is usually unknown, because we do not observe the full population.
The basic logic of statistical inference is this:

We use sample statistics to estimate population parameters.

This distinction is crucial. If we survey 1,000 voters and find that 52 percent support Candidate A, we know with certainty that 52 percent of the sample supports Candidate A. What we do not know with certainty is whether 52 percent of the entire population supports Candidate A.
Much of statistics is about quantifying that uncertainty.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
3. Why Sampling Creates Uncertainty
A sample statistic is usually not exactly equal to the population parameter. If we drew a different sample, we would probably get a slightly different statistic.
Suppose we want to estimate the average age of students in a class and randomly select ten students. We calculate the mean age of those ten students. If we randomly select another ten students, the mean will likely differ slightly. A third sample may differ again.
This does not mean anything has gone wrong. It is simply the result of sampling.
The sample statistic is known. The uncertainty comes from using that statistic to estimate the population parameter.
For example:

We know the sample mean.

We do not know the population mean.

We use the sample mean to estimate the population mean.

Because the sample is only part of the population, the estimate is uncertain.

A major goal of statistics is to tell us how much uncertainty remains. Later statistical tools, such as confidence intervals and hypothesis tests, help us answer questions like:

How close is our sample statistic likely to be to the population parameter?

How much random error should we expect?

How confident can we be in our estimate?

Is an observed difference likely to reflect a real difference in the population, or could it plausibly be due to random sampling error?

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
4. Populations Are Sometimes Hypothetical
It is tempting to think of populations as always concrete and observable. Sometimes they are. If we define our population as “all students enrolled in this course,” that population exists in a straightforward way.
But in social science and policy research, populations are often partly hypothetical.
Consider a pilot policy program. Suppose a city wants to test a new job-training program. It recruits a group of participants and measures outcomes after completion. Those participants are the sample. But what is the population?
The population is not simply “everyone currently in the pilot,” because those are the people in the sample. Instead, the population might be all people who would participate if the program were implemented more broadly. That population does not yet exist. It is hypothetical.
The sample helps us estimate what might happen in that broader, future population.
Something similar can happen even when we seem to have complete data. Suppose we study voting behavior in the 117th Congress. We might have data on every roll-call vote cast by every member. In one sense, that is population data for the 117th Congress. But if our goal is to understand how Congress works as an institution, the 117th Congress is only one instance of a broader process.
We may want to understand how party, district characteristics, ideology, electoral competition, or constituent preferences shape legislative behavior more generally. The particular Congress we observe is only one possible version of Congress. Different electoral outcomes, redistricting decisions, retirements, scandals, national events, and close races could have produced a different set of members and a different pattern of votes.
In that sense, our observed Congress is a sample from a broader set of possible Congresses.
This idea can feel strange at first. But it captures an important feature of social research: we often use the specific world we observe to learn about broader processes that could operate across many possible worlds.
The data in front of us are concrete. The population we care about may be broader, more abstract, or even hypothetical.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
5. Why Sampling Method Matters
The quality of a sample depends heavily on how it is collected.
Ideally, a sample should be representative of the population. A representative sample resembles the population in the ways that matter for the research question.
If we want to estimate national public opinion, our sample should reflect the national population. If we want to understand school performance across a state, our sample should reflect the schools in that state. If we want to evaluate a policy program, our sample should resemble the people who would actually experience that program.
The gold standard is often the random sample, because random selection gives each member of the population a known chance of selection. Random sampling allows us to use probability theory to estimate sampling error.
But true random sampling is often difficult, expensive, or impossible. As a result, researchers use many different sampling strategies. Some are probability-based and involve random selection. Others are non-probability approaches that do not rely on random selection.
Both types are useful in different contexts, but they support different kinds of conclusions.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
6. Non-Probability Sampling
In non-probability sampling, cases are not selected through a random process. This means we generally cannot use standard probability theory to calculate sampling error in the usual way.
Non-probability samples are common, especially in exploratory research, qualitative research, pilot studies, hard-to-reach populations, and situations where probability sampling is too expensive or impractical.
Major types of non-probability sampling include:

Convenience sampling

Purposive sampling

Snowball sampling

Quota sampling

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
7. Convenience Sampling
A convenience sample consists of cases that are easy to access.
For example, a psychology professor may recruit undergraduate students to participate in an experiment. Students see a posting, sign up, complete the study, and perhaps receive course credit or payment. This is convenient for the researcher because students are nearby and available.
Convenience sampling is inexpensive and practical, but it has obvious limitations. Undergraduate students at one university may not represent adults in general, young people in general, or even college students in general. They may differ in age, education, socioeconomic background, political attitudes, psychological traits, or willingness to participate in research.
Convenience samples can be useful for some purposes, especially early-stage research or controlled experiments. But researchers must be cautious about generalizing from them to broader populations.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
8. Purposive Sampling
In purposive sampling, researchers deliberately select cases because they are especially relevant to the research question.
For example, suppose a researcher wants to understand the experiences of community organizers working on housing policy. Rather than randomly sampling the general population, the researcher may intentionally seek out people who have direct experience with housing advocacy.
Purposive sampling is common in qualitative research, case studies, elite interviews, and policy analysis. The goal is not always statistical representativeness. Instead, the goal may be to gather information-rich cases.
For example, if we are studying how local governments respond to water contamination crises, we may intentionally select cities that experienced such crises. A random sample of all cities might include very few relevant cases.
Purposive sampling can provide deep insight, but it does not support the same kind of statistical generalization as probability sampling.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
9. Snowball Sampling
In snowball sampling, researchers begin with one or more participants and then ask them to identify others who should be included.
For example, after interviewing one activist, the researcher might ask:

Who else should I talk to?

That person recommends others, who recommend still others, and the sample expands outward like a snowball.
Snowball sampling is especially useful when studying networks, hidden populations, marginalized groups, or communities where trust matters. It can help researchers reach people who would be difficult to identify through standard sampling methods.
However, snowball samples can be biased toward connected individuals and networks. People are likely to recommend others they know, who may share similar views, experiences, or social positions.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
10. Quota Sampling
In quota sampling, researchers decide in advance how many cases they want from particular groups, then recruit participants until those quotas are filled.
For example, a researcher may want a sample that is:

50 percent women and 50 percent men

30 percent from one age group, 40 percent from another, and 30 percent from another

20 percent from each of five geographic regions

Quota sampling can help ensure that important groups appear in the sample. However, the individuals within each quota are not necessarily randomly selected.
For example, a survey firm might decide it needs 100 respondents aged 18–29, then recruit whoever is easiest to reach until it fills that quota. That sample may still be biased if the people easiest to reach differ from those who are harder to reach.
Quota sampling can improve sample composition, but it does not eliminate the need to worry about representativeness.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
11. Probability Sampling
In probability sampling, random selection plays some role in determining which cases are included. Each case in the population has a known, nonzero probability of selection.
Probability sampling is important because it allows researchers to use probability theory to estimate sampling error. In other words, it gives us a mathematical basis for saying how uncertain our estimates are.
Common probability sampling methods include:

Simple random sampling

Systematic random sampling

Stratified random sampling

Cluster sampling

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
12. Simple Random Sampling
A simple random sample is the most basic form of probability sample. Every member of the population has an equal probability of being selected.
For example, suppose we have a list of all 10,000 students at a university and randomly select 500 names. If every student has the same chance of being selected, this is a simple random sample.
Simple random sampling is the foundation of much statistical theory. Many formulas introduced in basic statistics assume that the data come from a simple random sample.
In practice, however, true simple random samples are often difficult to obtain. We may not have a complete list of the population. Contacting randomly selected people may be expensive. Some people may refuse to participate. Others may be impossible to reach.
This gap between theory and reality is important. Introductory statistics often begins with the assumption of simple random sampling, but real-world data frequently violate that assumption.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
13. Systematic Random Sampling
A systematic random sample selects cases using a fixed interval from a list.
For example, suppose we have a list of union members and want to sample every fifth person. We might randomly choose a starting point among the first five names, then select every fifth name after that.
Systematic sampling can be practical when working with long lists, especially if full randomization is inconvenient.
However, systematic sampling can produce bias if the list has a hidden pattern. For example, if every fifth name on a list corresponds to a particular type of member because of how the list is organized, the sample may not be representative.
Systematic sampling works best when the ordering of the list is unrelated to the variables we care about.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
14. Stratified Random Sampling
A stratified random sample divides the population into groups, called strata, and then randomly samples within each stratum.
Strata are usually defined by characteristics that matter for the research question or for representativeness. Examples include:

geographic region

race or ethnicity

gender

age group

income category

school type

urban, suburban, and rural areas

For example, suppose we want a national survey that is representative by region. We could divide the country into regions and then randomly sample people within each region.
Stratified sampling has several advantages.
First, it can improve representativeness. By ensuring that each stratum is included, we reduce the risk that random chance will produce too many cases from one group and too few from another.
Second, it can reduce sampling variability. If important groups are properly represented, sample statistics may fluctuate less across repeated samples.
Third, it helps with subgroup analysis. If we want to compare groups, we need enough cases in each group. A simple random sample may include too few members of a small group to support meaningful analysis. Stratified sampling can intentionally collect enough cases from that group.
For example, if a group makes up only 5 percent of the population, a simple random sample of 1,000 people would include about 50 members of that group on average. That may not be enough for detailed analysis. A stratified design could oversample that group to ensure a larger number of cases.
The disadvantages are also important. To use stratified sampling, we need to know which cases belong to which strata. The strata should be mutually exclusive, meaning each case belongs to only one stratum. If our classification is inaccurate or incomplete, the sampling design may be flawed.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
15. Cluster Sampling
Cluster sampling divides the population into clusters, randomly selects clusters, and then samples cases within those clusters.
Clusters are often geographic. Examples include:

counties

census tracts

neighborhoods

villages

schools

classrooms

city blocks

Cluster sampling is common in large-scale surveys, especially when in-person data collection is required.
Suppose we want to conduct a nationwide household survey. A simple random sample might require interviewers to travel to thousands of scattered addresses across the country. That would be extremely expensive. Instead, researchers can randomly select geographic clusters, send interviewers to those locations, and then randomly select households within each cluster.
This preserves randomness while reducing cost.
Cluster sampling is often a multi-stage process. For example:

Randomly select cities.

Within selected cities, randomly select neighborhoods.

Within selected neighborhoods, randomly select blocks.

Within selected blocks, randomly select households.

Within selected households, randomly select individuals.

This design is far more practical than sending interviewers to randomly scattered individuals across an entire country.
Cluster sampling differs from stratified sampling. In stratified sampling, strata are usually internally similar and analytically meaningful. In cluster sampling, clusters are often intended to be miniature versions of the broader population, or at least heterogeneous enough to contain variation.
In practice, large surveys often combine stratification and clustering.
For example, the Demographic and Health Surveys, often conducted in countries around the world, use cluster-based designs. Survey teams may select clusters such as villages or urban neighborhoods, then interview households within those areas. This allows researchers to produce nationally representative estimates while keeping fieldwork manageable.
The major advantage of cluster sampling is cost. The major disadvantage is complexity. Standard statistical formulas often assume simple random sampling. Clustered designs violate that assumption because people within the same cluster may be more similar to one another than people in different clusters. As a result, specialized survey methods are often needed.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
16. Theory Versus Reality in Sampling
Much of introductory statistics assumes simple random sampling. This assumption makes the theory easier to learn and provides a foundation for understanding inference.
But real surveys are rarely simple random samples.
High-quality surveys often use complex designs involving stratification, clustering, oversampling, and weighting. These designs are not flaws. They are often intentional choices made to improve representativeness, reduce cost, or ensure adequate sample sizes for important subgroups.
For example, the American National Election Studies is a rigorous survey of political attitudes and behavior in the United States. But it is not a simple random sample. It may intentionally oversample some groups so researchers can analyze those groups with sufficient precision.
If we analyze such a survey naively, without applying the appropriate weights, our results may not match known population values. For example, a survey may appear to overrepresent supporters of one candidate or demographic group. That does not necessarily mean the survey is bad. It may mean we have not yet applied the weights that correct for the sampling design.
The central point is this:

Real-world samples often require real-world corrections.

A good researcher asks not only what is in the data, but how the data were collected.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
17. Random Sampling Error
Even a perfectly random sample will usually differ from the population.
This difference is called random sampling error.
Random sampling error occurs because we observe only part of the population. By chance, the sample may include slightly more of one kind of case and slightly fewer of another. As a result, the sample statistic will usually differ from the population parameter.
For example, suppose the true average age in a class is 20.4 years. If we randomly sample ten students, the average age in the sample might be 20.1, 20.7, or 19.9. These differences are not caused by bias or bad methods. They are simply the result of random selection.
The good news is that random sampling error is the kind of error statistics is designed to handle. Probability theory allows us to estimate how large random sampling error is likely to be.
Later, when we study confidence intervals, we will be able to make statements such as:

We are 95 percent confident that the population value lies within this range.

That does not mean our sample statistic is uncertain. The sample statistic is known. What is uncertain is how close it is to the population parameter.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
18. Bias
Random sampling error is expected and manageable. Bias is more dangerous.
Bias occurs when the sample is systematically different from the population in a way that affects the results.
With random sampling error, mistakes are due to chance. With bias, mistakes have a direction. The sample tends to overrepresent or underrepresent certain cases, views, behaviors, or characteristics.
This is a serious problem because standard statistical methods assume that errors are random. If the sampling process is biased, larger sample sizes do not necessarily solve the problem. A biased sample can produce a very precise estimate of the wrong value.
Two important forms of bias are:

Selection bias

Non-response bias

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
19. Selection Bias
Selection bias occurs when the sampling method systematically includes some kinds of cases and excludes others.
For example, suppose a researcher conducts a telephone survey by calling landline phones during weekday afternoons. This sampling method is likely to reach people who are at home during the day and who still use landlines. It may overrepresent retirees, people who work from home, or people without full-time employment outside the home. It may underrepresent younger adults, people who rely only on cell phones, and people who work during the day.
The problem is not simply that some people are missing. The problem is that the missing people differ systematically from those included.
Selection bias was once less severe for landline telephone surveys than it is today. By the 1970s, most American households had landline telephones. A landline survey could reach a large share of the population, though not all of it.
Today, many adults are cell-phone-only, and many people who still have landlines rarely answer them. A landline-only survey now excludes a large and systematically different portion of the population.
This matters because people who are reachable by landline may differ politically, socially, and demographically from people who are not. If those differences are related to the survey topic, the results will be biased.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
20. Example: Landlines and Election Polling
Election polling provides a clear example of selection bias.
In the 2012 U.S. presidential election, some polls used landline-only samples, while others included cell phones. Analysts noticed that landline-only polls tended to produce different estimates than polls that included cell-phone respondents.
This mattered because cell-phone-only voters differed from landline respondents. They tended to be younger and demographically distinct in ways that were politically relevant.
A landline-only poll might show one candidate leading by a small margin, while a poll including cell phones might show a different result. The issue was not random sampling error alone. It was that the landline-only sampling frame systematically missed part of the electorate.
This illustrates a broader lesson:

A sample can be large and still be biased if the sampling method systematically excludes relevant groups.

The size of the sample does not fix a flawed sampling frame.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
21. Non-Response Bias
Non-response bias occurs when people selected for a sample do not participate, and those who do participate differ systematically from those who do not.
Non-response is extremely common. Many people refuse to answer surveys. Others cannot be reached. Some begin a survey but skip particular questions.
Non-response alone is not necessarily bias. If the people who respond and the people who do not respond are similar with respect to the question we care about, then non-response may not distort the results.
The problem arises when willingness to respond is related to the outcome being measured.
For example, suppose we conduct an election survey. People who are highly interested in politics may be more willing to participate than people who are disengaged. If politically engaged people have different candidate preferences than disengaged people, the survey will be biased.
Similarly, if a survey asks about income, people with very high or very low incomes may be less willing to answer. If missing income responses are not random, estimates of average income may be biased.
The key question is:

Are respondents systematically different from non-respondents in ways that matter for the research question?

If yes, non-response bias is a problem.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
22. Low Response Rates and Polling
Modern survey research faces a major challenge: response rates are low.
In many phone surveys, only a small percentage of calls result in a completed interview. If only 6 percent of people contacted complete a survey, the researcher must make many calls to obtain a sufficient sample size.
Low response rates do not automatically mean a survey is biased. But they increase the risk of bias because the final sample depends heavily on who is willing to participate.
Even small differences in willingness to respond can produce large changes in survey results.
Imagine that the true electorate is evenly divided:

Candidate A: 47 percent

Candidate B: 47 percent

Undecided: 6 percent

Now consider two scenarios.
Scenario 1: Equal Response Rates
Supporters of both candidates are equally likely to respond to the survey. Suppose 10 percent of each group responds.
In this case, the expected survey result remains 47 percent for Candidate A and 47 percent for Candidate B. Random sampling error may cause some variation, but there is no systematic bias.
Scenario 2: Slightly Unequal Response Rates
Now suppose Candidate B’s supporters are just slightly more likely to respond. Candidate A’s supporters respond at 10 percent, while Candidate B’s supporters respond at 11 percent.
That small difference can produce a noticeable shift in the poll. Candidate B may appear to lead, even though the true electorate remains tied.
This can happen after major campaign events. Suppose a debate energizes one candidate’s supporters and discourages the other’s. The underlying electorate may not have changed much, but the willingness to answer a political survey may have shifted. Polls may then show movement that partly reflects response patterns rather than true persuasion.
This is why survey researchers worry so much about non-response. When response rates are low, small differences in who answers can matter a great deal.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
23. When Is Non-Response a Problem?
Non-response becomes problematic when it is systematic with respect to the variable we are trying to measure.
If we ask about voting behavior, non-response is a problem if voters and non-voters respond at different rates.
If we ask about candidate preference, non-response is a problem if supporters of one candidate respond at higher rates than supporters of another.
If we ask about income, non-response is a problem if people at different income levels are more or less likely to answer.
If non-response is random with respect to the research question, it mainly reduces sample size. If non-response is systematic, it biases the results.
This distinction is crucial.
A survey with a low response rate can still be useful if non-response is unrelated to the variables of interest. A survey with a high response rate can still be biased if the remaining non-response is systematic in an important way.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
24. Why Weighting Is Necessary
Many surveys intentionally or unintentionally overrepresent some groups and underrepresent others.
For example, suppose a survey sample is 20 percent age 65 or older. But census data tell us that only 16 percent of the relevant population is age 65 or older.
Older adults are overrepresented in the sample. If older adults answer certain questions differently from younger adults, then the sample statistic will be distorted.
The purpose of survey weighting is to correct known imbalances between the sample and the population.
Survey weights adjust how much each respondent contributes to the final estimate. Cases from overrepresented groups are down-weighted. Cases from underrepresented groups are up-weighted.
The goal is to make the weighted sample resemble the population.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
25. A Simple Weighting Example
Suppose we conduct a survey and find:

20 percent of the sample is age 65 or older.

80 percent of the sample is under age 65.

But from census data, we know:

16 percent of the population is age 65 or older.

84 percent of the population is under age 65.

Older adults are overrepresented. Younger adults are underrepresented.
To correct this, we assign weights.
For older adults:
[ = ]
[ = = 0.80 ]
Each older respondent receives a weight of 0.80. That means each older respondent counts slightly less than one full respondent in weighted calculations.
For younger adults:
[ = = 1.05 ]
Each younger respondent receives a weight of 1.05. That means each younger respondent counts slightly more than one full respondent.
After weighting, the age composition of the sample better reflects the age composition of the population.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
26. What Weighting Does
Weighting changes how statistics are calculated.
In an unweighted analysis, every case counts equally. If a survey has 1,000 respondents, each respondent contributes 1/1,000 of the total.
In a weighted analysis, some cases count more and others count less.
For example:

A respondent with a weight of 0.80 counts as 0.80 of a case.

A respondent with a weight of 1.05 counts as 1.05 cases.

A respondent with a weight of 2.00 counts as two cases.

This may sound strange, but the logic is straightforward. If a group is underrepresented in the sample, each member of that group in the sample must stand in for more people in the population. If a group is overrepresented, each member must stand in for fewer people.
Weighting is especially important in surveys with complex sampling designs, including stratification, clustering, and oversampling.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
27. Oversampling and Weighting
Researchers sometimes intentionally oversample small groups.
Suppose a group makes up only 5 percent of the population. In a simple random sample of 1,000 people, we would expect only about 50 respondents from that group. That may be too few for meaningful subgroup analysis.
A researcher may intentionally sample 200 people from that group. This allows for more precise analysis within the group.
But if we want to estimate population-level statistics, we cannot allow that group to count as 20 percent of the sample when it is only 5 percent of the population. We must apply weights to bring the group back into its proper population proportion.
This is common in high-quality surveys. Oversampling improves our ability to study smaller groups, while weighting restores population representativeness for overall estimates.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
28. Weighting and Election Surveys
Election surveys provide a useful example.
Suppose a survey appears to show that 53 percent of respondents voted for one candidate, even though the actual election result was lower. This does not necessarily mean the survey is poor. It may mean the raw, unweighted sample overrepresents groups that favored that candidate.
Once survey weights are applied, the estimate may move closer to the known population value.
This is why analysts must be careful when using survey data. If a survey includes weights, those weights are usually there for a reason. Ignoring them may produce misleading estimates.
In introductory exercises, researchers and students sometimes work with unweighted data for simplicity. But in applied survey analysis, weights are often essential.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
29. Weighting Is Not Magic
Survey weighting is powerful, but it does not solve every problem.
Weighting can correct for known differences between the sample and the population. For example, if we know the population distribution of age, gender, race, education, region, or party registration, we can weight the sample to match those distributions.
But weighting cannot easily correct for unknown differences.
Suppose people who are highly politically engaged are more likely to answer a survey. If we do not know exactly how political engagement is distributed in the population, or how it relates to response probability, weighting may not fully solve the problem.
Similarly, if people who distrust institutions are less likely to answer surveys, and that distrust is related to the outcome we care about, standard weights may not fully correct the bias.
Weighting also increases complexity. Weighted data require appropriate statistical methods, especially when combined with clustering and stratification. Standard formulas may underestimate uncertainty if they ignore the survey design.
The practical lesson is:

Weighting can improve representativeness, but it depends on knowing how the sample differs from the population.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
30. Sampling Error Versus Bias
It is useful to distinguish clearly between random sampling error and bias.








Problem
What It Means
Can Standard Statistics Handle It?




Random sampling error
The sample differs from the population by chance
Yes


Selection bias
The sampling method systematically excludes or includes certain cases
Not automatically


Non-response bias
Respondents differ systematically from non-respondents
Not automatically


Measurement bias
The variable itself is measured in a systematically inaccurate way
Not automatically



Random sampling error is expected. It is built into statistical inference. Bias is more dangerous because it can systematically push estimates away from the truth.
A larger sample reduces random sampling error. But a larger sample does not necessarily reduce bias. If the sampling method is biased, collecting more data through the same biased method may simply produce a more precise wrong answer.
For example, a landline-only poll with 100,000 respondents may still be biased if landline users differ from the population in politically relevant ways.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
section: 31. The Central Lesson: Samples Are Designed
text:
31. The Central Lesson: Samples Are Designed
Samples do not simply appear. They are designed, collected, filtered, and sometimes adjusted.
When evaluating a sample, we should ask:

What is the population of interest?

What is the sampling frame?

Who had a chance of being selected?

Who was excluded from the start?

Was selection random?

Were some groups intentionally oversampled?
