Who refused to participate?

Are respondents different from non-respondents?

Are weights needed?

Were weights applied?

Does the final sample support the conclusions being drawn?

Sampling is not just a technical detail. It determines what kind of claims we can make.
A study based on a convenience sample may produce useful insights, but we should be careful about generalizing to a broad population. A probability sample supports stronger inference, but only if the sampling design is understood and properly analyzed. A complex survey may be excellent, but only if we use the weights and design information correctly.
The sample is the bridge between the data we have and the population we care about. If that bridge is weak, our conclusions may not hold.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
Key Terms
Population
The full set of cases the researcher wants to study or draw conclusions about.
Sample
A subset of the population used to estimate characteristics of the population.
Parameter
A true characteristic of a population, such as a population mean or proportion.
Statistic
A characteristic calculated from a sample, such as a sample mean or sample proportion.
Representative sample
A sample that resembles the population in the ways that matter for the research question.
Non-probability sampling
Sampling methods that do not use random selection.
Convenience sample
A sample made up of cases that are easy to access.
Purposive sample
A sample in which cases are deliberately selected because they are especially relevant.
Snowball sample
A sample built by asking participants to recommend other participants.
Quota sample
A sample designed to include predetermined numbers of cases from specific groups.
Probability sampling
Sampling methods in which cases have a known, nonzero probability of selection.
Simple random sample
A sample in which every member of the population has an equal probability of selection.
Systematic random sample
A sample selected by choosing every kth case from a list, usually after a random starting point.
Stratified random sample
A sample created by dividing the population into strata and randomly sampling within each stratum.
Cluster sample
A sample created by dividing the population into clusters, randomly selecting clusters, and then sampling cases within selected clusters.
Random sampling error
The difference between a sample statistic and a population parameter caused by random chance.
Selection bias
Bias that occurs when the sampling method systematically includes or excludes certain types of cases.
Non-response bias
Bias that occurs when those who respond differ systematically from those who do not.
Survey weighting
A statistical adjustment that changes how much each case contributes to estimates so the sample better reflects the population.
Oversampling
Intentionally sampling some groups at higher rates than their population share, often to allow subgroup analysis.

title: Chapter 2: Sampling
text:
Chapter Summary
Sampling allows researchers to use data from a subset of cases to learn about a larger population. The population is the full group of interest, while the sample is the group actually observed. Population characteristics are parameters; sample characteristics are statistics.
Sampling creates uncertainty because sample statistics usually differ from population parameters. Random sampling error is expected and can be quantified using probability theory. Bias is more serious because it occurs when a sample systematically differs from the population in ways related to the research question.
Researchers use many sampling methods. Non-probability approaches, such as convenience, purposive, snowball, and quota sampling, can be useful but generally do not support the same kind of statistical inference as probability samples. Probability methods, such as simple random, systematic, stratified, and cluster sampling, use random selection and provide a stronger basis for inference.
Real-world surveys often use complex sampling designs. They may stratify, cluster, or oversample certain groups. These designs can improve data quality or reduce costs, but they also require careful analysis. Survey weights are often needed to correct for differences between the sample and the population.
The most important lesson is that sampling shapes inference. Before trusting a statistical result, we need to understand how the sample was collected, who was included, who was excluded, who responded, and whether the final data can support the conclusions being drawn.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
1. Concepts
A concept is an idea, label, or category that we use to describe something in the world. Concepts are the building blocks of research. They allow us to identify, describe, compare, and eventually explain social and political phenomena.
In everyday life, we use concepts constantly. We talk about things like:

ideology

intelligence

poverty

education

tolerance

democracy

corruption

religiosity

state capacity

These words refer to things we generally believe are real in some meaningful sense, even if they are not directly visible or easy to measure. For example, we may believe that “intelligence” exists, but we cannot simply point to it in the world in the same way we can point to a chair. We may believe that “poverty” exists, but deciding exactly who counts as poor requires choices. We may speak of someone as “educated,” but that could mean many different things: years of schooling, degrees earned, skills acquired, knowledge gained, or some combination of these.
In this sense, scientific inquiry begins much like language learning. A child learning language points to objects and asks, in effect, “What is that?” Learning about the social world begins similarly: we label and describe. But scientific understanding requires more than naming things. It requires us to study relationships between the things we name.
For example, suppose we have a theory that education is related to tolerance. Perhaps we believe that people with more education tend to be more tolerant of people who are different from them. That is a theoretical claim. To test it, however, we need to measure both education and tolerance. And that is where the difficulty begins.
Measuring education may sound easy at first. We could count years of schooling. But does a person who spent six years completing college necessarily have more education than someone who completed it in four? Does formal schooling capture trade skills, informal learning, or knowledge acquired outside institutions? What about differences in educational quality across schools or countries?
Measuring tolerance may be even harder. Do we ask people whether they consider themselves tolerant? Do we observe their behavior? Do we ask about attitudes toward specific groups? Each option captures something slightly different.
The point is not that measurement is impossible. The point is that measurement always requires choices, and those choices have consequences.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
2. What Makes a Concept Useful?
Researchers often create new concepts. This is one reason academic fields sometimes develop so much jargon. Not all concepts are equally useful, however. A useful concept should have several qualities.
First, it should resonate with others. When we use the term, other people should have at least a reasonably shared understanding of what we mean.
Second, it should have an empirical domain. That is, it should refer to things that can, at least in principle, be observed, identified, or measured.
Third, it should be fruitful. A good concept helps us ask interesting questions, develop explanations, and understand relationships in the world.
Fourth, it should be distinct from related concepts. If two concepts overlap too much, it becomes difficult to know what exactly we are studying.
Consider the concept of state capacity. In political science, state capacity refers to the ability of a state, or government, to do what it sets out to do. A high-capacity state can implement policies, collect taxes, enforce laws, provide services, and maintain order.
This concept is related to, but distinct from, governance. Governance often has a normative component. A well-governed country is usually understood as one in which the state acts effectively and in ways that serve citizens’ interests. State capacity, by contrast, is more neutral. A state may have high capacity and use that capacity for harmful or repressive purposes. It may be effective without being just.
That distinction matters. If we want to study state capacity, we need to measure the government’s ability to act, not whether we approve of what the government does.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
3. From Concepts to Variables: Operationalization
Once we identify a concept, we need to turn it into something we can record as data. This process is called operationalization.
Operationalization is the process of taking an abstract concept and developing a concrete measurement strategy that produces a variable.
The process usually involves several steps:

Identify the concept.
What do we want to study?
Develop a conceptual definition.
What exactly do we mean by the concept?
Create a measurement strategy.
What procedure will we use to measure it?
Record the result as a variable.
What values will appear in our data set?

A variable is the recorded result of measurement. It must have at least two possible values. If every case has the same value, then it is not really a variable; it is a constant.
For example, if we conduct a survey of people and ask, “Are you human?” we will presumably get the same answer from everyone. That would not be a useful variable. If we ask, “What is your age?” or “What is your highest level of education?” we expect answers to vary across people.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
4. Example: Measuring Political Ideology
To see operationalization in practice, consider the concept of political ideology.
At the conceptual level, ideology refers to a structured set of political beliefs. But that definition is still abstract. To measure ideology, we need to decide what kind of structure we think ideology has.
One common strategy is to imagine ideology as a single dimension running from liberal to conservative, or left to right. This imposes a particular structure on the concept. It assumes that people can be located somewhere along one ideological spectrum.
That may be useful, but it is not the only way to think about ideology. Political beliefs may have multiple dimensions. For example, someone may be economically conservative but socially liberal, or economically progressive but socially conservative. A single left-right scale may not capture that complexity.
Still, the one-dimensional liberal-conservative scale has been widely used, especially in survey research. One of the most important examples comes from the American National Election Studies. The ANES is a long-running study of political opinion in the United States, conducted during presidential election years. Much of the foundational work behind this study was developed at the University of Michigan’s Institute for Social Research.
The ANES asks respondents a question similar to the following:

We hear a lot of talk these days about liberals and conservatives. Here is a seven-point scale on which the political views that people may hold are arranged from extremely liberal to extremely conservative. Where would you place yourself on this scale?

The scale is usually arranged as follows:

Extremely liberal

Liberal

Slightly liberal

Moderate or middle of the road

Slightly conservative

Conservative

Extremely conservative

This measurement strategy produces a variable with seven possible values.
Notice how many choices are embedded in this measure. The researchers have decided that ideology can be represented as a single dimension. They have decided that “liberal” and “conservative” are meaningful endpoints. They have decided that respondents can place themselves on the scale. They have also attached numbers to the categories.
Those numbers are useful, but we need to be careful. This is an ordinal scale, meaning the categories have an order. A score of 6 is more conservative than a score of 5, and a score of 5 is more conservative than a score of 4. But we cannot be certain that the distance between 4 and 5 is the same as the distance between 6 and 7.
That matters because, strictly speaking, we should be cautious about calculating averages from ordinal scales. In practice, social scientists often do calculate means of ordinal variables like ideology. This is not necessarily disastrous, especially when the scale behaves approximately like a numeric scale. But it is important to understand the assumption being made.
There are formal rules, and then there is what researchers often do. The two do not always match perfectly.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
5. Example: Measuring Education
Education is another concept that initially seems easy to measure but becomes complicated quickly.
One common strategy is to measure education by years of schooling. This has several advantages. It is simple, easy to ask in a survey, and widely understood. If someone says they completed twelve years of schooling, we generally understand what that means. It also allows for straightforward comparisons across individuals.
But years of schooling may not capture what we really mean by education. Two people may spend the same number of years in school and learn very different things. Educational quality varies dramatically across schools and countries. Some people acquire substantial knowledge or skills outside formal schooling. Others attend school without gaining the skills we might associate with being educated.
Another strategy is to measure education by highest degree completed. This is also common. A survey might ask whether a person has completed high school, some college, a bachelor’s degree, a master’s degree, a professional degree, or a doctorate.
This measure captures formal credentials, which can be important for employment, income, and social status. But it also has limitations. A bachelor’s degree in one field may not be directly comparable to a bachelor’s degree in another. A professional degree, a master’s degree, and a PhD are not always easily ranked along a single dimension. The content, duration, and meaning of degrees differ across contexts.
A third strategy might focus on skills, such as literacy, numeracy, writing ability, or technical competency. This may come closer to what some people mean by education, but it is harder to measure. Asking people to report how much they know is unreliable. Testing people may be expensive, time-consuming, or culturally biased.
Each measurement strategy captures part of the concept and misses part of it. There is no perfect measure. The researcher’s task is to choose a strategy that is appropriate for the research question and to be honest about its limitations.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
6. Measurement Choices in Existing Data
Researchers often do not collect their own data. Instead, they use data collected by governments, organizations, schools, agencies, or previous researchers. In these cases, the measurement strategy has already been chosen.
For example, education researchers may use administrative data from a state department of education. Those data may include variables such as race and ethnicity, eligibility for free or reduced-price lunch, standardized test scores, school attendance, and graduation status. The researcher does not get to redesign how those variables were measured. Instead, the researcher must understand the choices that were already made.
Census data provide a powerful example. The U.S. Census has measured race and ethnicity in different ways over time. The categories have changed repeatedly, reflecting political, social, and scientific assumptions of different eras.
Historically, race was not always self-identified by respondents. Enumerators often assigned racial categories based on their own observation. Earlier censuses also used a much smaller number of categories, forcing many people into classifications that did not reflect their identities. People of mixed racial background often had to select one category or were assigned one.
By 2000, respondents could choose more than one racial category. The number of available categories has expanded, and write-in options have become more common. This allows for richer self-identification, but it also creates complications for analysis.
The 2020 Census, for example, separated questions about Hispanic, Latino, or Spanish origin from questions about race. Respondents could indicate whether they were of Hispanic, Latino, or Spanish origin and then separately select one or more racial categories. They could also provide more specific origins, such as Mexican, Puerto Rican, Cuban, or another identity.
This structure reflects an important distinction: Hispanic or Latino origin is treated as an ethnicity, not a race. But identities are complex. “Hispanic” often refers to Spanish-speaking heritage, while “Latino” often refers to Latin American geographic origin. These categories do not always map neatly onto how people understand themselves.
The race question includes categories such as White, Black or African American, American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian origin categories, Native Hawaiian and Pacific Islander categories, and other write-in options. Some options refer to racial categories, some to national origins, and some to broader geographic or ethnic groupings.
This flexibility can better reflect how people identify. But it also creates analytical challenges. If researchers download millions of records, they must decide how to group responses. Should someone who selects multiple categories be counted in each? Should they be coded as multiracial? How should write-in responses be treated? What happens when categories have different meanings across cultural contexts?
These are not merely technical questions. They are political and social questions as well. Census categories can shape identity. When a state repeatedly offers certain categories, families and communities may come to understand themselves through those categories. Measurement does not merely reflect the world; sometimes it helps construct the world.
Measurement choices also have material consequences. Census data influence political representation, federal funding, civil rights enforcement, and public policy. Groups may lobby to be included in official categories because recognition affects visibility and resources.
The lesson is simple but important: whenever we see a table, graph, or statistical result, we should ask how the variables were measured. We should not lose our critical instincts. Every data set reflects measurement decisions, and those decisions affect interpretation.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
7. Assessing Measurement Quality
A good measurement strategy should be both reliable and valid.
These are two of the most important concepts in research methods.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
section: 8. Change Does Not Necessarily Mean Unreliability
text:
8. Change Does Not Necessarily Mean Unreliability
One point often causes confusion: if the thing being measured changes, then the measurement should change.
Suppose we ask students how many times they engaged in binge drinking during the past month. If we ask at the beginning of the semester, the answer may differ from the answer during midterms. That does not necessarily mean the measure is unreliable. It may simply mean behavior changed.
But suppose our real goal is to measure a more stable trait, such as a person’s general drinking habits. Asking only about last week may not be reliable for that broader concept. Last week could have been unusual.
This distinction matters. A question can be reliable for a narrow concept but unreliable for a broader one.
For example:

How many times did you attend religious services last week?

This may reliably measure whether someone attended services last week. But it may not reliably measure religiosity as a broader personal characteristic.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
9. Response Bias
A common threat to measurement quality is response bias.
Response bias occurs when the way a question is asked, or the context in which it is asked, leads respondents to give answers that do not accurately reflect their true beliefs, behaviors, or experiences.
This often happens when respondents feel social pressure.
For example, surveys frequently ask:

Did you vote in the last election?

In the United States, the percentage of people who say yes is usually higher than actual turnout. Some respondents may misremember. Some may generally think of themselves as voters and answer accordingly. Others may know that voting is socially approved and feel pressure to say yes.
Response bias can also occur when questions ask about illegal or sensitive behavior. Suppose researchers want to study corruption and ask business owners:

Did you pay a bribe to receive a permit?

Respondents may be reluctant to admit participating in an illegal transaction. A less threatening question might ask:

How common is it for businesses in your industry to be asked for unofficial payments when applying for permits?

This does not eliminate the problem, but it may reduce the pressure on respondents.
Similarly, if researchers want to study racial prejudice, directly asking people whether race influenced their vote may not produce truthful answers. Respondents may not want to admit prejudice, or they may not be fully aware of how race shaped their behavior. Researchers may therefore use indirect methods, such as experiments that vary candidate race while holding policy positions constant.
Good measurement requires anticipating how people interpret questions and how social pressures shape responses.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
10. Example: Measuring Religiosity
Religiosity is a useful example because it is difficult to define and measure.
Suppose we want to know how religious a person is. We might ask:

Did you attend religious services last week?
Yes or no.

This question is simple and may be reliable for measuring attendance last week. But is it valid as a measure of religiosity?
There are several problems. A person may attend services for family or social reasons without feeling religious. Another person may be deeply religious but unable to attend services because of illness, disability, transportation barriers, or because their religious practice does not center on formal services. Religious holidays may also affect attendance in particular weeks.
A broader question might be:

How frequently do you attend religious services?
Every week, almost every week, once or twice a month, a few times a year, or never.

This improves on the previous question by capturing usual behavior rather than one week. But it still assumes that religious service attendance is closely related to religiosity.
Another possibility is:

Is religion an important part of your life?
Yes or no.

This gets closer to internal belief, but it forces a complex matter into a binary answer. Someone might feel that religion is somewhat important, occasionally important, culturally important, spiritually important, or important in some contexts but not others.
A more scaled question might be:

On a scale from 1 to 10, how much have you internalized a religious belief system and made its practice a regular part of your life?

This appears more nuanced, but it has its own problems. The wording is complex. “Internalized a religious belief system” may mean different things to different people. The question also asks two things at once: whether someone has internalized beliefs and whether they practice them regularly. A person may have strong beliefs but little practice, or regular practice but weaker belief.
The scale itself is also ambiguous. What does a 7 mean? How is it different from a 6 or an 8? Different respondents may interpret the numbers very differently.
This example illustrates a general rule: the hardest part of data analysis is often not the statistical analysis itself. It is creating measures that actually capture the concepts we care about.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
11. Variables
A measurement strategy produces a variable. A variable is a recorded feature that can take on different values across cases.
Variables have properties. These properties determine what we can do with the data. They affect whether we can calculate means, create certain types of graphs, use particular statistical tests, or interpret results in specific ways.
Three important ways to classify variables are:

Categorical versus quantitative

Level of measurement: nominal, ordinal, interval, or ratio

Discrete versus continuous

These classifications overlap, but each gives us useful information.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
12. Categorical and Quantitative Variables
The most basic distinction is between categorical and quantitative variables.
A quantitative variable uses numbers in a mathematically meaningful way. The values represent amounts.
Examples include:

income

age

temperature

number of siblings

years of education

distance

time

With quantitative variables, the numbers have mathematical meaning. If one person earns $40,000 and another earns $80,000, the second person earns twice as much. If one object is 10 centimeters long and another is 20 centimeters long, the second is twice as long.
A categorical variable places cases into categories. The categories may be named with words or represented by numbers, but the numbers do not necessarily have mathematical meaning.
Examples include:

political party

region

gender

religious affiliation

employment status

agreement or disagreement with a statement

For example, a data set might code Democrats as 1, Republicans as 2, and Independents as 3. But that does not mean Republicans are “twice” Democrats or that Independents are “three times” Democrats. The numbers are just labels.
This distinction is crucial because statistical software will often calculate things whether or not they make conceptual sense. The software may compute a mean for party identification coded as 1, 2, and 3. But that mean is meaningless unless the numbers represent real quantities.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
13. Levels of Measurement
The second classification concerns the level of measurement. The three most important levels are:

Nominal

Ordinal

Interval

A fourth, ratio, is often treated separately, though for many practical purposes it can be understood as a special kind of interval variable.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
14. Discrete and Continuous Variables
The third classification concerns whether a variable is discrete or continuous.
A discrete variable has a well-defined set of possible values. Count variables are discrete.
Examples include:

number of children

number of siblings

number of schools in a district

number of votes

number of arrests

You can have 2 children or 3 children, but not 2.47 children.
Categorical variables are also discrete because cases fall into distinct categories.
A continuous variable can, in principle, take on infinitely many values within a range.
Examples include:

distance

time

height

weight

temperature

A distance can be measured in meters, centimeters, millimeters, or smaller units. In principle, we can always divide the scale more finely.
In practice, continuous variables are often recorded in rounded form. Age is technically continuous, but surveys usually record age in whole years. A three-year-old may proudly say they are “three and a half,” but most data sets do not record age that precisely.
Likewise, income may seem continuous in principle, but in practice it is often recorded in dollars or categories. Measurement always involves practical choices.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
15. Why Variable Type Matters
The properties of variables determine what we can do with them.
For example, the type of graph we use depends on the variable. A continuous variable is often displayed using a histogram, where bars touch because the underlying scale is continuous. A discrete categorical variable is often displayed using a bar chart, where bars are separated because the categories are distinct.
Variable type also affects what summary statistics are meaningful.
The three common measures of central tendency are:

Mode: the most common value or category

Median: the middle value when cases are ordered

Mean: the arithmetic average

Which measures can we use?



Level of Measurement
Mode
Median
Mean




Nominal
Yes
No
No


Ordinal
Yes
Yes
Usually no, though sometimes approximated


Interval/Ratio
Yes
Yes
Yes



For nominal variables, only the mode makes sense. If religious affiliation is coded as Protestant = 1, Catholic = 2, Jewish = 3, Muslim = 4, Hindu = 5, and no religion = 6, calculating the average would not produce a meaningful result.
For ordinal variables, the median and mode are meaningful because the categories can be ordered. But the mean is questionable unless we are willing to assume that the distances between categories are approximately equal.
For interval and ratio variables, the mean, median, and mode are all possible, though the mode is often less useful when there are many unique values.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
16. Dichotomous Variables
A dichotomous variable has exactly two categories.
Examples include:

yes/no

employed/not employed

voted/did not vote

Democrat/not Democrat

treatment/control

heads/tails

Dichotomous variables are technically nominal because they consist of categories. However, they have special properties when coded as 0 and 1.
For example:

Employed = 1

Not employed = 0

If we calculate the mean of this variable, the result is the proportion of cases coded 1.
Suppose we have ten people. Seven are employed and three are not. The variable contains seven 1s and three 0s. The mean is:
[ = 0.7 ]
That means 70 percent of the sample is employed.
This is a special case where taking the mean of a nominal variable makes sense because of the 0/1 coding. Dichotomous variables are also important in regression analysis, where they can be used as indicators or “dummy variables.”
The term dummy variable simply means that the variable stands in for membership in a category. It is not a judgment about the variable or the people in the category.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
17. Practical Flexibility in Measurement
The rules about measurement levels are important, but researchers sometimes bend them for practical reasons.
For example, researchers often calculate means for ordinal scales. Grade point averages are a familiar example. Letter grades are ordinal: A is higher than B, B is higher than C, and so on. Yet schools often convert them into numbers and calculate averages.
Similarly, political scientists often calculate mean ideology scores using seven-point ideology scales. This is not strictly ideal, but if the scale behaves approximately like an interval variable, the mean may still be useful.
Researchers also sometimes convert interval or ratio variables into ordinal categories. Income is a common example. Instead of asking people to report exact income, a survey might ask them to choose a range:

Less than $10,000

$10,000 to $24,999

$25,000 to $49,999

$50,000 to $99,999

$100,000 or more

This loses information, but it has advantages. People may be more willing to report a category than an exact income. They may also know their approximate income range more easily than their precise income. In policy research, income categories may correspond to eligibility thresholds for public programs.
Sometimes researchers treat discrete variables as continuous when there are many possible values. For example, the number of students in a school is technically discrete. A school can have 423 students or 424 students, but not 423.6 students. Yet if we are analyzing hundreds of schools with enrollments ranging from 50 to 3,000, we might graph enrollment using a histogram and treat it as approximately continuous.
The key is not to memorize rules mechanically. The key is to understand what assumptions we are making and whether those assumptions are reasonable for the analysis at hand.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
18. Indexes and Scales
Researchers often combine multiple items into a single measure. Two common terms for such measures are index and scale. These terms are sometimes used loosely, but they have distinct meanings.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
section: 19. The Central Lesson: Data Are Produced by Methods
text:
19. The Central Lesson: Data Are Produced by Methods
The most important lesson of this chapter is that data are not simply “out there” waiting to be collected. Data are produced through methods.
Every variable reflects choices:

What concept are we trying to measure?

How do we define it?

What procedure do we use?

What categories or scale do we provide?

Who reports the information?

What incentives or pressures affect the response?

How are answers coded?

What information is lost when we simplify?

These choices matter. They affect what we can conclude from our analysis.
A classic illustration is the fishing net. If a net has large holes, small fish pass through. After pulling up the net, we might conclude that there are no small fish in the ocean. But that conclusion would be wrong. The problem is not the ocean; it is the measurement strategy.
The same is true in social research. If our survey, administrative data, or coding strategy cannot capture certain people, behaviors, identities, or experiences, we may mistakenly conclude that they do not exist or do not matter.
Good researchers remain humble about measurement. They ask not only, “What do the data show?” but also, “How were these data created?”

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
Key Terms
Concept
An abstract idea or label used to describe something in the world.
Operationalization
The process of turning a concept into a measurable variable.
Variable
A recorded characteristic that can take on different values.
Measurement strategy
The procedure used to measure a concept and record it as data.
Reliability
The consistency or reproducibility of a measure.
Validity
The extent to which a measure captures the concept it is intended to measure.
Response bias
A measurement problem that occurs when respondents give answers that do not accurately reflect their true beliefs, behaviors, or experiences.
Categorical variable
A variable that places cases into categories.
Quantitative variable
A variable whose values are numbers with mathematical meaning.
Nominal variable
A categorical variable with no inherent ordering among categories.
Ordinal variable
A categorical variable whose categories have a meaningful order.
Interval variable
A quantitative variable with equal distances between values.
Ratio variable
An interval variable with a true zero.
Discrete variable
A variable with distinct, separate possible values.
Continuous variable
A variable that can, in principle, take on infinitely many values within a range.
Dichotomous variable
A variable with exactly two categories, often coded 0 and 1.
Index
A measure created by combining multiple indicators, often by adding them together.
Scale
A measure designed to locate cases along an underlying dimension, often using ordered response options.

title: Chapter 1: Measurement
text:
Chapter Summary
Measurement is the foundation of statistical analysis. Before we can analyze data, we must decide how to turn abstract concepts into concrete variables. This process requires conceptual clarity, practical judgment, and awareness of trade-offs.
No measure is perfect. A measure may be easy to collect but only loosely connected to the concept. Another may be conceptually strong but difficult to administer. Existing data may be convenient but built on measurement choices we would not have made ourselves.
Reliable measures produce consistent results. Valid measures capture the intended concept. The best measures are both reliable and valid, but many real-world measures fall short in some way.
Finally, variables have properties that shape statistical analysis. Whether a variable is nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio, discrete, continuous, categorical, quantitative, or dichotomous affects what summaries, graphs, and statistical procedures are appropriate.
Measurement is not a preliminary nuisance before the “real” analysis begins. Measurement is part of the analysis. If we do not understand how the data were produced, we cannot responsibly interpret what the data seem to show.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
1. Distributions
A distribution describes how the values of a variable appear in a sample or population.
More specifically, a distribution tells us the relative frequency with which different values of a variable occur. Some values may appear often, while others may appear rarely. Describing that pattern is one of the first steps in understanding data.
For example, suppose we have survey data on party identification. We may want to know how many respondents identify as Democrats, Republicans, Independents, or members of another party. The distribution of party identification tells us how the sample is divided across those categories.
Or suppose we have data on age. Some ages may be more common than others. A sample of undergraduate students will likely have many people between 18 and 22, while a sample of registered voters will likely have a much wider age distribution.
Distributions can be described in tables, graphs, and summary statistics.
Two broad features of distributions are especially important:

Central tendency
Where is the “typical” value?
Dispersion
How spread out are the values?

Before we can summarize a distribution, however, we need to think about the type of variable we are describing.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
section: 2. Distributions of Categorical and Discrete Variables
text:
2. Distributions of Categorical and Discrete Variables
For categorical or discrete variables, we often begin with a frequency distribution.
A frequency distribution shows how many cases fall into each value or category of a variable.
For example, consider a survey question about religious service attendance. Respondents may be categorized as follows:









Religious Service Attendance
Frequency
Percent
Cumulative Percent




Never
2,377
40.4%
40.4%


A few times a year
880
15.0%
55.4%


Once or twice a month
749
12.7%
68.1%


Almost every week
559
9.5%
77.6%


Every week
964
16.4%
94.0%


More than once a week
353
6.0%
100.0%


Total
5,882
100.0%




The frequency column gives the number of respondents in each category.
The percent column gives the relative frequency of each category. For example, 40.4 percent of respondents said they never attend religious services.
The cumulative percent column adds categories as we move down the table. After the first row, we have accounted for 40.4 percent of the sample. After the second row, we have accounted for 55.4 percent. By the final row, we have accounted for 100 percent of the sample.
Frequency tables are especially useful for categorical variables because they show which categories are common and which are rare.
The same information can also be displayed visually using a bar chart.
A bar chart is appropriate for categorical or discrete data because the values are distinct from one another. The bars should be separated to indicate that the categories are separate.
For example, a bar chart of religious service attendance would have one bar for “Never,” another for “A few times a year,” another for “Once or twice a month,” and so on. The height of each bar could represent either the frequency or the percentage of respondents in that category.
The tallest bar identifies the most common category. In this example, “Never” is the most common response.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
3. Distributions of Continuous Variables
For continuous variables, the logic is slightly different.
A continuous variable can, in principle, take on infinitely many values within a range. Examples include:

age
income
height
weight
temperature
time
distance

Because continuous variables can take so many possible values, we usually do not list every individual value in a frequency table. Instead, we group values into ranges called bins.
For example, if we are describing age in a survey of adults, we might use bins such as:



Age Range
Frequency
Percent
Cumulative Percent




18–29
451
21.5%
21.5%


30–39
386
18.4%
39.9%


40–49
359
17.1%
57.0%


50–59
402
19.2%
76.2%


60–69
301
14.4%
90.6%


70+
197
9.4%
100.0%



The exact bins depend on the data and the purpose of the analysis. Often, bins are equal in width. For age, we might use ten-year intervals. But sometimes we use wider bins at the high or low end if there are fewer cases in those ranges.
The key rule is that bins should not overlap. A person should fit into one and only one bin.
For continuous variables, the standard visual display is a histogram.
A histogram resembles a bar chart, but the bars touch. The touching bars indicate that the underlying variable is continuous. The boundaries between bins are artificial divisions imposed for display purposes.
For example, someone who is 39 years and 11 months old is very close to someone who is 40 years old. A histogram visually reflects that continuity by placing the bars next to one another.
The choice of bin width matters. If bins are too wide, we may hide important variation. If bins are too narrow, the graph may become noisy and difficult to interpret.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
4. Describing Distributions
Once we have examined a distribution, we usually want to summarize it.
Descriptive statistics generally fall into two main categories:

Measures of central tendency
Measures of dispersion

A measure of central tendency describes what is typical, central, or common in a distribution.
The three most common measures of central tendency are:

mean
median
mode

A measure of dispersion describes how spread out the data are.
Common measures of dispersion include:

range
interquartile range
variance
standard deviation

Not every measure is appropriate for every variable. The measurement level of the variable determines what summaries make sense.
For example, the mean of income is meaningful. The mean of political party identification, if party is coded as Democrat = 1, Republican = 2, Independent = 3, is not meaningful. Statistical software may calculate it anyway, but the result does not make conceptual sense.
Software will often do what you ask, even when you should not have asked. The responsibility remains with the researcher.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
5. Central Tendency
A measure of central tendency describes what is typical in a distribution.
In everyday language, people often use the word “average” loosely. In statistics, we should be more precise. The word “average” can refer to different ideas, so it is better to specify whether we mean the mean, median, or mode.
These three measures capture different aspects of typicality:

The mean is the arithmetic center.
The median is the middle value in a rank ordering.
The mode is the most common value.

Sometimes these three measures are the same. Often, they are not.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
6. The Mean
The mean is the arithmetic center of a distribution.
It is the value we get by adding up all observations and dividing by the number of observations.
For a population, the mean is represented by the Greek letter mu:
[ ]
For a sample, the mean is usually represented by placing a bar over the variable name:
[ {Y} ]
The mean is appropriate for interval- or ratio-level variables. It is not appropriate for nominal variables, and it is only sometimes used for ordinal variables when researchers are willing to treat the ordinal scale as approximately interval-level.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
7. Summation Notation
To express the formula for the mean, we need summation notation.
Suppose we have a variable (Y), and each case has a value of that variable:
[ Y_1, Y_2, Y_3, , Y_N ]
The subscript identifies the case. (Y_1) is the value of (Y) for the first case, (Y_2) for the second case, and so on.
The Greek capital letter sigma,
[ ]
means “sum” or “add up.”
So this expression:
[ _{i=1}^{N} Y_i ]
means:
[ Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3 + + Y_N ]
In words: add up all the values of (Y) from the first case through the final case.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
8. The Population Mean
If we have data for the full population, the population mean is:
[ Y = {i=1}^{N} Y_i ]
Equivalently:
[ _Y = ]
where:

(Y_i) is the value of (Y) for case (i)
(N) is the size of the population
(_Y) is the population mean of (Y)

The mean can also be understood as an expected value.
The expected value of (Y), written (E(Y)), is the value we would expect on average if we randomly selected a case from the population.
For a finite population where each case is equally likely to be selected:
[ E(Y) = _{i=1}^{N} Y_i ]
Because each case has probability (1/N), this simplifies to the familiar formula for the mean:
[ E(Y) = _{i=1}^{N} Y_i ]
Thus:
[ E(Y) = _Y ]
The mean is the probability-weighted center of the distribution.
This idea will become important later. For example, if we roll a fair six-sided die, the possible values are 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6. Each has probability (1/6). The expected value is:
[ E(Y) = 1() + 2() + 3() + 4() + 5() + 6() ]
[ E(Y) = 3.5 ]
Notice that 3.5 is not a possible die roll. We cannot roll a 3.5. But 3.5 is still the expected value, because it is the mathematical center of the probability distribution.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
9. The Sample Mean
Usually, we do not observe the full population. Instead, we have a sample.
For a sample of size (n), the sample mean is:
[ {Y} = _{i=1}^{n} Y_i ]
Equivalently:
[ {Y} = ]
The sample mean ({Y}) is a statistic. It estimates the population mean (_Y), which is a parameter.
This distinction is important:

(_Y) is the true mean in the population.
({Y}) is the mean in the sample.
We usually use ({Y}) to estimate (_Y).

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
10. The Median
The median is the middle value in an ordered distribution.
To find the median, we rank the data from lowest to highest and identify the value that divides the data in half. Fifty percent of the observations lie at or below the median, and fifty percent lie at or above it.
The median is the 50th percentile.
A percentile is a position in a distribution below which a certain percentage of observations fall. For example:

The 25th percentile is the point below which 25 percent of the data fall.
The 50th percentile is the point below which 50 percent of the data fall.
The 75th percentile is the point below which 75 percent of the data fall.

The median can be used for ordinal, interval, and ratio variables. It cannot be used for nominal variables because nominal categories have no inherent order.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
11. Finding the Median
To find the median:

Sort the observations from lowest to highest.
Identify the middle position.
Find the value associated with that position.

If the sample size is odd, there is one middle observation.
If the sample size is even, the median lies halfway between the two middle observations.
A useful formula for finding the median position is:
[ ]
where (n) is the sample size.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
12. The Mode
The mode is the most common value in a distribution.
For example, if the most common response to a survey question is “Never,” then “Never” is the mode.
The mode is the only measure of central tendency that can be used with nominal variables. It can also be used with ordinal and interval-level variables.
The mode captures a different idea of typicality than the mean or median. It tells us what value appears most often, not where the mathematical center or rank-order middle lies.
A distribution can have:

one mode
more than one mode
no meaningful mode

For continuous variables, the mode can be less useful. If values are measured very precisely, many observations may have unique values. In that case, the “most common” value may be arbitrary or nonexistent.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
section: 13. Which Measures of Central Tendency Are Available?
text:
13. Which Measures of Central Tendency Are Available?
The measurement level of the variable determines which measures of central tendency make sense.



Measurement Level
Mean
Median
Mode




Nominal
No
No
Yes


Ordinal
Usually no
Yes
Yes


Interval/Ratio
Yes
Yes
Yes



For nominal variables, only the mode is meaningful.
For ordinal variables, the median and mode are meaningful. The mean is usually not appropriate unless we are willing to treat the ordinal scale as approximately interval-level.
For interval and ratio variables, all three are available, though the mode may be less informative for continuous variables.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
section: 14. Comparing the Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
14. Comparing the Mean, Median, and Mode
The mean, median, and mode often tell different stories about the same distribution.
The mean is sensitive to extreme values. If a distribution contains very large or very small outliers, the mean can be pulled toward those outliers.
For example, imagine calculating the average wealth of people in a classroom. If everyone in the room has modest wealth, the mean may give a reasonable sense of typical wealth. But if Elon Musk walks into the room, the mean wealth skyrockets. The mean would no longer describe the typical person very well.
The median is less sensitive to extreme values. Adding one extremely wealthy person does not dramatically change the middle position in the rank ordering.
This is why income is often described using the median rather than the mean. Income distributions are usually positively skewed, with a small number of very high-income households pulling the mean upward. Median household income often gives a better sense of the typical household.
The mode identifies the most common value. For categorical variables, this may be the most useful summary. For example, if the most common religious service attendance category is “Never,” that tells us something important about the sample, even though it is not a mathematical center.
The best measure depends on the variable and the purpose of the description.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
15. Dispersion
Measures of central tendency tell us what is typical. Measures of dispersion tell us how spread out the data are.
Two distributions can have the same mean but very different levels of dispersion.
For example, consider two classes with the same average exam score of 80.
In Class A, most students scored between 78 and 82.
In Class B, some students scored near 50 and others near 100.
Both classes have the same mean, but Class B has much more dispersion.
Common measures of dispersion include:

range
interquartile range
variance
standard deviation

As with central tendency, the appropriate measure depends on the measurement level of the variable.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
16. Dispersion for Nominal Variables
For nominal variables, most formal measures of dispersion do not apply.
Nominal categories have no inherent order and no meaningful distance between categories. Therefore, we cannot calculate a range, interquartile range, variance, or standard deviation.
Instead, we describe the distribution qualitatively.
For example, we might say:

The data are concentrated in one category.
The data are evenly distributed across categories.
Two categories dominate the distribution.
Several categories are rare.

Suppose a religious affiliation variable has 70 percent of respondents in one category and very few in the others. We would say the distribution is highly concentrated. If each category has roughly the same percentage, we would say the distribution is more evenly dispersed.
This is still a description of dispersion, but it is not based on mathematical distance.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
17. The Range
The range is the distance between the highest and lowest observed values in the data.
For interval or ratio variables:
[ = - ]
For example, if the youngest person in a sample is 18 and the oldest is 74, the range is:
[ 74 - 18 = 56 ]
The range is simple and easy to understand, but it is crude. It depends entirely on the two most extreme values.
If we add one very old person to an age distribution, the range may increase dramatically. If we remove one extreme observation, the range may shrink dramatically. Because of this, the range can be highly sensitive to outliers.
For ordinal variables, the range can be described qualitatively rather than numerically. For example, responses might range from “Strongly disagree” to “Strongly agree.” We generally should not treat that as a numeric distance unless the ordinal scale is being treated as approximately interval-level.
Also note that the range refers to the observed data, not the full possible range of the variable. Human age can theoretically extend beyond 100, but if the oldest person in our sample is 83, then 83 is the maximum observed value.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
18. Percentiles and Quantiles
To understand the interquartile range, we need to return to percentiles.
A percentile identifies a position in a distribution. The 30th percentile is the value below which 30 percent of the data fall. The 80th percentile is the value below which 80 percent of the data fall.
Some percentiles are especially important.
The 25th percentile is called the first quartile, or (Q_1).
The 50th percentile is the median, or (Q_2).
The 75th percentile is called the third quartile, or (Q_3).
A quartile is a position, not a range. (Q_1) is the point that separates the lowest 25 percent of the data from the upper 75 percent. (Q_3) is the point that separates the lowest 75 percent from the upper 25 percent.
More generally, when we divide data into equal-sized parts, we are creating quantiles.
Examples include:

quartiles: four equal parts
quintiles: five equal parts
deciles: ten equal parts
percentiles: one hundred equal parts

Quantiles help us describe where observations fall in a distribution.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
19. The Interquartile Range
The interquartile range, or IQR, measures the spread of the middle 50 percent of the data.
It is the distance between the 75th percentile and the 25th percentile:
[ IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 ]
The IQR tells us how far we must go on the variable’s scale to cover the middle half of the distribution.
If the IQR is small, the middle 50 percent of the data are tightly clustered. If the IQR is large, the middle 50 percent are more spread out.
The IQR is less sensitive to extreme values than the range. Adding a very large or very small outlier may greatly affect the range, but it usually has little effect on the IQR.
This makes the IQR especially useful for skewed distributions or distributions with outliers.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
20. Finding the Interquartile Range
To find the IQR:

Sort the data from lowest to highest.
Find the median.
Split the data into a lower half and an upper half.
Find the median of the lower half. This is (Q_1).
Find the median of the upper half. This is (Q_3).
Subtract:

[ IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 ]
There are slightly different conventions for how to handle the middle observation when the sample size is odd. Some methods include the median in both halves; others exclude it from both halves. With small samples, this can produce slightly different answers. With large samples, the difference is usually negligible.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
21. IQR for Ordinal Variables
For ordinal variables, the IQR can still be useful, but we should interpret it qualitatively rather than as a precise numeric distance.
Suppose we return to religious service attendance:



Religious Service Attendance
Percent
Cumulative Percent




Never
40.4%
40.4%


A few times a year
15.0%
55.4%


Once or twice a month
12.7%
68.1%


Almost every week
9.5%
77.6%


Every week
16.4%
94.0%


More than once a week
6.0%
100.0%



To find (Q_1), we locate the category containing the 25th percentile.
The first category, “Never,” already brings us to 40.4 percent. Therefore, the 25th percentile falls within “Never.”
So:
[ Q_1 = ]
To find (Q_3), we locate the category containing the 75th percentile.
The cumulative percentage reaches 77.6 percent at “Almost every week.” Therefore:
[ Q_3 = ]
So the middle 50 percent of the distribution ranges from “Never” to “Almost every week.”
This is not a numeric distance. It is a description of which ordered categories contain the middle half of the data.
Also notice that the median is not the middle category in the list. The median is the category containing the middle person. In this example, the cumulative percentage passes 50 percent at “A few times a year,” so the median category is “A few times a year.”

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
22. Box-and-Whisker Plots
The interquartile range often appears in a graph called a box-and-whisker plot, or simply a box plot.
A box plot usually displays:

the median
(Q_1)
(Q_3)
the interquartile range
whiskers showing additional spread
sometimes individual outliers

The box itself represents the middle 50 percent of the data. The line inside the box marks the median.
The whiskers may be defined in different ways. In some graphs, they extend to the minimum and maximum values. In others, they extend to values within 1.5 times the IQR, with points beyond that shown as outliers.
Box plots are useful for comparing distributions across groups.
For example, suppose we compare the heights of soccer players by position. Goalkeepers may have a higher median height and a smaller IQR, meaning they are generally tall and relatively clustered in height. Defenders may show a wider IQR, indicating more variation. A box plot allows us to see these differences quickly.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
23. Variability Around the Mean
The range and IQR describe spread using positions in the distribution. Variance and standard deviation describe spread around the mean.
These measures apply to interval- and ratio-level variables. They are not appropriate for nominal variables. They are also generally not appropriate for ordinal variables unless the researcher is treating the ordinal scale as approximately interval-level.
The central question is:

How far are observations, typically, from the mean?

If most observations are close to the mean, dispersion is low. If many observations are far from the mean, dispersion is high.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
24. Deviations from the Mean
For each observation, we can calculate its deviation from the mean:
[ Y_i - ]
for a population, or:
[ Y_i - {Y} ]
for a sample.
A deviation tells us how far an observation is from the mean and in which direction.
If the deviation is positive, the observation is above the mean.
If the deviation is negative, the observation is below the mean.
For example, suppose we have a population of five values:
[ 5, 12, 6, 9, 3 ]
The mean is:
[ = = = 7 ]
Now calculate each deviation from the mean:



Value (Y_i)
Mean ()
Deviation (Y_i - )




5
7
-2


12
7
5


6
7
-1


9
7
2


3
7
-4



If we add the deviations, we get:
[ -2 + 5 - 1 + 2 - 4 = 0 ]
This always happens. The sum of deviations from the mean is always zero.
That creates a problem. If we try to calculate the average deviation from the mean, the positive and negative deviations cancel out.
So we need a way to make deviations positive before averaging them.
One option would be to take absolute values. That produces a statistic called the mean absolute deviation. But for reasons that become important in more advanced statistics, squared deviations have better mathematical properties.
So instead of taking absolute values, we square the deviations.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
25. Sum of Squares
A squared deviation is:
[ (Y_i - )^2 ]
for a population, or:
[ (Y_i - {Y})^2 ]
for a sample.
Using the same data:









Value (Y_i)
Mean ()
Deviation (Y_i - )
Squared Deviation ((Y_i - )^2)




5
7
-2
4


12
7
5
25


6
7
-1
1


9
7
2
4


3
7
-4
16



Now add the squared deviations:
[ 4 + 25 + 1 + 4 + 16 = 50 ]
This total is called the sum of squares.
For a population:
[ SS = _{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - )^2 ]
For a sample:
[ SS = _{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - {Y})^2 ]
The more spread out the data are around the mean, the larger the sum of squares will be.
If every observation is exactly equal to the mean, every deviation is zero, and the sum of squares is zero.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
26. Population Variance
The variance is the mean squared deviation from the mean.
For a population, variance is represented by:
[ ^2 ]
The formula is:
[ ^2 = _{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - )^2 ]
Equivalently:
[ ^2 = ]
Using our example, the sum of squares is 50 and the population size is 5:
[ ^2 = = 10 ]
So the population variance is 10.
The variance is the expected squared distance between an observation and the mean.
However, variance is difficult to interpret because it is expressed in squared units. If the variable is age, the variance is in “years squared.” If the variable is income, the variance is in “dollars squared.” These units are not intuitive.
To return to the original units of the variable, we take the square root. That gives us the standard deviation.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
27. Population Standard Deviation
The standard deviation is the square root of the variance.
For a population, standard deviation is represented by:
[ ]
The formula is:
[ = ]
Equivalently:
[ = ]
Using our example:
[ ^2 = 10 ]
So:
[ = ]
The standard deviation can be interpreted as the typical distance of observations from the mean, in a root-mean-square sense.
A small standard deviation means observations tend to be close to the mean.
A large standard deviation means observations tend to be spread farther from the mean.
The standard deviation cannot be negative. Its smallest possible value is zero, which occurs only when every observation has the same value.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
section: 28. Sample Variance and Sample Standard Deviation
text:
28. Sample Variance and Sample Standard Deviation
In practice, we usually do not have population data. We have sample data.
The sample standard deviation is represented by:
[ s ]
The sample variance is:
[ s^2 ]
The formula for the sample variance is:
[ s^2 = _{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - {Y})^2 ]
The formula for the sample standard deviation is:
[ s = ]
Notice the denominator:
[ n - 1 ]
not:
[ n ]
This difference is important.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
29. Why Do We Divide by (n - 1)?
When calculating the population variance, we divide by (N). When calculating the sample variance, we divide by (n - 1).
Why?
Because the sample variance is used to estimate the population variance. We do not know the population mean (), so we use the sample mean ({Y}) instead.
But ({Y}) is itself estimated from the same sample data. Once we use the data to estimate the mean, we lose one independent piece of information for estimating variability around that mean.
This is the idea of degrees of freedom.
The sample has (n) observations, but after estimating the sample mean, only (n - 1) observations are free to vary independently around that mean.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
30. Degrees of Freedom
Degrees of freedom can be difficult at first, so consider a simple example.
Suppose we have two observations:
[ 2, 4 ]
The mean is:
[ {Y} = 3 ]
Now suppose you know the mean is 3 and I tell you that one observation is 2. What must the other observation be?
It must be 4.
It is not free to be anything else, because the two values must average to 3.
With two observations and a known sample mean, only one observation is free to vary. The other is determined.
That is why, when estimating variance around a sample mean, we have:
[ n - 1 ]
degrees of freedom.
The same logic applies to larger samples. If we have 100 observations and we know the sample mean, then once 99 values are known, the final value is fixed. It must be whatever value makes the mean come out correctly.
So when calculating sample variance, we divide by (n - 1) to account for the fact that we already used the data to estimate the mean.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
section: 31. The Correction Matters Most in Small Samples
text:
31. The Correction Matters Most in Small Samples
Dividing by (n - 1) rather than (n) makes the estimate of variance slightly larger.
This corrects a tendency for samples to underestimate population variability. A sample is unlikely to capture the full spread of the population, especially when the sample is small. It may miss extreme values in the tails of the distribution.
Using (n - 1) inflates the estimate slightly, making it a better estimator of the population variance.
The correction matters more when the sample is small.
For example, dividing by 6 instead of 7 makes a noticeable difference. But dividing by 1,499 instead of 1,500 makes almost no difference.
As sample size increases, the distinction between (n) and (n - 1) becomes less important.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
section: 32. Summary of Variance and Standard Deviation Formulas
text:
32. Summary of Variance and Standard Deviation Formulas
For a population:
[ = _{i=1}^{N} Y_i ]
[ ^2 = _{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - )^2 ]
[ = ]
For a sample:
[ {Y} = _{i=1}^{n} Y_i ]
[ s^2 = _{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - {Y})^2 ]
[ s = ]
The sample statistics ({Y}), (s^2), and (s) estimate the population parameters (), (^2), and ().

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
33. Skewness
A distribution is skewed if it is asymmetric.
A symmetric distribution has the same general shape on both sides. A classic example is the normal distribution, or bell curve. In a perfectly symmetric normal distribution, the mean, median, and mode all line up.
A skewed distribution has a longer tail on one side than the other.
There are two main types of skewness:

Positive skew, also called right skew
Negative skew, also called left skew

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
34. Positive Skew
A distribution has positive skew if it has a long tail extending toward higher values.
In a positively skewed distribution, most observations are concentrated toward the lower end, while a smaller number of observations stretch far to the right.
Income is a common example. Most households earn relatively moderate incomes, while a smaller number earn extremely high incomes. Those high-income observations create a long right tail.
In positively skewed distributions, the mean is often greater than the median because the high values pull the mean upward.
Typically:
[ > ]
However, this is a rule of thumb, not a law. The best way to assess skewness is to look at the distribution itself.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
35. Negative Skew
A distribution has negative skew if it has a long tail extending toward lower values.
In a negatively skewed distribution, most observations are concentrated toward the higher end, while a smaller number stretch far to the left.
For example, an easy exam might produce a negatively skewed distribution if most students score high but a few score much lower.
In negatively skewed distributions, the mean is often less than the median because the low values pull the mean downward.
Typically:
[ < ]
Again, this is only a rule of thumb. Some discrete distributions may violate this pattern.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
section: 36. Skewness and Choice of Summary Statistic
text:
36. Skewness and Choice of Summary Statistic
Skewness matters because it affects how we describe what is typical.
When a distribution is symmetric, the mean and median may be similar. In that case, either may provide a reasonable summary of central tendency.
When a distribution is skewed, the mean and median can differ substantially.
Consider household income. Suppose the median household income is:
[ $56,516 ]
while the mean household income is:
[ $79,263 ]
These numbers tell different stories.
The mean is higher because high-income households pull it upward. The median tells us the income of the household in the middle of the distribution. Half of households earn less than the median, and half earn more.
If we want to describe the typical household, the median is usually more informative. If we want to calculate total income divided equally across households, the mean may be appropriate.
Neither statistic is inherently wrong. They answer different questions.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
37. Reading a Skewed Distribution
When examining a distribution, ask:

Is the distribution symmetric?
Is there a long tail?
If so, does the tail extend toward higher or lower values?
Are there outliers?
How different are the mean and median?
Which measure of central tendency best communicates what is typical?

A positively skewed distribution has a long right tail.
A negatively skewed distribution has a long left tail.
The name of the skew refers to the direction of the tail.
This is easy to mix up. Remember: the skew points toward the tail, not toward where most of the data are concentrated.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
38. Matching Statistics to Measurement Level
The most important practical rule is that descriptive statistics must match the measurement level of the variable.









Variable Type
Useful Displays
Central Tendency
Dispersion




Nominal
Frequency table, bar chart
Mode
Qualitative description


Ordinal
Frequency table, bar chart
Median, mode
Range, IQR by category


Interval/Ratio, discrete
Frequency table, bar chart, histogram if many values
Mean, median, mode
Range, IQR, variance, standard deviation


Interval/Ratio, continuous
Histogram, box plot
Mean, median, sometimes mode
Range, IQR, variance, standard deviation



Software may compute statistics that are not meaningful. A researcher must know when a statistic is appropriate.
For example:

A mean of income is meaningful.
A mean of religious affiliation is not meaningful.
A median of ideology may be meaningful if ideology is ordered.
A standard deviation of party identification is not meaningful if party categories are just labels.
A mode can be used for almost any variable, but may be unhelpful for continuous data.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
39. Descriptive Statistics as Interpretation
Descriptive statistics are not just mechanical calculations. They are choices about how to represent data.
Suppose we describe income using the mean. We emphasize total income divided across households. Suppose we describe it using the median. We emphasize the household in the middle. Suppose we describe it using a histogram. We show the entire shape of the distribution, including skewness and outliers.
Each description is true in a narrow sense, but each highlights different features.
Good descriptive analysis often uses multiple tools:

a graph to show the shape of the distribution
a measure of central tendency to identify what is typical
a measure of dispersion to show spread
attention to skewness and outliers
careful interpretation based on the measurement level of the variable

Descriptive statistics are the foundation for later statistical analysis. If we do not understand the basic structure of the data, we are not ready to model relationships or test hypotheses.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Key Terms
Distribution
The pattern of values of a variable in a sample or population.
Frequency distribution
A table showing how many cases fall into each value or category.
Relative frequency
The proportion or percentage of cases in a category.
Cumulative frequency
The running total of frequencies as one moves through ordered categories.
Bar chart
A graph used to display categorical or discrete data.
Histogram
A graph used to display continuous data grouped into bins.
Central tendency
A summary of what is typical or central in a distribution.
Mean
The arithmetic center of a distribution.
Expected value
The probability-weighted average value of a variable.
Median
The middle value in an ordered distribution; the 50th percentile.
Mode
The most common value or category.
Dispersion
The spread of values in a distribution.
Range
The distance between the highest and lowest observed values.
Percentile
A position below which a given percentage of the data fall.
Quartile
A position dividing the data into quarters. (Q_1) is the 25th percentile, (Q_2) is the median, and (Q_3) is the 75th percentile.
Interquartile range
The distance between (Q_3) and (Q_1), covering the middle 50 percent of the data.
Sum of squares
The sum of squared deviations from the mean.
Variance
The mean squared deviation from the mean.
Standard deviation
The square root of the variance; a measure of typical spread around the mean.
Degrees of freedom
The number of independent pieces of information available for estimating a statistic.
Skewness
Asymmetry in a distribution.
Positive skew
A distribution with a long tail extending toward higher values.
Negative skew
A distribution with a long tail extending toward lower values.

title: Chapter 3: Descriptive Statistics
text:
Chapter Summary
Descriptive statistics allow us to summarize and understand data before moving to more advanced analysis. The first step is to examine a variable’s distribution: the pattern of values in the sample or population.
Categorical and discrete variables are often summarized using frequency tables and bar charts. Continuous variables are often summarized using histograms, where values are grouped into bins.
Measures of central tendency describe what is typical. The mean is the arithmetic center, the median is the middle value in a rank ordering, and the mode is the most common value. Which measure is appropriate depends on the measurement level of the variable and the shape of the distribution.
Measures of dispersion describe how spread out the data are. The range captures the distance between the minimum and maximum observed values. The interquartile range captures the spread of the middle 50 percent. Variance and standard deviation measure spread around the mean.
The standard deviation is especially important because it appears throughout statistical analysis. It is based on squared deviations from the mean and, for sample data, is calculated using (n - 1) degrees of freedom.
Finally, skewness describes asymmetry. Positively skewed distributions have long right tails; negatively skewed distributions have long left tails. Skewness affects how we interpret the mean, median, and mode. In skewed distributions, the median often gives a better sense of what is typical than the mean.
The central lesson is that descriptive statistics are not merely calculations. They are tools for representing data accurately. Good analysis begins by choosing the right summaries for the right variables.

title: Chapter 6: Sampling Distributions
section: 1. From Probability Distributions to Sampling Distributions
text:
1. From Probability Distributions to Sampling Distributions
A probability distribution describes the probabilities associated with the possible values of a random variable.
A sampling distribution is a special kind of probability distribution. It describes the probabilities associated with the possible values of a sample statistic.
Examples of sample statistics include:

a sample mean, ({X})
a sample proportion, ()
a sample standard deviation, (s)
a difference between two sample means
a regression coefficient

These statistics are random variables because they are calculated from random samples.
Once we have a sample, the statistic is fixed. If we calculate the mean of our sample, there is only one value it can take for that sample. But before we collect the sample, we do not know what the mean will be. Different random samples would produce different sample means.
A sampling distribution describes how those sample means, proportions, or other statistics would vary across repeated random samples.

title: Chapter 6: Sampling Distributions
text:
2. A Thought Exercise: Rolling Dice
Imagine rolling a fair six-sided die 200 times and calculating the mean of the 200 rolls.
The expected value of a fair die roll is:
[ E(X) = 3.5 ]
That is because the possible values are:
[ 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 ]
and the mean of those values is:
[ = 3.5 ]
If we rolled the die infinitely many times, the mean would converge to 3.5. But if we roll it only 200 times, the sample mean will probably not be exactly 3.5. It might be 3.42, 3.57, 3.49, or 3.61.
Now imagine that everyone in a class rolls a die 200 times and calculates their own mean. Each person would get a different sample mean. Those means would vary because each set of 200 rolls is a different random sample from the same underlying probability distribution.
The sample mean is therefore a random variable.
Now imagine repeating this process not 40 or 50 times, but 1,000 times. We would have 1,000 sample means. If we graphed those means, we would see a distribution.
That distribution is the sampling distribution of the sample mean.

title: Chapter 6: Sampling Distributions
text:
3. What a Sampling Distribution Shows
A sampling distribution tells us how a statistic behaves across repeated random samples.
For example, the sampling distribution of ({X}) tells us the probability that the sample mean falls in different ranges.
It answers questions like:

How likely is the sample mean to be close to the population mean?
How likely is it to be far away?
How much does the sample mean vary across repeated samples?
What is the typical size of random sampling error?

In practice, we usually do not observe the sampling distribution. We usually collect one sample and calculate one sample mean. We do not repeatedly collect thousands of samples.
But statistical theory tells us what the sampling distribution looks like under certain conditions. Simulation can help us see the idea, but theory is what lets us use it in real research.

title: Chapter 6: Sampling Distributions
text:
4. Sample Distribution Versus Sampling Distribution
Because the terms sound similar, it is worth being very clear.
A sample distribution is the distribution of the actual data in one sample.
For example, if we survey 1,000 people and record their incomes, the sample distribution shows how those 1,000 incomes are distributed.
A sampling distribution is the distribution of a sample statistic across repeated samples.
For example, if we repeatedly survey 1,000 people, calculate the mean income in each sample, and then graph all those sample means, that graph is the sampling distribution of the sample mean.
The sample distribution is observed.
The sampling distribution is theoretical.
The sample distribution describes values of individual observations.
The sampling distribution describes values of statistics.
This distinction is central.

title: Chapter 6: Sampling Distributions
text:
5. Why Sampling Distributions Matter
Sampling distributions matter because they allow us to estimate uncertainty.
