0.300
1.000



Suppose we want:
[ P(Yes Democrat) ]
That is, among Democrats, what is the probability of supporting a minimum wage increase?
Use the formula:
[ P(Yes Democrat) = ]
Substitute values:
[ P(Yes Democrat) = ]
[ P(Yes Democrat) ]
So, given that a respondent is a Democrat, the probability that they support raising the minimum wage is about 0.84.
Compare that to the unconditional probability:
[ P(Yes) = 0.646 ]
Knowing that a person is a Democrat changes our estimate of the probability that they support raising the minimum wage.
That suggests party identification and minimum wage attitudes are not independent.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
26. Another Conditional Probability Example
Now suppose we want:
[ P(Independent No) ]
That is, among those who oppose raising the minimum wage, what is the probability that a person is an independent?
Use the formula:
[ P(Independent No) = ]
Substitute values:
[ P(Independent No) = ]
[ P(Independent No) ]
So among people who oppose raising the minimum wage, about 32.5 percent are independents.
Notice that this is close to the unconditional probability of being an independent:
[ P(Independent) = 0.337 ]
That particular conditional probability does not change much. But that alone does not prove independence for the full relationship. Other categories may still differ substantially.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
27. Independence
Two events are independent if knowing that one occurred does not change the probability that the other occurred.
Events (A) and (B) are independent if:
[ P(A B) = P(A) ]
Equivalently:
[ P(B A) = P(B) ]
If the conditional probability equals the unconditional probability, then the condition did not matter.
For example, suppose the probability that a randomly selected person has a job is:
[ P(Job) = 0.60 ]
Now suppose we condition on having a high school degree. If:
[ P(Job High School Degree) = 0.60 ]
then having a high school degree provides no information about whether someone has a job. The two events are independent.
But if:
[ P(Job High School Degree) > 0.60 ]
then employment and high school degree status are dependent. Knowing one changes the probability of the other.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
28. Independence with Dice
Suppose we roll two dice, one orange and one white.
Let:
[ A = ]
[ B = ]
The probability that the orange die is 1 is:
[ P(A) = ]
The probability that both happen is:
[ P(A B) = ]
The probability that the white die is 3 is:
[ P(B) = ]
Now calculate:
[ P(A B) = ]
[ P(A B) = ]
[ P(A B) = ]
This equals (P(A)). So the events are independent.
Knowing the white die is 3 does not change the probability that the orange die is 1.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
29. Independence with Cards
Suppose we draw one card from a standard 52-card deck.
There are 26 red cards, so:
[ P(Red) = = ]
There are 4 aces, so:
[ P(Ace) = = ]
There are 2 red aces, so:
[ P(Red Ace) = ]
Now calculate:
[ P(Red Ace) = ]
[ P(Red Ace) = ]
[ P(Red Ace) = ]
This equals (P(Red)). So being told that the card is an ace does not change the probability that it is red.
Redness and aceness are independent.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
30. Testing for Independence in Tables
In a joint probability table, we can test for independence by comparing conditional and unconditional probabilities.
Using the minimum wage table:
[ P(Yes) = 0.646 ]
But:
[ P(Yes Democrat) = 0.840 ]
Since:
[ 0.840 ]
party identification and support for raising the minimum wage are not independent.
If two variables have multiple categories, checking one conditional probability may be enough to show dependence. If one conditional probability differs from the unconditional probability, independence fails.
However, finding one category where the conditional probability matches the unconditional probability is not enough to prove independence. Other categories may differ and counterbalance one another.
To establish independence across categorical variables, the relevant conditional probabilities must match across the categories.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
31. General Multiplication Rule
The general multiplication rule gives the probability that two events both occur:
[ P(A B) = P(A)P(B A) ]
Equivalently:
[ P(A B) = P(B)P(A B) ]
This formula always works.
It follows directly from the conditional probability formula:
[ P(B A) = ]
Rearranging gives:
[ P(A B) = P(A)P(B A) ]
This rule is especially useful when events are dependent.
For example, suppose:
[ P(Republican) = 0.300 ]
and:
[ P(No Republican) = ]
Then:
[ P(Republican No) = P(Republican)P(No Republican) ]
[ P(Republican No) = 0.300 () ]
[ P(Republican No) = 0.181 ]

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
32. Multiplication Rule for Independent Events
If two events are independent, then:
[ P(B A) = P(B) ]
So the general multiplication rule becomes:
[ P(A B) = P(A)P(B) ]
This formula should only be used when the events are independent.
For example, if we flip a fair coin twice, the probability of heads on the first flip is 0.5, and the probability of heads on the second flip is also 0.5.
Because the flips are independent:
[ P(Heads Heads) = 0.5 = 0.25 ]
For eight heads in a row:
[ P(8 ) = 0.5^8 ]
[ P(8 ) = 0.0039 ]
That is:
[ ]
The probability is small, but not impossible.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
33. Permutations and Repeated Trials
Suppose we flip a fair coin eight times.
The probability of one specific sequence, such as:
[ T H H H H H H H ]
is:
[ 0.5^8 = ]
But now suppose we ask:

What is the probability of getting exactly seven heads in eight flips?

There are multiple sequences that produce exactly seven heads:
[ T H H H H H H H ]
[ H T H H H H H H ]
[ H H T H H H H H ]
and so on.
There are eight possible positions for the one tail. Each sequence has probability (1/256).
So:
[ P(7 ) = 8 ]
[ P(7 ) = = ]
This is the beginning of the logic behind the binomial distribution, which describes the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials where each trial has two possible outcomes.
We will return to that idea later.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
34. Summary of Core Probability Rules
The major rules introduced so far are:
Complement Rule
[ P() = 1 - P(A) ]
General Addition Rule
[ P(A B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A B) ]
Multiplication Rule for Dependent Events
[ P(A B) = P(A)P(B A) ]
or:
[ P(A B) = P(B)P(A B) ]
Multiplication Rule for Independent Events
[ P(A B) = P(A)P(B) ]
Use the independent-events version only when you know the events are independent.
When in doubt, use the general multiplication rule.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
35. Probability Tree Diagrams
A probability tree is a visual tool for representing sequences of probabilistic events.
A tree begins with a node. Branches extend from the node to represent possible outcomes. Each branch has a probability. The probabilities coming out of a node must sum to 1.
For example, a coin toss has two branches:

Heads: 0.5
Tails: 0.5

If we toss a coin twice, the tree has two stages.
First toss:

Heads: 0.5
Tails: 0.5

Second toss after heads:

Heads: 0.5
Tails: 0.5

Second toss after tails:

Heads: 0.5
Tails: 0.5

The possible sequences are:

HH
HT
TH
TT

Each sequence has probability:
[ 0.5 = 0.25 ]
To find the probability of a path, multiply along the branches.
To find the probability of an event that includes multiple paths, add the probabilities of those paths.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
36. Example: Heads Exactly Once
Suppose we toss a coin twice and ask:

What is the probability of getting heads exactly once?

The favorable paths are:

HT
TH

Each has probability 0.25.
So:
[ P() = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50 ]

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
37. Example: Heads At Least Once
Now ask:

What is the probability of getting heads at least once?

The favorable paths are:

HH
HT
TH

So:
[ P() = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.75 ]
We can also use the complement rule.
The only way not to get heads at least once is to get no heads:

TT

That has probability 0.25.
So:
[ P() = 1 - P() ]
[ P() = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75 ]
The complement rule is often faster when an event has many possible paths.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
38. Probability Trees with Unequal Probabilities
Suppose airport security randomly sends travelers to either a short screening line or a long screening line.
Assume:
[ P(Short) = 0.25 ]
[ P(Long) = 0.75 ]
Now consider two travelers, one after the other, and assume their assignments are independent.
For the first traveler:

Short: 0.25
Long: 0.75

For the second traveler, regardless of what happened to the first:

Short: 0.25
Long: 0.75

The joint probabilities are:



First Traveler
Second Traveler
Probability




Short
Short
(0.25 = 0.0625)


Short
Long
(0.25 = 0.1875)


Long
Short
(0.75 = 0.1875)


Long
Long
(0.75 = 0.5625)



The probabilities sum to 1.
The probability that both get the short line is:
[ 0.0625 ]
The probability that exactly one gets the short line is:
[ 0.1875 + 0.1875 = 0.375 ]
The probability that at least one gets the short line is:
[ 1 - P(Long, Long) ]
[ 1 - 0.5625 = 0.4375 ]

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
39. Probability Trees with Conditional Probabilities
Now suppose the process is not independent.
Imagine that if the first traveler gets the short line, the system becomes less likely to send the next traveler to the short line.
For the first traveler:
[ P(Short) = 0.25 ]
[ P(Long) = 0.75 ]
If the first traveler gets the short line:
[ P(Short First Short) = 0.10 ]
[ P(Long First Short) = 0.90 ]
If the first traveler gets the long line:
[ P(Short First Long) = 0.25 ]
[ P(Long First Long) = 0.75 ]
Now the second-stage probabilities depend on what happened in the first stage.
The joint probabilities are:



First Traveler
Second Traveler
Probability




Short
Short
(0.25 = 0.025)


Short
Long
(0.25 = 0.225)


Long
Short
(0.75 = 0.1875)


Long
Long
(0.75 = 0.5625)



This tree shows dependence. The probabilities facing the second traveler change depending on what happened to the first traveler.
In a probability tree:

Branch probabilities are conditional probabilities when they depend on previous outcomes.
Final path probabilities are joint probabilities.
Probabilities leaving each node must sum to 1.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
section: 40. Probability Tree Example: Coin and Die
text:
40. Probability Tree Example: Coin and Die
Suppose we use the following strange procedure.
First, flip a fair coin.

If the coin lands tails, report the number 3.
If the coin lands heads, roll a fair die and report the die result.

What is the probability of hearing each number?
If the coin lands tails, which happens with probability (1/2), you hear 3.
If the coin lands heads, which happens with probability (1/2), then each die result occurs with probability (1/6).
So for numbers 1, 2, 4, 5, and 6:
[ P() = = ]
For 3, there are two paths:

Tails, then report 3:

[ ]

Heads, then roll 3:

[ = ]
So:
[ P() = + ]
[ P() = + ]
[ P() = ]
If you hear the number 2, you know the coin must have landed heads. It is impossible to hear 2 after tails.
If you hear 3, the coin could have landed tails or heads. Probability trees help us keep track of these possibilities.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
41. Cross-Tabulation
A contingency table, also called a cross-tabulation or cross-tab, displays the joint distribution of two categorical variables.
Contingency tables are useful for asking whether the distribution of one variable changes across categories of another variable.
For example, we might ask:

Does religiosity affect support for federal spending on public schools?

Suppose one survey question asks whether federal spending on public schools should:

decrease
stay the same
increase

Another question asks whether religion is an important part of the respondent’s life:

yes
no

A contingency table can show whether opinions about school spending differ between those who say religion is important and those who say it is not.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
42. Dependent and Independent Variables
When building a contingency table, it is often helpful to place the dependent variable in the rows and the independent variable in the columns.
The dependent variable is the outcome we want to explain.
The independent variable is the variable we think might affect the outcome.
For example:

Dependent variable: opinion about federal school spending
Independent variable: whether religion is important

This arrangement makes it easier to compare column percentages side by side.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
section: 43. Frequencies, Row Percentages, Column Percentages, and Cell Percentages
text:
43. Frequencies, Row Percentages, Column Percentages, and Cell Percentages
A contingency table can display several kinds of information.
Frequencies
Raw counts show how many cases fall into each combination of categories.
Row Percentages
Row percentages add across each row to 100 percent. They show how cases in a row are distributed across columns.
Column Percentages
Column percentages add down each column to 100 percent. They show how cases in a column are distributed across rows.
Cell Percentages
Cell percentages show the percentage of all cases that fall into each cell. These are similar to joint probabilities.
For example, if 99 out of 4,230 respondents both say religion is not important and support decreasing school spending, then the cell percentage is:
[ % ]
The corresponding joint probability is:
[ 0.0234 ]

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
44. Example: Religion and School Spending
Suppose we produce a contingency table with column percentages.








Federal Spending on Public Schools
Religion Not Important
Religion Important




Decrease
6.77%
8.09%


Keep the same
20.93%
24.89%


Increase
72.30%
67.02%


Total
100.00%
100.00%



Because the percentages add down each column, we can compare the two groups side by side.
Among people who say religion is not important, 72.30 percent support increasing federal spending on public schools.
Among people who say religion is important, 67.02 percent support increasing spending.
The difference is about five percentage points. That suggests a modest relationship: people who say religion is important may be somewhat less likely to support increasing federal school spending.
But this difference is not enormous. Later, we will learn statistical tests, such as the chi-square test, that help us evaluate whether differences like this are large enough to provide strong evidence of a relationship in the population.

title: Chapter 4: Probability
text:
45. Contingency Tables and Conditional Probabilities
Column percentages are conditional probabilities.
For example:
[ P(Increase Religion Not Important) = 0.723 ]
[ P(Increase Religion Important) = 0.670 ]
If the variables were perfectly independent, these conditional probabilities would be the same across columns.
If they differ, the variables may be dependent.
A contingency table is therefore a practical tool for studying whether two categorical variables are related.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
1. What Is a Significance Test?
A significance test is a procedure for evaluating whether sample evidence is strong enough to reject a hypothesis about a population parameter.
The population parameter might be:

a population mean, ()
a population proportion, ()
a difference between two means
a difference between two proportions
a relationship between variables
a regression coefficient

For now, we focus on one-sample tests involving either one mean or one proportion.
Examples of hypotheses include:

Fewer than 40 percent of Americans approve of a president’s performance.
The average American watches 2.5 hours of television per day.
A vaccine education program changes vaccination rates.
A new gasoline blend reduces average air pollution.
Support for raising the minimum wage differs by party identification.

In each case, we use sample data to evaluate a claim about the population.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
2. The General Approach
The general approach to significance testing is:

Make a hypothesis about a population parameter.
Use sample data to estimate that parameter.
Use sampling distribution theory to determine how unusual the sample statistic would be if the hypothesis were true.
Reject the hypothesis if the sample statistic is too unlikely under that hypothesis.

The phrase “too unlikely” is important. Sample statistics vary because of random sampling error. A sample mean or sample proportion will almost never equal the population parameter exactly. Some difference is expected.
The question is whether the difference is large enough that random sampling error alone becomes an implausible explanation.
If the sample statistic is close to the hypothesized value, we do not reject the hypothesis.
If the sample statistic is far from the hypothesized value, we may reject the hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
3. Relationships Between Variables
Significance tests become especially useful when we evaluate relationships between variables.
For example, we might hypothesize that:

approval of a president is lower among women than among men
people with more education watch less television
parents who receive vaccine education are more likely to vaccinate their children
a new air quality regulation reduces average pollution
a job training program increases employment rates

In these cases, we usually begin by assuming there is no relationship or no effect. That assumption is the null hypothesis.
Only if the data provide strong enough evidence do we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that the relationship or effect is statistically significant.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
4. The Null Hypothesis
The null hypothesis is the baseline assumption we test against.
It is written:
[ H_0 ]
This is pronounced “H-zero,” not “H-oh.”
The null hypothesis usually states that nothing has changed, there is no effect, or there is no relationship.
For example:

A program has no effect.
A population mean is equal to a particular value.
A population proportion is equal to a particular value.
Two groups have the same mean.
Two variables are independent.

The null hypothesis is not necessarily what we believe. In fact, researchers often hope to reject it. But we begin by assuming it is true, and then ask whether the data provide enough evidence to reject it.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
5. The Alternative Hypothesis
The alternative hypothesis is the hypothesis we consider if the null hypothesis is rejected.
It is written:
[ H_A ]
The alternative hypothesis usually represents the researcher’s substantive expectation.
For example, suppose we are evaluating a vaccine education program. The null hypothesis might be:
[ H_0: = 0.722 ]
meaning the vaccination rate among families receiving the program is 72.2 percent, the same as the baseline rate.
The alternative hypothesis might be:
[ H_A: ]
meaning the program changes the vaccination rate.
The alternative could reflect an increase or a decrease. If the program encourages vaccination, the rate may rise. If the program backfires, the rate may fall.
This is a two-sided alternative because it allows for change in either direction.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
6. Example: Vaccine Education Program
Suppose public health researchers want to evaluate a vaccine education program.
The goal is to increase childhood vaccination rates. However, previous research suggests that some attempts to persuade people about vaccination can backfire, especially if they seem judgmental or paternalistic. Therefore, the program might increase vaccination, decrease vaccination, or have no effect.
Suppose national records show that 72.2 percent of American children aged 19 to 35 months receive the full recommended series of vaccinations.
We use that as the baseline rate:
[ _0 = 0.722 ]
The hypotheses are:
[ H_0: = 0.722 ]
[ H_A: ]
The null hypothesis says the program has no effect.
The alternative hypothesis says the program changes the vaccination rate.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
7. Significance Level
The significance level is the threshold for rejecting the null hypothesis.
It is represented by the Greek letter alpha:
[ ]
Alpha is the probability of making a type I error, which means rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true.
Common values of alpha include:
[ = 0.10 ]
[ = 0.05 ]
[ = 0.01 ]
The most common choice is:
[ = 0.05 ]
This means we are willing to tolerate a 5 percent probability of rejecting a true null hypothesis.
Equivalently, we require 95 percent confidence before rejecting the null hypothesis.
The choice of alpha depends on context. If the consequences of a false positive are serious, we may require a smaller alpha, such as 0.01 or even lower. For example, when testing safety standards for bridges, airplanes, or medical treatments, we may want very strong evidence before rejecting the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
8. Alpha and Critical Values
Alpha defines the critical value of the test statistic.
A critical value is the threshold the test statistic must cross for us to reject the null hypothesis.
For a two-tailed test using the standard normal distribution:

If (= 0.05), then the critical values are:

[ z = -1.96 ]
and:
[ z = 1.96 ]
This is because 95 percent of the standard normal distribution lies between -1.96 and 1.96, leaving 2.5 percent in each tail.
If our test statistic falls beyond either critical value, we reject the null hypothesis.
For example, if:
[ z = 2.47 ]
then:
[ |z| > 1.96 ]
so we reject the null hypothesis at the 0.05 significance level.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
9. Test Statistics
A test statistic measures how far the sample statistic is from the null-hypothesized value in standard error units.
The general form is:
[ = {} ]
For a proportion, we usually use a z-statistic:
[ z = {_{}} ]
For a mean, we usually use a t-statistic:
[ t = {_{{Y}}} ]
The test statistic tells us how many standard errors away our sample statistic is from the null hypothesis.
A test statistic near zero means the sample statistic is close to the null hypothesis.
A large positive or negative test statistic means the sample statistic is far from the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
10. The p-Value
The p-value is one of the most important and most commonly misunderstood concepts in statistics.
The p-value is the probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of observing a sample statistic as far from the null hypothesis as the one we observed, or farther.
More formally:

The p-value is the probability of obtaining a result at least as extreme as the observed result, conditional on the null hypothesis being true.

The p-value is a conditional probability.
The condition is:
[ H_0 ]
A small p-value means the observed result would be unlikely if the null hypothesis were true.
A large p-value means the observed result is plausible under the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
11. p-Values and Alpha
The p-value is compared to alpha.
Decision rule:
[ p < H_0 ]
[ p H_0 ]
This is equivalent to comparing the test statistic to the critical value.
For a two-tailed z-test with (= 0.05):
[ |z| > 1.96 H_0 ]
At exactly the same point where the test statistic crosses the critical value, the p-value falls below alpha.
So these two decision rules are equivalent:

Reject if the test statistic exceeds the critical value in absolute value.
Reject if the p-value is less than alpha.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
12. p-Value Example
Suppose we conduct a two-tailed z-test and obtain:
[ z = 1.50 ]
The area in the upper tail beyond 1.50 is about 0.0668. Because this is a two-tailed test, we also include the lower tail beyond -1.50.
So:
[ p = 2(0.0668) = 0.1336 ]
The p-value is:
[ p = 0.1336 ]
If (= 0.05), then:
[ 0.1336 > 0.05 ]
So we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
Now suppose:
[ z = 2.10 ]
The two-tailed p-value is about:
[ p = 0.0358 ]
If (= 0.05), then:
[ 0.0358 < 0.05 ]
So we reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
13. Vaccine Education Example
Return to the vaccine education program.
The baseline vaccination rate is:
[ _0 = 0.722 ]
The null and alternative hypotheses are:
[ H_0: = 0.722 ]
[ H_A: ]
Suppose we randomly select 400 families to receive the vaccine education program. Three years later, we observe whether their children received the full course of vaccinations.
The sample proportion vaccinated is:
[ = 0.751 ]
The sample size is:
[ n = 400 ]
We want to know whether 0.751 is far enough from 0.722 to reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
14. Step 1: Choose Alpha
Use:
[ = 0.05 ]
This is a two-tailed test, so the critical values are:
[ z = -1.96 ]
and:
[ z = 1.96 ]
We will reject the null hypothesis if:
[ |z| > 1.96 ]

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 15. Step 2: Calculate the Standard Error Under the Null
text:
15. Step 2: Calculate the Standard Error Under the Null
For a significance test involving a proportion, we calculate the standard error using the null-hypothesized proportion.
That is important.
For a confidence interval, we use ().
For a significance test, we use (_0), because we assume the null hypothesis is true.
The standard error is:
[ _{} = ]
Substitute values:
[ _{} = ]

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
16. Step 3: Calculate the z-Statistic
Use:
[ z = {_{}} ]
Substitute values:
[ z = ]
[ z = ]
[ z ]
The sample proportion is 1.29 standard errors above the null-hypothesized value.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 17. Step 4: Compare to the Critical Value
text:
17. Step 4: Compare to the Critical Value
The critical value is:
[ 1.96 ]
Our test statistic is:
[ z = 1.29 ]
Since:
[ |1.29| < 1.96 ]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
The observed vaccination rate is higher than the baseline rate, but not far enough away to be statistically significant at the 0.05 level.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
18. Step 5: Calculate the p-Value
Using a z-table, the upper-tail area beyond:
[ z = 1.29 ]
is:
[ 0.0985 ]
Because this is a two-tailed test, double it:
[ p = 2(0.0985) ]
[ p = 0.197 ]
So:
[ p = 0.197 ]
Since:
[ 0.197 > 0.05 ]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
Interpretation:

If the true vaccination rate were 0.722, there would be about a 19.7 percent probability of observing a sample proportion as far from 0.722 as 0.751, or farther, in a sample of 400.

That probability is too large for us to reject the null hypothesis at the 0.05 level.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
19. The Role of Sample Size
Now suppose the sample size were:
[ n = 1000 ]
while the sample proportion remained:
[ = 0.751 ]
The standard error becomes:
[ _{} = ]
[ ]
Now the z-statistic is:
[ z = ]
[ z ]
Now:
[ |2.04| > 1.96 ]
So we reject the null hypothesis at the 0.05 level.
The sample proportion did not change. The difference from the null hypothesis did not change. What changed was the sample size.
Larger samples reduce the standard error, making it easier to detect smaller differences.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
20. Standard Procedure
When conducting a significance test, follow these steps.
Step 1: Check Assumptions
Ask:

Is the sample random or plausibly representative?
Are there obvious sources of selection bias or response bias?
What kind of variable do we have?
Is this a mean, a proportion, or something else?
Is the sample size large enough for the test we want to use?
If testing a mean with (n < 30), can we assume the population is normal?
If testing a proportion, is the sample large enough for the normal approximation?

In this course, the most important assumption checks will usually involve sample size and choosing the correct test statistic.
Step 2: State the Hypotheses
State:
[ H_0 ]
and:
[ H_A ]
Be sure hypotheses are about population parameters, not sample statistics.
Step 3: Calculate the Test Statistic
Use the appropriate formula.
For a proportion:
[ z = {} ]
For a mean:
[ t = {s/} ]
Step 4: Compare to the Critical Value
Use alpha to determine the critical value.
For a two-tailed z-test with:
[ = 0.05 ]
the critical value is:
[ 1.96 ]
Reject if:
[ |z| > 1.96 ]
For t-tests, use the t-table with the appropriate degrees of freedom.
Step 5: Calculate the p-Value
Find the probability of observing a test statistic as extreme as yours, or more extreme, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
For two-tailed tests, remember to include both tails.
Step 6: State the Conclusion
Say either:

Reject the null hypothesis.
Fail to reject the null hypothesis.

Do not say “accept the null hypothesis.”

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
21. Why We Need the t-Distribution
When testing hypotheses about means, we usually use the t-distribution.
The t-distribution is similar to the standard normal distribution. It is:

symmetric
bell-shaped
centered at zero
has declining probability as we move away from the center

But the t-distribution has more area in the tails, especially when sample size is small.
Why?
Because when we test a mean, we usually do not know the population standard deviation (). We estimate it using the sample standard deviation (s). That introduces additional uncertainty.
The t-distribution accounts for that uncertainty.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
22. Sample Size and the t-Distribution
The shape of the t-distribution depends on degrees of freedom.
For a one-sample test of a mean:
[ df = n - 1 ]
When the sample size is small, the t-distribution has thick tails. This makes it harder to reject the null hypothesis because we need a larger test statistic.
As the sample size increases, the t-distribution approaches the standard normal distribution.
For large samples, the t and z distributions are nearly identical.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 23. When to Use t Instead of z
text:
23. When to Use t Instead of z
For means, think t.
Use the t-distribution when testing a hypothesis about a mean, especially when () is unknown.
If the sample size is large, t and z will be nearly identical. But using t is generally safer.
If (n < 30), using t requires that the population distribution be approximately normal.
The t-distribution does not fix a non-normal population when the sample is small.
That is important.
If the sample size is small and the population is skewed, the sampling distribution may not be approximately normal, and the usual t-test may not be appropriate.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
24. Reading the t-Table
A t-table differs from a z-table.
In many t-tables:

rows correspond to degrees of freedom
columns correspond to tail areas or confidence levels
cells contain t critical values

For a 95 percent confidence interval or a two-tailed test with (= 0.05), each tail contains:
[ 0.025 ]
So use the column for 0.025 in the upper tail.
If:
[ df = 13 ]
then the critical value for a two-tailed test with (= 0.05) is approximately:
[ t^* = 2.16 ]
This is larger than 1.96 because the t-distribution has heavier tails.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
25. Air Quality Example
Suppose a city has historically had an average Air Quality Index of:
[ _0 = 175 ]
Higher AQI values are worse. The city introduces a new gasoline blend intended to reduce pollution.
After the new gasoline blend is introduced, researchers observe air quality over 14 days.
The sample mean AQI is:
[ {Y} = 155 ]
The sample standard deviation is:
[ s = 30 ]
The sample size is:
[ n = 14 ]
We want to test whether the average AQI has changed.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
26. Step 1: Check Assumptions
We are testing a mean, so we use a t-test.
The sample size is:
[ n = 14 ]
which is less than 30.
Therefore, to use the t-test, we need to assume that the population distribution of daily AQI values is approximately normal.
This is not an assumption about the sampling distribution. It is an assumption about the population distribution.
If the population distribution is not normal and (n < 30), the t-test may not be appropriate.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
27. Step 2: State the Hypotheses
The null hypothesis is that the mean AQI is unchanged:
[ H_0: = 175 ]
The alternative hypothesis is that the mean AQI has changed:
[ H_A: ]
This is a two-tailed test.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
28. Step 3: Calculate the t-Statistic
The formula is:
[ t = {s/} ]
Substitute values:
[ t = {30/} ]
First calculate the standard error:
[ ]
Now calculate:
[ t = ]
[ t ]
The sample mean is 2.49 standard errors below the null-hypothesized mean.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 29. Step 4: Compare to the Critical Value
text:
29. Step 4: Compare to the Critical Value
Degrees of freedom are:
[ df = n - 1 = 14 - 1 = 13 ]
For a two-tailed test with:
[ = 0.05 ]
and:
[ df = 13 ]
the critical value is approximately:
[ t^* = 2.16 ]
We compare the absolute value of the test statistic to the critical value:
[ |-2.49| = 2.49 ]
Since:
[ 2.49 > 2.16 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
30. Step 5: Calculate the p-Value
Using a t-table, we look along the row for:
[ df = 13 ]
Our test statistic has magnitude:
[ 2.49 ]
In the t-table, 2.49 falls between the critical values associated with upper-tail areas of 0.025 and 0.010.
Because this is a two-tailed test, double those tail areas.
So the p-value is between:
[ 2(0.010) = 0.020 ]
and:
[ 2(0.025) = 0.050 ]
Thus:
[ 0.020 < p < 0.050 ]
Using software, the p-value is approximately:
[ p = 0.027 ]
Since:
[ 0.027 < 0.05 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
31. Step 6: State the Conclusion
We reject the null hypothesis at the 0.05 level.
There is statistically significant evidence that the average AQI changed after the new gasoline blend was introduced.
Because the sample mean is lower than 175, the evidence is consistent with the gasoline blend reducing AQI.
A careful conclusion might be:

Assuming the population distribution of AQI is approximately normal, the sample provides statistically significant evidence at the 0.05 level that the mean AQI differs from 175. Because the sample mean is 155, the evidence suggests that AQI decreased after the gasoline blend was introduced.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
32. Two-Tailed Versus One-Tailed Tests
Most significance tests are two-tailed.
A two-tailed test rejects the null hypothesis if the sample statistic is far enough from the null hypothesis in either direction.
For example:
[ H_0: = 175 ]
[ H_A: ]
This rejects if the mean is much lower or much higher than 175.
A one-tailed test rejects the null hypothesis only in one direction.
For example, if we specifically predict that AQI decreased, we might write:
[ H_0: ]
[ H_A: < 175 ]
Now we reject only if the sample mean is sufficiently below 175.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
33. Alpha in a One-Tailed Test
In a two-tailed test with:
[ = 0.05 ]
alpha is split between the two tails:
[ 0.025 ]
in each tail.
In a one-tailed test, all of alpha goes into one tail.
So if:
[ = 0.05 ]
then the rejection region is 5 percent in one tail.
For a z-test, the one-tailed critical value for (= 0.05) is about:
[ 1.645 ]
rather than:
[ 1.96 ]
This makes it easier to reject the null hypothesis in the predicted direction.
Because of that, one-tailed tests should be used cautiously and only when the direction of the alternative hypothesis is clearly justified in advance.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 34. When to Use a One-Tailed Test
text:
34. When to Use a One-Tailed Test
A one-tailed test may be appropriate when:

theory strongly predicts a direction
only one direction is substantively meaningful
the researcher specifies the direction before seeing the data

For example:
[ H_A: < 175 ]
would be appropriate if the only meaningful claim is that the policy reduced AQI.
However, two-tailed tests are standard. If a researcher uses a one-tailed test, they should explain why.
One-tailed tests can be tempting because they make it easier to find statistical significance. That is precisely why they should not be chosen after looking at the data.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
35. Environmental Spending Example
Suppose a survey asks whether federal spending on the environment should be:

increased
kept about the same
decreased

We focus on the proportion who say spending should be increased.
Suppose:
[ n = 5884 ]
and:
[ = 0.41 ]
We want to test whether the population proportion differs from 0.40.
The hypotheses are:
[ H_0: = 0.40 ]
[ H_A: ]
Use:
[ = 0.05 ]

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
36. Check Assumptions
This is a proportion test, so we need enough cases in both categories.
The two categories are:

supports increasing spending
does not support increasing spending

The sample is large:
[ n = 5884 ]
The null-hypothesized proportion is:
[ _0 = 0.40 ]
A rule of thumb is:
[ n (_0, 1-_0) > 10 ]
Here:
[ 5884 (0.40, 0.60) ]
[ = 5884(0.40) ]
[ = 2353.6 ]
This is far greater than 10, so the z-test is appropriate.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
37. Calculate the z-Statistic
The standard error under the null is:
[ _{} = ]
Substitute values:
[ _{} = ]

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
38. Calculate the p-Value
Using a z-table:
[ P(Z > 1.67) = 0.0475 ]
Because this is a two-tailed test:
[ p = 2(0.0475) ]
[ p = 0.095 ]
Since:
[ 0.095 > 0.05 ]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
Conclusion:

The sample does not provide statistically significant evidence at the 0.05 level that the population proportion differs from 0.40.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 39. Why z Does Not Always Work for Proportions
text:
39. Why z Does Not Always Work for Proportions
Proportions are based on counts.
If we toss a coin five times, the possible proportions of heads are:
[ 0, , , , , 1 ]
These are discrete values.
With very small samples, the distribution of possible sample proportions does not look like a smooth normal curve. It looks like a set of distinct probabilities.
The z-distribution is continuous and symmetric. It is only an approximation to the binomial distribution.
When the sample is large, the approximation works well. When the sample is small, it may not.
In small-sample proportion tests, use the binomial distribution.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
40. Binomial Test Logic
The binomial distribution gives the exact probability of observing (x) successes in (n) trials if the true probability of success is ():
[ P(X=x) = x(1-){n-x} ]
For a binomial significance test, we do not need to convert the sample result into a z-statistic.
Instead, we calculate the exact probability of getting an outcome as extreme as the observed outcome, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
That probability is the p-value.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
41. Example: Hiring Discrimination
Suppose a workplace discrimination case concerns hiring decisions.
Assume the applicant pool is 50 percent white and 50 percent nonwhite, and assume all applicants are similarly qualified.
If hiring is race-neutral, then the probability that a hired applicant is white is:
[ _0 = 0.50 ]
Suppose 10 people are hired.
If hiring is race-neutral, the number of white hires follows a binomial distribution:
[ X Binomial(n=10, ) ]
Now suppose the observed hiring outcome is extremely imbalanced, such as 9 or 10 white hires, or 0 or 1 white hires.
For a two-tailed test, we include both extremes.
The probability of getting 0 or 1 white hires, or 9 or 10 white hires, under the null hypothesis, is approximately:
[ p = 0.022 ]
Since:
[ 0.022 < 0.05 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.
Even with a sample size of only 10, the result is unlikely under race-neutral hiring.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
42. Why the Binomial Is Exact
The binomial distribution is exact for dichotomous outcomes under the conditions of the binomial model.
It does not require the sample size to be large.
It does not approximate a continuous curve.
It calculates the exact probability of each possible count.
The z-test is convenient for large samples, but the binomial test is more accurate, especially when:

sample size is small
the hypothesized proportion is near 0 or 1
one category has very few cases

Software can easily calculate exact binomial probabilities, even when hand calculations would be tedious.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
43. Two Kinds of Error
A significance test can lead to two types of error.
Type I Error
A type I error occurs when we reject the null hypothesis even though it is true.
This is a false positive.
The probability of a type I error is:
[ ]
Type II Error
A type II error occurs when we fail to reject the null hypothesis even though it is false.
This is a false negative.
The probability of a type II error is:
[ ]

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
44. Error Table



Reality
Reject (H_0)
Fail to Reject (H_0)




(H_0) is true
Type I error
Correct decision


(H_0) is false
Correct decision
Type II error



A type I error is rejecting a true null hypothesis.
A type II error is failing to reject a false null hypothesis.
One way to remember:

Type I error: false positive
Type II error: false negative

The story of “the boy who cried wolf” contains both:

The boy says there is a wolf when there is no wolf: type I error.
Later, there is a wolf, but no one believes him: type II error.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
45. Reducing Type I Error
To reduce the probability of a type I error, lower alpha.
For example, use:
[ = 0.01 ]
instead of:
[ = 0.05 ]
This makes it harder to reject the null hypothesis.
However, lowering alpha increases the probability of a type II error, because it becomes harder to reject even a false null hypothesis.
There is a tradeoff.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
46. Reducing Type II Error
To reduce the probability of a type II error, increase sample size.
Larger samples reduce the standard error, making it easier to detect real differences.
If the null hypothesis is false, a larger sample makes the sample statistic more likely to cluster around the true value rather than around the null-hypothesized value. This makes rejecting the false null hypothesis more likely.
The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis is called statistical power.
Power is:
[ 1 - ]
Increasing sample size increases power.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 47. Sample Size and Type I Error
text:
47. Sample Size and Type I Error
Increasing sample size does not reduce the type I error rate.
This may seem counterintuitive.
The type I error rate is set by alpha. If (= 0.05), then under the scenario that the null hypothesis is true, we reject 5 percent of the time.
Larger samples make the sampling distribution narrower, but the rejection threshold is defined in standard error units. The type I error rate remains 0.05 because alpha defines the probability of falling in the rejection region.
Sample size affects type II error and power, not the type I error rate.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
48. Statistical Significance Is Probabilistic
A statistically significant result is not definitive proof.
It means the observed result would be unlikely under the null hypothesis.
But unlikely events happen.
If (= 0.05), then even when the null hypothesis is true, about 5 percent of tests will reject it by chance alone.
That is the meaning of type I error.
Statistical significance is evidence, not certainty.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
49. Multiple Testing and False Positives
If researchers conduct many tests, some will be statistically significant by chance alone.
Suppose the null hypothesis is true for 20 different tests, and each test uses:
[ = 0.05 ]
On average, about 1 of the 20 tests will be statistically significant just by chance.
This creates a multiple testing problem.
A famous illustration involves jellybeans and acne. Suppose researchers test whether jellybeans cause acne and find no relationship. Then they test many colors of jellybeans separately: purple, brown, pink, blue, teal, red, yellow, green, and so on. Eventually, one color produces (p < 0.05).
A headline appears:

Green jellybeans cause acne.

But the result may simply be a false positive caused by repeated testing.
This is why researchers should be cautious when interpreting isolated significant findings, especially when many tests were conducted.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
text:
50. Publication Bias
Statistically significant results are more likely to be published than null results.
This creates publication bias.
If journals and media outlets focus mainly on significant findings, the published literature may overrepresent false positives and exaggerated effects.
Null results are still valuable. If a vaccine education program has no measurable effect, that is important information. If a policy intervention does not work, policymakers should know that.
But null findings often receive less attention because they are less exciting.
This is a problem for science and public policy.

title: Chapter 8: Significance Tests
section: 51. Do Not Accept the Null Hypothesis
text:
51. Do Not Accept the Null Hypothesis
When a significance test does not reject the null hypothesis, we say:

Fail to reject the null hypothesis.

We do not say:

Accept the null hypothesis.

Why?
Because failing to reject means the data are consistent with the null hypothesis. But they may also be consistent with many nearby alternatives.
A non-significant result does not prove the null hypothesis is true. It simply means we do not have enough evidence to reject it.
The correct language is:

Reject (H_0)
Fail to reject (H_0)

Not:

Accept (H_0)

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 1. What Is a Mean Comparison Table?
text:
1. What Is a Mean Comparison Table?
A mean comparison table displays the mean of an interval-level variable across categories of another variable.
The variable whose mean we calculate is usually the outcome variable, or dependent variable. It must be measured at the interval or ratio level.
The variable that creates the groups is categorical. It may be nominal or ordinal.
For example, suppose we want to know whether students whose teachers have master’s degrees score higher on standardized tests than students whose teachers do not have master’s degrees.
The outcome variable is:

student test score

The grouping variable is:

teacher has a master’s degree
teacher does not have a master’s degree

A mean comparison table would show the mean test score in each group.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
2. General Structure
A simple mean comparison table might look like this:









Category of (X)
Mean of (Y)
Number of Cases
Percent of Sample




Category 1
({Y}_1)
(n_1)
%


Category 2
({Y}_2)
(n_2)
%


Category 3
({Y}_3)
(n_3)
%


Total
({Y})
(n)
100%



The full sample is divided into subsamples based on the categories of (X). Within each subsample, we calculate the mean of (Y).
The table provides a descriptive comparison. It tells us whether the sample means differ across groups.
However, the table alone does not tell us whether those differences are statistically significant.
For that, we need a significance test.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 3. Example: Racial Representation and Arrest Disparities
text:
3. Example: Racial Representation and Arrest Disparities
Consider a study by political scientist Laurel Eckhouse on racial disparities in arrests for minor offenses.
The research question is whether racial representation on city councils is associated with racial disparities in policing.
In the United States, policing is largely local. City governments often have authority over police departments, budgets, oversight, and local ordinances. Therefore, the racial composition of a city council may plausibly affect policing practices.
The independent variable is city council composition:

majority white city council
majority persons of color city council

The dependent variable is a measure of racial disparity in arrests.
Eckhouse focuses on arrests for minor offenses, where police officers often have discretion. Examples might include loitering, being in a park after hours, disorderly conduct, or other low-level offenses. Discretion is important because disparities may be more likely to appear when officers have more room to decide whether to arrest, warn, or ignore.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
4. Measuring Arrest Disparities
A simple count of arrests would not be sufficient because cities differ in population size.
Instead, Eckhouse uses an arrest rate ratio:
[ = {} ]
This ratio compares the arrest rate for Black residents to the arrest rate for white residents.
Interpretation:

A value of 1 means equal arrest rates.
A value of 2 means the arrest rate for Black residents is twice the arrest rate for white residents.
A value above 1 indicates higher arrest rates for Black residents.
A value below 1 would indicate lower arrest rates for Black residents.

This measure allows comparison across cities of different sizes.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 5. Mean Comparison Table: City Council Composition
text:
5. Mean Comparison Table: City Council Composition
The mean comparison table from the study is approximately:



City Council Composition
Mean Arrest Rate Ratio
Number of Cities




Majority white city council
2.63
1,094


Majority persons of color city council
1.88
94


Overall
2.57
1,188



The mean arrest rate ratio is 2.63 in cities with majority white city councils. That means Black residents are arrested for minor offenses at about 2.6 times the rate of white residents.
In cities with majority persons of color city councils, the mean arrest rate ratio is 1.88.
The observed difference is:
[ 2.63 - 1.88 = 0.75 ]
This descriptive table suggests that racial disparities in arrests are lower in cities with majority persons of color city councils.
But is that difference statistically significant?
And even if it is statistically significant, is it causal?
Those are separate questions.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 6. The Difference of Means as a Statistic
text:
6. The Difference of Means as a Statistic
When we compare two means, we estimate a new statistic:
[ {Y}_2 - {Y}_1 ]
This statistic estimates the difference between two population means:
[ _2 - _1 ]
The null hypothesis is usually that there is no difference:
[ H_0: _2 - _1 = 0 ]
The alternative hypothesis is:
[ H_A: _2 - _1 ]
If the observed difference is large relative to its standard error, we reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
7. Why the Standard Error Changes
When we tested one mean, we compared a sample mean to a fixed null-hypothesized value.
Now we compare two sample means. Both are estimated with uncertainty.
Each sample mean has its own standard error:
[ SE_{{Y}_1} ]
and:
[ SE_{{Y}_2} ]
Because both means are uncertain, the difference between them is also uncertain.
The standard error for the difference must incorporate uncertainty from both samples.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
8. Independent and Dependent Samples
Before calculating the standard error, we must determine whether the samples are independent or dependent.
Independent Samples
Samples are independent when the observations in one group are not explicitly matched with observations in the other group.
Examples:

Democrats versus Republicans
treatment group versus control group in a randomized experiment
cities with majority white city councils versus cities with majority persons of color city councils
democracies versus non-democracies
students in one program versus students not in the program

Independent samples are the default in most two-sample tests.
Dependent Samples
Samples are dependent when observations are explicitly paired.
Examples:

the same people measured before and after an intervention
twins split between treatment and control
matched pairs of similar cases
households measured before and after lead pipe replacement

Dependent samples require a different test because the observations are connected.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 9. Standard Error for the Difference of Means: Independent Samples
text:
9. Standard Error for the Difference of Means: Independent Samples
For independent samples, the standard error of the difference between two means is:
[ SE_{{Y}_2-{Y}_1} = ]
For a single mean:
[ SE_{{Y}} = ]
So for two independent means:
[ SE_{{Y}_2-{Y}_1} = ]
where:

(s_1) is the standard deviation in sample 1
(s_2) is the standard deviation in sample 2
(n_1) is the size of sample 1
(n_2) is the size of sample 2

This formula takes the variance of each sample mean, adds them together, and then takes the square root.
The structure resembles the Pythagorean theorem: square, add, square root. Statistics has a few geometry flashbacks. It happens.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 10. Confidence Interval for a Difference of Means
text:
10. Confidence Interval for a Difference of Means
The confidence interval for a difference of means is:
[ ({Y}_2 - {Y}1) t^* SE{{Y}_2-{Y}_1} ]
or:
[ ({Y}_2 - {Y}_1) t^* ]
The point estimate is the observed difference between sample means.
The margin of error is:
[ t^* SE_{{Y}_2-{Y}_1} ]
The t critical value depends on degrees of freedom.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 11. Degrees of Freedom for Independent-Sample Mean Tests
text:
11. Degrees of Freedom for Independent-Sample Mean Tests
Degrees of freedom are more complicated for two independent means than for one mean.
For one mean:
[ df = n - 1 ]
For two independent means without assuming equal variances, the exact degrees of freedom are given by the Satterthwaite approximation, which is complicated:
[ df = { + } ]
Software uses this formula.
By hand, we often use a conservative approximation:
[ df = (n_1-1, n_2-1) ]
That is, take the smaller sample size and subtract 1.
This underestimates the true degrees of freedom, making the test slightly more conservative. If we can reject the null hypothesis using this conservative approximation, we would also reject using the more exact degrees of freedom.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 12. Example: City Councils and Arrest Disparities
text:
12. Example: City Councils and Arrest Disparities
Return to the arrest disparity example.

The observed difference is:
[ 2.63 - 1.88 = 0.75 ]
The standard error for the difference is:
[ SE = ]
[ SE ]
So the estimated difference is:
[ 0.75 ]
with a standard error of about:
[ 0.136 ]
That difference is large relative to the standard error.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
13. Confidence Interval for the Difference
Suppose software gives a t critical value of approximately:
[ t^* = 1.973 ]
Then the 95 percent confidence interval is:
[ 0.75 (0.136) ]
[ 0.75 ]
Lower bound:
[ 0.75 - 0.268 = 0.482 ]
Upper bound:
[ 0.75 + 0.268 = 1.018 ]
So the confidence interval is approximately:
[ 0.48 1.02 ]
Interpretation:

Across repeated samples, 95 percent of confidence intervals constructed using this procedure would contain the true difference in mean arrest rate ratios. In this sample, the interval ranges from about 0.48 to 1.02.

Because the interval does not include 0, we expect to reject the null hypothesis of no difference.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 14. Significance Test for the Difference of Means
text:
14. Significance Test for the Difference of Means
The null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: _2 - _1 = 0 ]
The alternative hypothesis is:
[ H_A: _2 - _1 ]
The t-statistic is:
[ t = {SE_{{Y}_2-{Y}_1}} ]
Substitute values:
[ t = ]
[ t ]
This is far larger than a typical 95 percent critical value near 2.
Therefore, we reject the null hypothesis.
There is statistically significant evidence that mean arrest rate ratios differ between cities with majority white city councils and cities with majority persons of color city councils.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
15. Statistical Significance Is Not Causality
The difference is statistically significant.
But statistical significance does not prove causality.
This is an observational study. City council composition was not randomly assigned. Cities with majority white councils may differ from cities with majority persons of color councils in many ways.
Possible confounders include:

city size
racial composition of the population
poverty rates
homelessness rates
policing strategies
police department demographics
local ordinances
neighborhood segregation
council election systems
regional political history
length of time with a racially diverse council

The difference-of-means test establishes that the means differ in the sample, and likely in the broader population represented by the sample. It does not by itself establish that council composition caused the difference.
To strengthen causal claims, researchers may use methods such as statistical controls, regression, matching, panel data, qualitative case studies, or quasi-experimental designs.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
16. Democracies and Infant Mortality
Suppose we want to compare infant mortality in democracies and non-democracies.
Infant mortality is measured as deaths before age one per 1,000 live births.
The data are:









Regime Type
Mean Infant Mortality
Standard Deviation

Democracy

The observed difference is:
[ 40.0 - 20.7 = 19.3 ]
Infant mortality is about 19.3 deaths per 1,000 births higher in non-democracies than in democracies.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
17. Checking Assumptions
These are independent samples. Democracies and non-democracies are different groups of countries.
We should not automatically assume equal variances. Infant mortality may vary differently across democracies and non-democracies. In fact, non-democracies may include both very high-performing and very low-performing states, creating greater variation.
So we proceed with the independent-samples difference-of-means test without assuming equal variances.
Using the conservative degrees of freedom approximation:
[ df = (75-1, 101-1) ]
[ df = (74, 100) ]
[ df = 74 ]

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
18. Standard Error for the Difference
Use:
[ SE = ]
Substitute values:
[ SE = ]
[ SE ]
So the observed difference is:
[ 19.3 ]
with a standard error of:
[ 3.92 ]

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
19. Confidence Interval
Using the conservative degrees of freedom and a 95 percent confidence interval, suppose the t critical value is approximately:
[ t^* = 2.00 ]
Then:
[ 19.3 (3.92) ]
[ 19.3 ]

[ 19.3 - 7.84 = 11.46 ]
Upper bound:
[ 19.3 + 7.84 = 27.14 ]
The 95 percent confidence interval is approximately:
[ 11.5 27.1 ]
The interval does not include 0, suggesting a statistically significant difference.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
20. Significance Test
The hypotheses are:
[ H_0: {non-democracy} - {democracy} = 0 ]
[ H_A: {non-democracy} - {democracy} ]
The t-statistic is:
[ t = ]
[ t ]
Since:
[ 4.92 > 2.00 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.
There is statistically significant evidence that mean infant mortality differs between democracies and non-democracies.
Again, causality requires further analysis. Democracies and non-democracies differ in wealth, institutions, health systems, conflict exposure, education, and many other factors.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
21. Comparing Two Proportions
A difference-of-proportions test compares the proportion in one group to the proportion in another group.
Examples:

graduation rates among program participants versus nonparticipants
vaccination rates in treatment and control groups
support for a policy among Democrats and Republicans
employment rates among those who completed training and those who did not

The population parameters are:
[ _1 ]
and:
[ _2 ]
The sample estimates are:
[ _1 ]
and:
[ _2 ]
The null hypothesis is usually:
[ H_0: _2 - _1 = 0 ]
or equivalently:
[ H_0: _1 = _2 ]
The alternative is:
[ H_A: _2 - _1 ]

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 22. Sample Size Rule for Difference of Proportions
text:
22. Sample Size Rule for Difference of Proportions
To use the z-test for a difference of proportions, each of the four cells should have at least about 10 cases.
Why four cells?
Because we have:

group 1, in the category
group 1, not in the category
group 2, in the category
group 2, not in the category

For example, if comparing graduation rates among program participants and nonparticipants, we need at least 10 cases in each of:

participants who graduated
participants who did not graduate
nonparticipants who graduated
nonparticipants who did not graduate

If this rule is not met, the z-test may not be appropriate.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
23. Example: Graduation Program
Suppose we evaluate a program intended to increase high school graduation.

[ _{program} = = 0.892 ]

The observed difference is:

The program group has a graduation rate about 4.9 percentage points higher than the no-program group.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 24. Confidence Interval for a Difference of Proportions
text:
24. Confidence Interval for a Difference of Proportions
For a confidence interval, we use the two sample proportions separately.
The standard error is:
[ SE = ]
The confidence interval is:
[ (_2 - _1) z^* SE ]
Using the example:
[ 0.049 ]
The margin of error is approximately:
[ 0.09 ]
So the confidence interval is:
[ 0.049 ]
Lower bound:
[ 0.049 - 0.09 = -0.041 ]
Upper bound:
[ 0.049 + 0.09 = 0.139 ]
So the 95 percent confidence interval is approximately:
[ -0.04 0.14 ]
Because the interval includes 0, we expect not to reject the null hypothesis of no difference.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 25. Why Can the Confidence Interval Include Negative Values?
text:
25. Why Can the Confidence Interval Include Negative Values?
A difference of proportions can be negative.
If the program group has a lower graduation rate than the no-program group, then:
[ {program} - {no program} < 0 ]
So a confidence interval for a difference can cross 0.
A confidence interval that includes 0 means that no difference is a plausible value. In such a case, we generally fail to reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 26. Significance Test for Difference of Proportions
text:
26. Significance Test for Difference of Proportions
For a significance test, the standard error is calculated differently than for a confidence interval.
Under the null hypothesis:
[ _1 = _2 ]
So we assume there is one common population proportion.
We estimate that common proportion using the pooled sample proportion:
[ = ]
In the graduation example:
[ = ]
[ = ]
[ = 0.864 ]
The null-hypothesis standard error is:
[ SE_0 = ]
Equivalently:
[ SE_0 = ]
Using the example:
[ SE_0 ]

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
27. z-Test for Difference of Proportions
The z-statistic is:
[ z = {SE_0} ]
Using the observed difference:
[ z = ]
[ z ]
The critical value for a two-tailed test with (= 0.05) is:
[ 1.96 ]
Since:
[ 1.04 < 1.96 ]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
There is not statistically significant evidence at the 0.05 level that the program changed graduation rates.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
28. What Are Dependent Samples?
Samples are dependent when cases in one sample are explicitly matched with cases in the other sample.
Common examples include:

before-and-after measurements of the same people
twins split across treatment and control
matched pairs of similar people or places
households measured before and after an intervention

In dependent samples, the two observations are linked.
This changes the analysis.
Instead of comparing two independent group means, we compare differences within pairs.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 29. Turning a Two-Sample Problem into a One-Sample Problem
text:
29. Turning a Two-Sample Problem into a One-Sample Problem
With dependent samples, we create a new variable:
[ Y_d = Y_2 - Y_1 ]
This is the difference for each matched pair.
Then we analyze the mean of those differences:
[ {Y}_d ]
The null hypothesis is:
[ H_0: _d = 0 ]
where (_d) is the population mean of the paired differences.
The alternative is:
[ H_A: _d ]
This is now a one-sample test on the difference variable.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
30. Example: Matched Pairs
Suppose we have four matched pairs:



Pair
(Y_2)
(Y_1)
