The mean of the differences is:
[ {Y}_d = ]
[ {Y}_d = 0.75 ]
The mean of (Y_2) is:
[ {Y}_2 = 4.5 ]
The mean of (Y_1) is:
[ {Y}_1 = 3.75 ]
The difference of the means is:
[ 4.5 - 3.75 = 0.75 ]
So:
[ {Y}_d = {Y}_2 - {Y}_1 ]
The mean of the differences equals the difference of the means.
The advantage of the dependent-samples approach is that it uses the pairing information.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
31. Standard Error for Dependent Samples
For dependent samples, calculate the standard deviation of the difference variable:
[ s_d ]
Then the standard error is:
[ SE_{{Y}_d} = ]
where (n) is the number of matched pairs.
The confidence interval is:
[ {Y}_d t^*() ]
The t-statistic is:
[ t = {s_d/} ]
Degrees of freedom are:
[ df = n - 1 ]
This is simply a one-sample t-test on the paired differences.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
32. Example: Lead Pipe Replacement
Suppose a city replaces lead water supply lines.
Researchers select 250 households. They measure lead levels before the replacement and again after the replacement.
For each household:
[ Y_d = - ]
Suppose the mean difference is:
[ {Y}_d = -3.2 ]
parts per billion. This means lead levels fell by 3.2 parts per billion on average.
Suppose the standard deviation of differences is:
[ s_d = 23.7 ]
and:
[ n = 250 ]
The standard error is:
[ SE = ]
[ SE ]
The t-statistic is:
[ t = ]
[ t ]
Degrees of freedom:
[ df = 249 ]
The critical value for a two-tailed 95 percent test is close to 1.97.
Since:
[ |-2.13| > 1.97 ]
we reject the null hypothesis.
There is statistically significant evidence that lead levels changed. Because the mean difference is negative, the evidence suggests lead levels decreased after pipe replacement.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 33. What Is the Equal Variance Assumption?
text:
33. What Is the Equal Variance Assumption?
In an independent-samples difference-of-means test, we sometimes assume that the two populations have equal variances:
[ _1^2 = _2^2 ]
This is called the equal variance assumption.
If the assumption is true, the two sample variances estimate the same underlying population variance. We can pool them together to estimate one common variance.
This can be useful, especially when sample sizes are small, because pooling uses information more efficiently.
But the assumption can be dangerous if it is wrong.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 34. When Might Equal Variances Be Plausible?
text:
34. When Might Equal Variances Be Plausible?
Equal variances may be plausible when the two groups are very similar except for treatment assignment.
For example, in a randomized experiment, treatment and control groups come from the same sample and are randomly assigned. It may be reasonable to assume that the outcome variable has similar variance in both groups.
Another possible example: a teacher compares test scores from two successive third-grade classes taught in the same school, assuming the student populations are similar from year to year.
But even these examples require caution. An intervention might affect not only the mean but also the variance. For instance, a teaching method might help lower-performing students more than higher-performing students, reducing variation.
The equal variance assumption is theoretical. We do not know population variances, so we cannot truly verify it with certainty.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
35. Why the Assumption Matters
Without assuming equal variances, the standard error is:
[ SE = ]
Degrees of freedom are estimated using the Satterthwaite formula or a conservative approximation.
With equal variances, we pool the variances and use:
[ df = n_1+n_2-2 ]
This gives the maximum possible degrees of freedom.
More degrees of freedom generally means a smaller critical value, making it easier to reject the null hypothesis.
Therefore, if the equal variance assumption is wrong, using it can increase the risk of an incorrect conclusion.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
36. Pooled Standard Deviation
When assuming equal variances, we calculate a pooled variance:
[ s_p^2 = { n_1+n_2-2 } ]
Then take the square root:
[ s_p = ]
This is a weighted average of the two sample variances, weighted by their degrees of freedom.
The larger sample contributes more to the pooled estimate.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
37. Standard Error with Equal Variances
Once we have the pooled standard deviation, the standard error for the difference of means is:
[ SE = s_p ]
The confidence interval is:
[ ({Y}_2-{Y}_1) t^*s_p ]
The t-statistic is:
[ t = {s_p } ]
Degrees of freedom are:
[ df = n_1+n_2-2 ]

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
38. Example: Temperatures in Ann Arbor
Suppose we compare mean temperatures in Ann Arbor in 1900 and 2000.
Imagine the data are:



Year
Mean Temperature
Standard Deviation
Sample Size




1900
47
14
15


2000
50
11
366



The observed difference is:
[ 50 - 47 = 3 ]
Suppose we make the equal variance assumption. That means we assume the variance of daily temperatures is the same in 1900 and 2000.
This may or may not be reasonable. If climate change affects not only average temperature but also variability, the assumption may fail.
Still, for illustration, calculate the pooled standard deviation.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
39. Pooled Standard Deviation Calculation
Use:
[ s_p^2 = { 15+366-2 } ]
[ s_p^2 = {379} ]
[ s_p ]
Now calculate the standard error:
[ SE = 11.3 ]
[ SE ]
The t-statistic is:
[ t = ]
[ t ]
Degrees of freedom:
[ df = 15+366-2 = 379 ]
The critical value is close to 1.96.
Since:
[ 1.02 < 1.96 ]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
There is not statistically significant evidence, in this illustrative example, that mean temperature changed.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 40. When the Usual Tests Do Not Work
text:
40. When the Usual Tests Do Not Work
The usual t-tests and z-tests rely on assumptions.
For means, if samples are small and the population is not normal, the t-test may not be appropriate.
For proportions, if cell counts are too small, the z-test may not be appropriate.
When those assumptions fail, we can use non-parametric or exact tests.
These tests often go directly to a p-value rather than using a t or z statistic.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 41. Fisher’s Exact Test for Small-Sample Proportions
text:
41. Fisher’s Exact Test for Small-Sample Proportions
When comparing two proportions, if any of the four cells has fewer than about 10 cases, use Fisher’s exact test rather than the z-test.
Fisher’s exact test evaluates how unusual the observed table is under the null hypothesis of no association.
It holds the row and column totals fixed and considers all possible tables with those totals. It then asks what proportion of those possible tables are as extreme as, or more extreme than, the observed table.
That proportion is the p-value.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
42. Example: Sleep and Master’s Students
Suppose we ask whether people get enough sleep.



Group
Enough Sleep
Not Enough Sleep
Total




Master’s students
2
11
13


Other young professionals
5
3
8


Total
7
14
21



Several cells have fewer than 10 cases, so a z-test for difference of proportions is not appropriate.
Use Fisher’s exact test.
Suppose Fisher’s exact test gives:
[ p = 0.056 ]
At:
[ = 0.05 ]
we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
At:
[ = 0.10 ]
we would reject.
The result suggests evidence of a difference, but not quite enough for the conventional 0.05 threshold.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 43. Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney Test for Small-Sample Independent Means
text:
43. Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney Test for Small-Sample Independent Means
When comparing two means with independent samples, if samples are small and population normality cannot be assumed, use a non-parametric alternative.
One option is the Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney test.
This test does not compare means directly. Instead, it ranks all observations from both groups together and examines whether one group tends to have higher ranks than the other.
It is useful when:

the outcome is at least ordinal
samples are independent
sample sizes are small
the normality assumption is not plausible

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 44. Example: Democracy and GDP per Capita
text:
44. Example: Democracy and GDP per Capita
Suppose we compare GDP per capita in democracies and non-democracies in Latin America in 1975.
There are only 24 countries:

12 democracies
12 non-democracies

GDP per capita is skewed, so the population normality assumption is not plausible. A standard t-test may not be appropriate.
The Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney test ranks all 24 countries by GDP per capita and compares the rank positions of democracies and non-democracies.
If the two groups are similar, their ranks should be mixed. If one group tends to have higher GDP per capita, that group should have systematically higher ranks.
Suppose the test gives:
[ p = 0.67 ]
This is large, so we fail to reject the null hypothesis.
There is no statistically significant evidence that GDP per capita differs between democracies and non-democracies in this small sample.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 45. Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test for Dependent Samples
text:
45. Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test for Dependent Samples
For dependent samples, if the sample is small and the population of differences is not normally distributed, use the Wilcoxon signed-rank test.
This is the non-parametric counterpart to the paired t-test.
It uses the ranks of the paired differences rather than assuming the differences are normally distributed.
Use it when:

samples are paired
the outcome is at least ordinal
the sample size is small
the distribution of differences is not plausibly normal

The details are less important here than knowing when the test is appropriate.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
46. Why Add a Control Variable?
When comparing group means using observational data, groups may differ in more than one way.
Suppose we compare life expectancy in democracies and non-democracies.
If democracies have higher life expectancy, is that because they are democracies?
Maybe. But democracies also tend to be wealthier. Wealthier countries usually have better health systems, nutrition, infrastructure, and education.
So country wealth may confound the relationship between democracy and life expectancy.
To address this, we can introduce a control variable.
A control variable is a variable we hold constant while examining the relationship between an independent variable and an outcome.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 47. Controlling in a Mean Comparison Table
text:
47. Controlling in a Mean Comparison Table
Suppose:

(Y): life expectancy
(X): democracy status
(Z): GDP per capita category

We can compare mean life expectancy across democracy categories while holding GDP per capita constant.
For example, divide countries into:

low GDP per capita
middle GDP per capita
high GDP per capita

Then compare democracies and non-democracies within each income category.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
section: 48. Example: Democracy, Wealth, and Life Expectancy
text:
48. Example: Democracy, Wealth, and Life Expectancy
Suppose the table is:



Regime Type
Low GDP
Middle GDP
High GDP
Overall




Non-democracy
57.8
68.7
73.5
64.5


Democracy
63.3
74.1
79.2
72.5



The overall comparison suggests democracies have higher life expectancy:
[ 72.5 - 64.5 = 8.0 ]
But we also compare within GDP categories.
Low-income countries:
[ 63.3 - 57.8 = 5.5 ]
Middle-income countries:
[ 74.1 - 68.7 = 5.4 ]
High-income countries:
[ 79.2 - 73.5 = 5.7 ]
Even after controlling for GDP category, democracies still have higher life expectancy in each income group.
This suggests the relationship between democracy and life expectancy is not entirely explained by GDP per capita.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
49. Additive Relationships
An additive relationship occurs when two variables each appear to matter, and their effects are relatively consistent across categories of the other variable.
In the example, democracy is associated with higher life expectancy within each GDP category. GDP is also associated with higher life expectancy within each regime type.
Both democracy and GDP appear to matter.
The effect of democracy is similar across income categories. That is an additive pattern.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
50. Spurious Relationships
A relationship is spurious if it disappears after controlling for another variable.
Suppose democracies had higher life expectancy overall, but within each GDP category, democracies and non-democracies had the same life expectancy.
Then the original relationship between democracy and life expectancy would be spurious.
GDP per capita would explain the apparent relationship.
This is like the helium balloon example: the string appeared related to floating, but helium was the true cause.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
51. Interactive Relationships
An interactive relationship occurs when the effect of one variable changes depending on the value of another variable.
For example, suppose democracy has:

little effect in low-income countries
moderate effect in middle-income countries
large effect in high-income countries

Then the effect of democracy depends on GDP level.
That is an interaction.
In table form, interactions appear when differences across one variable vary substantially across categories of the control variable.

title: Chapter 10: Comparison of Two Groups
text:
52. Limits of Controlled Mean Tables
Controlled mean comparison tables are useful, but they have limits.
Adding one control variable can already create many cells. Adding two or three controls quickly makes the table unwieldy.
For example, if:

democracy has 2 categories
GDP has 3 categories
region has 5 categories
colonial history has 4 categories

the number of combinations becomes large very quickly.
This is one reason researchers use regression. Regression allows us to control for multiple variables more efficiently.
Mean comparison tables are helpful for building intuition, but regression is more practical for more complex analysis.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
1. When Do We Use Correlation?
Correlation is used when the variables in the analysis are interval- or ratio-level variables.
Examples include:

income
age
life expectancy
unemployment rate
education spending
GDP per capita
test scores
temperature
unionization rate
inequality measures
democracy scores, if treated as interval-level

When variables are categorical, we use other tools, such as:

difference-of-means tests
difference-of-proportions tests
chi-square tests
ANOVA

But when both variables are quantitative, correlation is often a useful first step.
Because interval-level variables can take many values, we usually do not create joint frequency tables. There would be too many possible combinations. Instead, we use scatterplots and correlation coefficients.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
2. Scatterplots
A scatterplot displays the relationship between two quantitative variables.
One variable is placed on the horizontal axis, usually called (X). The other is placed on the vertical axis, usually called (Y).
Each point represents one case.
For example, if we are studying countries:

(X): democracy score
(Y): life expectancy

Each dot on the scatterplot is a country.
Scatterplots help us see whether the relationship is:

positive
negative
weak
strong
linear
nonlinear
influenced by outliers

Correlation analysis gives us a numerical summary of what we see visually in a scatterplot.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
3. Strength and Direction
Correlation describes two features of a relationship:

Direction
Strength

Direction
A relationship is positive if higher values of one variable tend to be associated with higher values of the other variable.
For example, if countries with higher education spending tend to have higher life expectancy, the relationship is positive.
A relationship is negative if higher values of one variable tend to be associated with lower values of the other variable.
For example, if countries with higher unionization tend to have lower economic inequality, the relationship is negative.
Important point:

If both variables decrease together, the relationship is positive, not negative.

Positive means the variables move in the same direction. Negative means they move in opposite directions.
Strength
Strength refers to how closely the data follow a linear pattern.
If the points in a scatterplot fall almost exactly along a straight line, the relationship is strong.
If the points are widely scattered, the relationship is weak.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
4. Pearson’s (r)
The most common measure of correlation is Pearson’s correlation coefficient, written:
[ r ]
Pearson’s (r) measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables.
It ranges from:
[ -1 r ]
The interpretation is:



Value of (r)
Interpretation




(r = 1)
Perfect positive linear relationship


(r = 0)
No linear relationship


(r = -1)
Perfect negative linear relationship



Values between these extremes indicate weaker or stronger relationships.
For example:

(r = 0.80): strong positive linear relationship
(r = 0.30): weak-to-moderate positive linear relationship
(r = -0.60): moderately strong negative linear relationship
(r = -0.05): very weak negative linear relationship
(r = 0.00): no linear relationship

The closer (r) is to 1 or -1, the stronger the linear relationship.
The closer (r) is to 0, the weaker the linear relationship.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
5. Positive Correlation
A positive correlation means that higher values of (X) tend to be associated with higher values of (Y).
In a scatterplot, the points tend to slope upward from left to right.
For example, suppose countries with higher democracy scores tend to have higher life expectancy. Then the correlation between democracy and life expectancy would be positive.
A perfect positive correlation would mean all points fall exactly on an upward-sloping line:
[ r = 1 ]
In real data, perfect correlation is rare. Social data are wonderfully messy, because humans insist on being complicated.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
6. Negative Correlation
A negative correlation means that higher values of (X) tend to be associated with lower values of (Y).
In a scatterplot, the points tend to slope downward from left to right.
For example, suppose countries with higher unionization rates tend to have lower economic inequality. Then the correlation between unionization and inequality would be negative.
A perfect negative correlation would mean all points fall exactly on a downward-sloping line:
[ r = -1 ]
Again, perfect negative correlation is rare in social science data.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
7. No Linear Correlation
If (r) is close to 0, the variables have little or no linear relationship.
A scatterplot with no linear correlation may look like a random cloud of points.
However, this is an important caution:

A correlation of zero means there is no linear relationship. It does not necessarily mean there is no relationship at all.

Two variables can be related in a nonlinear way.
For example, suppose the relationship between age and happiness is U-shaped: happiness is higher among young adults, lower in middle age, and higher again later in life. Pearson’s (r) might be close to zero because the positive and negative parts of the relationship cancel out, even though a real nonlinear relationship exists.
Pearson’s (r) measures linearity. It does not detect every kind of relationship.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
8. Magnitude and Direction
Suppose we calculate:
[ r = 0.77 ]
This indicates a strong positive linear relationship.
Suppose instead:
[ r = -0.60 ]
This indicates a moderately strong negative linear relationship.
Suppose:
[ r = -0.01 ]
This indicates essentially no linear relationship.
The sign tells us direction.
The absolute value tells us strength.
So:
[ r = -0.80 ]
is stronger than:
[ r = 0.30 ]
even though one is negative and one is positive.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
9. Correlation Does Not Measure Slope
Pearson’s (r) does not tell us how much (Y) changes when (X) changes.
It tells us how closely the data fit a straight line.
A correlation of 1 can occur for any positive slope, as long as the points fall perfectly on a line.
For example, both of these could have:
[ r = 1 ]

a line where (Y) increases by 1 for every 1-unit increase in (X)
a line where (Y) increases by 100 for every 1-unit increase in (X)

Correlation tells us linear strength and direction, not the magnitude of the change.
Regression will add that next piece by estimating a slope.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
10. Correlation and Causation
Correlation does not establish causation.
If two variables are correlated, several possibilities remain:

(X) causes (Y)
(Y) causes (X)
a third variable causes both
the relationship is spurious
the relationship reflects selection effects
the relationship is driven by outliers
the relationship is coincidental

For example, if democracy and life expectancy are positively correlated, democracy may contribute to better health outcomes. But wealth, education, health systems, state capacity, or regional history may also matter.
Correlation is useful for describing relationships. It is not enough to prove causality.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
11. The Logic of the Formula
The formula for Pearson’s (r) is:
[ r = { } ]
You do not need to memorize or calculate this formula by hand.
But understanding the logic helps.
The numerator measures covariation.
The denominator rescales that covariation by the total variation in (X) and (Y).
In rough terms:
[ r { X Y} ]
The result is a standardized measure bounded between -1 and 1.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
12. Deviations from the Mean
The formula asks whether (X) and (Y) tend to be above or below their means at the same time.
For each case, we calculate:
[ X_i - {X} ]
and:
[ Y_i - {Y} ]
These are deviations from the mean.
If (X_i) is above its mean, then:
[ X_i-{X} > 0 ]
If (X_i) is below its mean, then:
[ X_i-{X} < 0 ]
The same logic applies to (Y).
The key is the product:
[ (X_i-{X})(Y_i-{Y}) ]
This product is positive when (X) and (Y) are on the same side of their means.
It is negative when they are on opposite sides.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
13. Positive Contributions
A case contributes positive evidence for correlation when:

(X) is above its mean and (Y) is above its mean.
(X) is below its mean and (Y) is below its mean.

In the first case:
[ (+)(+) = + ]
In the second case:
[ (-)(-) = + ]
These cases suggest that (X) and (Y) move together.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
14. Negative Contributions
A case contributes negative evidence for correlation when:

(X) is above its mean and (Y) is below its mean.
(X) is below its mean and (Y) is above its mean.

In the first case:
[ (+)(-) = - ]
In the second case:
[ (-)(+) = - ]
These cases suggest that (X) and (Y) move in opposite directions.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
15. A Small Numerical Example
Suppose we have four cases:



Case
(X)
(Y)




1
6
8


2
3
7


3
4
4


4
5
7



Suppose:
[ {X} = 4 ]
and:
[ {Y} = 6 ]
Now calculate deviations:



Case
(X-{X})
(Y-{Y})
Product




1
2
2
4


2
-1
1
-1


3
0
-2
0


4
1
1
1



The products sum to:
[ 4 + (-1) + 0 + 1 = 4 ]
The sum is positive, meaning the overall covariation is positive.
This suggests a positive correlation between (X) and (Y).

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
16. Quadrants in a Scatterplot
A scatterplot can help us visualize the same logic.
Draw a vertical line at ({X}) and a horizontal line at ({Y}). This divides the scatterplot into four quadrants.
Upper-right quadrant
(X) is above its mean.
(Y) is above its mean.
This contributes positive correlation.
Lower-left quadrant
(X) is below its mean.
(Y) is below its mean.
This also contributes positive correlation.
Upper-left quadrant
(X) is below its mean.
(Y) is above its mean.
This contributes negative correlation.
Lower-right quadrant
(X) is above its mean.
(Y) is below its mean.
This contributes negative correlation.
If most points fall in the upper-right and lower-left quadrants, the correlation is positive.
If most points fall in the upper-left and lower-right quadrants, the correlation is negative.
If points are scattered evenly across all quadrants, the correlation is near zero.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
17. Pearson’s (r) as an Estimate
When we calculate Pearson’s (r) from sample data, it is a sample statistic.
It estimates the true correlation in the population.
Let the true population correlation be:
[ ]
The sample correlation is:
[ r ]
The null hypothesis is usually:
[ H_0: = 0 ]
The alternative is:
[ H_A: ]
In words, the null hypothesis says there is no linear correlation in the population.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
18. Standard Error of Pearson’s (r)
The standard error of Pearson’s (r) is:
[ SE_r = ]
where:

(r) is the sample correlation
(n) is the sample size

The degrees of freedom are:
[ df = n - 2 ]
Why (n-2)?
Because estimating the correlation requires estimating two means:

({X})
({Y})

We lose one degree of freedom for each estimated mean.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
19. t-Test for Pearson’s (r)
To test whether the correlation differs from zero, use:
[ t = ]
or:
[ t = { } ]
The test statistic follows a t-distribution with:
[ df = n - 2 ]
If the absolute value of the t-statistic exceeds the critical value, or if the p-value is less than alpha, we reject the null hypothesis.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
20. Example: Democracy and Life Expectancy
Suppose we calculate the correlation between democracy and female life expectancy across countries.
The result is:
[ r = 0.3751 ]
This indicates a positive relationship: more democratic countries tend to have higher female life expectancy.
The relationship is not extremely strong, but it is clearly positive.
If the p-value is very small, we reject:
[ H_0: = 0 ]
and conclude that the correlation is statistically significant.
A careful interpretation is:

Democracy and female life expectancy are positively correlated. The relationship is statistically significant, though the magnitude of the correlation is moderate.

Again, this does not by itself prove that democracy causes higher life expectancy.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
21. Correlation Commands
Correlation is usually calculated with software.
In Stata
A common command is:
pwcorr var1 var2, sig
The option:
sig
requests p-values.
If you want sample sizes as well, you can use:
pwcorr var1 var2, sig obs
In R
The base R command is:
cor.test(var1, var2)
For multiple variables, you can use:
cor(data[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")],
    use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
The option:
use = "pairwise.complete.obs"
tells R to use all available data for each pair of variables.
This matters when some variables have missing values. Without this option, R may drop any row with missing data on any variable in the correlation matrix, even if the pair currently being analyzed has no missing values.
For fuller output, including p-values and sample sizes, the corr.test function in the psych package is useful.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
22. Correlation Matrices
When we calculate correlations among several variables, software reports a correlation matrix.
A correlation matrix has the same variables in the rows and columns.
For example:




Variable 1
Variable 2
Variable 3
Variable 4




Variable 1
1.00
(r_{12})
(r_{13})
(r_{14})


Variable 2
(r_{21})
1.00
(r_{23})
(r_{24})


Variable 3
(r_{31})
(r_{32})
1.00
(r_{34})


Variable 4
(r_{41})
(r_{42})
(r_{43})
1.00



The diagonal values are always 1 because each variable is perfectly correlated with itself.
The matrix is symmetric:
[ r_{12} = r_{21} ]
[ r_{13} = r_{31} ]
and so on.
Some software reports the full matrix. Other software reports only one triangle of the matrix to avoid duplication.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
23. Example: Cross-National Correlations
Suppose we analyze several country-level variables:

autocracy score
education spending as a percentage of GDP
unemployment rate
Gini coefficient, a measure of income inequality
unionization rate

A correlation matrix might show that the correlation between unionization and inequality is:
[ r = -0.43 ]
This is a moderate negative relationship.
Interpretation:

Countries with higher unionization rates tend to have lower income inequality.

This is a descriptive association. It does not prove that unionization causes lower inequality. Other factors may influence both.
Suppose the correlation between autocracy and education spending is:
[ r = -0.08 ]
This is a very weak negative relationship.
Interpretation:

More autocratic countries tend to spend slightly less on education, but the relationship is weak.

Always interpret both direction and magnitude.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
24. Correlation Measures Linear Association
Pearson’s (r) measures linear relationships.
If the true relationship is curved, U-shaped, threshold-based, or otherwise nonlinear, Pearson’s (r) may be misleading.
Always look at a scatterplot when possible.
A correlation coefficient is useful, but it should not be the only thing you inspect.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
text:
25. Outliers Can Matter
Outliers can strongly influence correlations.
A single extreme case may make a relationship appear stronger, weaker, or even reverse its direction.
For example, a very wealthy country with unusual political institutions may influence a cross-national correlation between democracy and income.
In later regression analysis, we will return to outliers more directly. For now, the basic advice is:

Do not rely on Pearson’s (r) alone. Look at the data.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
section: 26. Correlation Does Not Control for Other Variables
text:
26. Correlation Does Not Control for Other Variables
Correlation is bivariate. It examines the relationship between two variables at a time.
It does not control for other factors.
For example, suppose democracy and life expectancy are correlated. That relationship might change after controlling for GDP per capita.
Correlation cannot tell us whether the relationship remains after accounting for other variables.
Regression analysis allows us to include controls.
Correlation is a useful starting point, not the final word.

title: Chapter 13: Correlation
section: 27. Statistical Significance Is Not Substantive Significance
text:
27. Statistical Significance Is Not Substantive Significance
With a large sample, even a weak correlation can be statistically significant.
For example:
[ r = 0.05 ]
may be statistically significant if (n) is very large.
But it may not be substantively meaningful.
Always ask:

Is the relationship statistically significant?
How strong is the relationship?
Is the relationship meaningful in context?
Does the relationship make theoretical sense?

A small p-value does not make a weak relationship important.
